#1 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 123 words

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatLow

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Real talk for every business owner paying suppliers in China. You sent CNY last week. Your supplier called and said they got USD. You're confused. You called your bank. They said it was the correspondent. You called the correspondent. They said it was the receiving bank. The receiving bank said the sender bank should have sent CNY. Nobody can tell you where the conversion happened. Because it could have happened at any of three places. Sender bank converts CNY to USD upfront. Or correspondent never had CNY. Or receiving bank converted on arrival. Three possible conversion points. Three spreads. Zero transparency. I see this every single week. Stop guessing where your money got converted. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. Same-day. One rate. No phone-tag with three different banks. Search WorldFirst. Now.

They said it was the correspondent. You called the correspondent. They said it was the receiving bank. The receiving bank said the sender bank should have sent CNY. Nobody can tell you where the conversion happened. Because it could have happened at any of three places. Sender bank converts CNY to USD upfront. Or correspondent never had CNY. Here's why no one can tell you where your CNY became USD. You sent CNY last week. Your supplier called and said they got USD. You're confused. You called your bank. They said it was the correspondent. You called the correspondent. They said it was the receiving bank. The receiving bank said the sender bank should have sent CNY. Nobody can tell you where the conversion happened. Because it could have happened at any of three places. Sender bank converts CNY to USD upfront. Or correspondent never had CNY. Or receiving bank converted on arrival. Three possible conversion points. Three spreads. Zero transparency. This happens every single week. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. Same-day. One rate. No phone-tag with three different banks. Search WorldFirst.

Here's why no one can tell you where your CNY became USD. Picture this. You sent CNY last week. Your supplier in China calls you and says, we received USD, not CNY. You are confused. You called your own bank. They said it must have been the correspondent bank in the middle. You called the correspondent bank. They said it must have been the receiving bank in China. The receiving bank said your sender bank should have sent real CNY in the first place. Three banks, three different stories. Nobody can tell you exactly where the swap happened. Why? Because it could have happened at any one of three places. The sender bank may have swapped CNY into USD before it left. The correspondent bank in the middle may have never had CNY to begin with, so it forwarded USD. The receiving bank may have just done the swap from USD to CNY on arrival, at its own rate. Three possible conversion points. Three possible spreads, which is the hidden margin inside the exchange rate. Zero transparency for you. This kind of phone tag between banks happens every single week to people paying China. We built WorldFirst to send CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. Same-day in most cases. One rate at the start. No middle banks to chase. No phone tag. Search WorldFirst.

220 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Eh this one I felt before, called my bank they really blame the other party. Relatable.
  • Who is WorldFirst tho. Never see them in Malaysia. Got physical office?
  • Every single week got people facing this? Sounds exaggerated but maybe.
  • Same-day one rate, ok but my bank also got same-day option. Difference how big?
  • No phone-tag part real lah. I waste 2 hours last month on this exact thing.
#2 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 131 words

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Watch this. You hit send on a CNY payment to your factory. By the time it lands, your CNY has been converted three different times. Once. Twice. Three times. Each conversion takes a 1% spread. Compound that. On a 50,000 dollar order, you just lost 1,500 dollars to invisible currency hops you didn't know existed. Do it twice a month. That's 36,000 a year. Over five years, your bank quietly took 180,000 from your business through conversion points you never saw. That's not a fee. That's the spread on three separate exchange rate hops. Sender bank. Correspondent. Beneficiary bank. Each one takes a bite. Listen. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. No intermediary conversion. No double conversion at the supplier's end. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Your supplier gets exactly what you sent. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

In this video: how a small wire fee quietly adds up over five years. You hit send on a CNY payment to your factory. By the time it lands, your CNY has been converted three different times. Once. Twice. Three times. Each conversion takes a 1% spread. Compound that. On a 50,000 dollar order, you just lost 1,500 dollars to invisible currency hops you didn't know existed. Do it twice a month. That's 36,000 a year. Over five years, your bank quietly took 180,000 from your business through conversion points you never saw. That's not a fee. That's the spread on three separate exchange rate hops. Sender bank. Correspondent. Beneficiary bank. Each one takes a bite. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. No intermediary conversion. No double conversion at the supplier's end. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Your supplier gets exactly what you sent. Search WorldFirst.

In this video: how a small wire fee quietly adds up over five years. You hit send on a CNY payment to your factory in China. You think the money goes straight from your bank to your supplier's bank. But that is not what happens. By the time it lands, your CNY may have been swapped between currencies three different times. Here is why. Each bank in the chain has its own currency rate, called an exchange rate. The gap between what you get on the news and what the bank actually gives you is called the spread. It is a hidden charge baked into the rate. Now picture three banks, each taking around 1% in spread. One percent at your bank. One percent at the bank in the middle, the correspondent bank. One percent at your supplier's bank when it converts back to CNY. On a 50,000 dollar order, that is roughly 1,500 dollars gone. Not as a fee on your statement. As a quiet bite out of the rate. Do this twice a month. That is around 36,000 dollars a year. Stretch it over five years. That is roughly 180,000 dollars your business never sees. That is a hire. A container of stock. A holiday for your family. Those are illustrative numbers based on common bank spreads, but the mechanism is real and happens on most CNY wires. We built WorldFirst differently. We send CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. No bank in the middle. No double conversion at the supplier's end. Same-day in most cases. Your supplier gets exactly what you sent. Search WorldFirst.

  • Every conversion 1%? My bank told me only one conversion happen, not three. Confusing.

  • Math too perfect lah, real world got weekend, public holiday, all kinds of delay.

#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 127 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Listen. If you're a business owner sending CNY to your Chinese supplier, there are three places your CNY can quietly turn into USD before it ever lands. Place one. Your sender bank converts CNY to USD upfront because USD is cheaper for them to route. Place two. The correspondent bank in the middle holds only USD, so it converts again. Place three. Your supplier's receiving bank gets USD and converts back to CNY at their bank's rate. That's three conversion points. Three spreads. Three margins your bank never tells you about. Now your supplier is short. They're calling you asking why they got 47,000 dollars when you sent the equivalent of 50,000. Stop. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. One rate. Zero double conversion. Same-day. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Listen. If you're a business owner sending CNY to your Chinese supplier, there are three places your CNY can quietly turn into USD before it ever lands. Place one. Your sender bank converts CNY to USD upfront because USD is cheaper for them to route. Place two. The correspondent bank in the middle holds only USD, so it converts again. Place three. Your supplier's receiving bank gets USD and converts back to CNY at their bank's rate. That's three conversion points. Three spreads. Three margins your bank never tells you about. Now your supplier is short. They're calling you asking why they got 47,000 dollars when you sent the equivalent of 50,000. Stop. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. One rate. Zero double conversion. Same-day. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Here's what every business owner paying suppliers in China needs to know about wire conversions. Place one. Your sender bank converts CNY to USD upfront because USD is cheaper for them to route. Place two. The correspondent bank in the middle holds only USD, so it converts again. Place three. Your supplier's receiving bank gets USD and converts back to CNY at their bank's rate. That's three conversion points. Three spreads. Three margins your bank never tells you about. Now your supplier is short. They're calling you asking why they got 47,000 dollars when you sent the equivalent of 50,000. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. One rate. Zero double conversion. Same-day. Search WorldFirst.

Here's what every business owner paying suppliers in China needs to know about wire conversions. Your CNY can quietly turn into USD on the way over. Not once. Up to three times. Here is how. Place one. Your own bank, the one sending the money. Most banks do not carry CNY on their pipes. They carry USD. So they swap your CNY into USD before sending. That is conversion number one. Place two. The bank in the middle. This is called a correspondent bank. It sits between your bank and your supplier's bank in China. It usually only holds USD. So if any CNY came through, it gets swapped again here. That is conversion number two. Place three. Your supplier's bank in China. It receives USD. It then swaps that USD back into CNY at its own rate, not yours. That is conversion number three. Three conversions. Three exchange rate spreads. Each one takes a small slice. Your bank never tells you about these slices. So your supplier ends up short. They call you and say, we only got 47,000 dollars when you sent 50,000. Now you have to wire the difference, or eat the loss. We built WorldFirst differently. We send CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. One rate. No double conversion. Same-day delivery in most cases. Search WorldFirst.

  • Who is WorldFirst? Never see this brand in Malaysia. Got license here or not?
  • Sound like ad la. Where the proof of zero double conversion?
  • My supplier never complain about getting USD. Maybe this only happen to small businesses?
  • Same day? Maybank also say same day but always next day. So how?
#4 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 144 words

Direct address + problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

If you wire money to China, you need to hear this. Real talk. There are three places your CNY can become USD and you're paying a spread at every single one of them. One. Your sender bank converts to USD before sending because their rails carry USD, not CNY. You eat that spread. Two. The intermediary correspondent bank only holds USD. So if any CNY got through, it gets converted again here. You eat that spread. Three. Your supplier's bank receives USD and converts to CNY locally at their rate, not yours. You eat that spread too. Three spreads. One wire. Your supplier gets less than you sent. Or worse, they get USD and you find out later they paid their factory short. Stop sending payments through banks that don't carry CNY. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. Same-day to Chinese bank accounts. One rate. If you're paying suppliers in China, you need WorldFirst. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

If you wire money to China, you need to hear this. Real talk. There are three places your CNY can become USD and you're paying a spread at every single one of them. One. Your sender bank converts to USD before sending because their rails carry USD, not CNY. You eat that spread. Two. The intermediary correspondent bank only holds USD. So if any CNY got through, it gets converted again here. You eat that spread. Three. Your supplier's bank receives USD and converts to CNY locally at their rate, not yours. You eat that spread too. Three spreads. One wire. Your supplier gets less than you sent. Or worse, they get USD and you find out later they paid their factory short. Stop sending payments through banks that don't carry CNY. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. Same-day to Chinese bank accounts. One rate. If you're paying suppliers in China, you need WorldFirst. Period. WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst. Period.

If you wire money to China and want every dollar to land, watch this. There are three places your CNY can become USD and you're paying a spread at every single one of them. One. Your sender bank converts to USD before sending because their rails carry USD, not CNY. You eat that spread. Two. The intermediary correspondent bank only holds USD. So if any CNY got through, it gets converted again here. You eat that spread. Three. Your supplier's bank receives USD and converts to CNY locally at their rate, not yours. You eat that spread too. Three spreads. One wire. Your supplier gets less than you sent. Or worse, they get USD and you find out later they paid their factory short. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. One rate. If you're paying suppliers in China, WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

If you wire money to China and want every dollar to land, watch this. There are three places your CNY can become USD on the way over, and you are paying a small currency spread at every single one of them. A spread is the gap baked into the exchange rate that the bank quietly keeps for itself. Here are the three places. Place one. Your own bank. Most banks do not carry CNY on their international pipes. They carry USD. So they swap your money to USD before it leaves. You eat that spread. Place two. The bank in the middle, called the correspondent bank. It usually only holds USD. So if any CNY came through, it gets swapped again right here. You eat that spread too. Place three. Your supplier's bank in China. It receives USD and has to swap it into CNY at its own rate, not the rate you saw. You eat that spread as well. Three spreads. One wire. The result. Your supplier gets less CNY than you thought you were sending. Or worse, they get USD they did not ask for, and you find out a week later they paid their factory short. We built WorldFirst to send CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. Same-day in most cases. One rate at the start, no surprises in the middle. If you pay suppliers in China, WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

Accusation opener + brutal specificity

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Your bank stole 1,500 dollars from your last CNY payment to China and you have no idea where it went. Here's how. You sent CNY. Or so you thought. Your sender bank converted it to USD first because USD is what their pipes carry. That's one conversion. Spread one. Then it hit a correspondent bank in Hong Kong or New York. They only hold USD. They held it as USD. Sent it forward as USD. Then your supplier's bank in Shenzhen received USD. They converted it back to CNY at their rate. That's spread three. Three rate margins on one wire. Your supplier got 47,000 dollars worth of CNY instead of 50,000. They're short. They're asking questions. You eat the loss or you wire the difference. Stop signing off on this. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. One conversion. Or none, if you're holding CNY already. Same-day to Chinese bank accounts. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

They held it as USD. Sent it forward as USD. Then your supplier's bank in Shenzhen received USD. They converted it back to CNY at their rate. That's spread three. Three rate margins on one wire. Your supplier got 47,000 dollars worth of CNY instead of 50,000. They're short. They're asking questions. You eat the loss or you wire the difference. Stop signing off on this. Here are the quiet bank tricks every business owner paying China should know. Here's how. You sent CNY. Or so you thought. Your sender bank converted it to USD first because USD is what their pipes carry. That's one conversion. Spread one. Then it hit a correspondent bank in Hong Kong or New York. They only hold USD. They held it as USD. Sent it forward as USD. Then your supplier's bank in Shenzhen received USD. They converted it back to CNY at their rate. That's spread three. Three rate margins on one wire. Your supplier got 47,000 dollars worth of CNY instead of 50,000. They're short. They're asking questions. You eat the loss or you wire the difference. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. One conversion. Here are the quiet bank tricks every business owner paying China should know. Here is how it can happen. You sent CNY. Or so you thought. The screen on your bank app said CNY. But here is what really happens behind the scenes. Step one. Your sender bank takes your CNY and swaps it into USD before sending. Why? Because USD is what their international pipes carry. CNY does not move smoothly through most western and SEA banks. That is conversion number one. A small slice gets taken on the rate. Step two. The wire arrives at a correspondent bank. Think of it as a middle bank that sits between your bank and your supplier's bank. It is often in Hong Kong, Singapore, or New York. These middle banks usually only hold USD. So your money sits there as USD. Sent forward as USD. Step three. Your supplier's bank in mainland China, say Shenzhen or Shanghai, receives USD. Their system swaps it back into CNY at their own rate, not yours. That is conversion number three. Each step takes a slice out of the rate. By the time the money lands, your supplier sees the equivalent of 47,000 dollars instead of 50,000. They call you asking where the difference went. You either eat the loss or you wire the difference. We built WorldFirst differently. We send CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. One conversion at the front, or zero if you are already holding CNY in your World Account. Same-day in most cases. Search WorldFirst.

  • Hong Kong correspondent? My bank go via Singapore. So which is correct?

  • Spread three? My bank only show one rate on the slip. Where the other two come from?

#6 PG2 Gary Vee Opinion-led 126 words

I'll say it opinion-as-truth + compound math

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

I'll say it. There's no such thing as a CNY wire from most banks. You think you're sending CNY. You're sending USD that gets converted to CNY somewhere down the line. And there are three places that can happen. Your sender bank. The correspondent. The supplier's bank. Each conversion point. Each spread. 1% here, 1% there, 0.5% at the end. That's 2.5% on every payment. On 50,000 dollars that's 1,250 gone. Per wire. Multiply that across a year. 30,000 dollars vanished into currency conversion you didn't know was happening. That's a hire. That's a container. That's your margin. WorldFirst carries CNY natively. Same-day to Chinese bank accounts. One conversion. Sometimes zero if you're holding CNY in the account already. The difference goes back into your business. Where it belongs. Period.

That's 2.5% on every payment. On 50,000 dollars that's 1,250 gone. Per wire. Multiply that across a year. 30,000 dollars vanished into currency conversion you didn't know was happening. That's a hire. That's a container. That's your margin. WorldFirst carries CNY natively. Same-day to Chinese bank accounts. One conversion. Sometimes zero if you're holding CNY in the account already. The difference goes back into your business. Where it belongs. Period.

In this video: why most banks don't actually send CNY, even when you ask them to. You think you're sending CNY. You're sending USD that gets converted to CNY somewhere down the line. And there are three places that can happen. Your sender bank. The correspondent. The supplier's bank. Each conversion point. Each spread. 1% here, 1% there, 0.5% at the end. That's 2.5% on every payment. On 50,000 dollars that's 1,250 gone. Per wire. Multiply that across a year. 30,000 dollars vanished into currency conversion you didn't know was happening. That's a hire. That's a container. That's your margin. WorldFirst carries CNY natively. Same-day to Chinese bank accounts. One conversion. Sometimes zero if you're holding CNY in the account already. The difference goes back into your business. Where it belongs.

In this video: why most banks don't actually send CNY, even when you ask them to. You think you are sending CNY because the screen on your bank app says CNY. But what really moves through the system is USD that gets swapped into CNY somewhere down the line. There are three places that swap can happen. Place one. Your own bank, the sender. Place two. The bank in the middle, called the correspondent bank. Place three. Your supplier's bank in China. Each one of these can do a swap. Each swap has a small spread baked into the exchange rate, often around 1 percent at the sender, 1 percent at the correspondent, and 0.5 percent at the receiving bank. Stack those up and you are paying around 2.5 percent of every payment just on FX, which is foreign exchange, with no fee line on your statement. On a 50,000 dollar payment, that is roughly 1,250 dollars gone. Per wire. Now do that across a year of regular supplier payments. You can lose around 30,000 dollars to currency hops you did not even know were happening. That is a hire. That is a container of stock. That is your margin. Those numbers are illustrative, but the mechanism is real. We built WorldFirst to carry CNY natively. Same-day to Chinese bank accounts in most cases. One conversion at the start, sometimes zero if you are already holding CNY in your World Account. The difference goes back into your business, where it belongs.

#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 140 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the problem most business owners paying Chinese suppliers don't understand. You think you sent CNY. You didn't. Here's what actually happened. Step one. Your sender bank doesn't have a direct CNY rail. They convert your money to USD on day one. Step two. The correspondent bank in the middle, the one routing the wire, only carries USD. So the USD stays USD. Step three. Your supplier's bank receives USD and has to convert it to CNY locally because your supplier holds a CNY account. Three conversion points. Each one with a spread. Each one taking 0.5 to 1.5% silently. On 50,000 dollars, that's 1,500 dollars in invisible spread. Your supplier ends up holding USD they didn't ask for, or shorter CNY than expected. Stop. WorldFirst routes CNY directly to Chinese accounts. One rate. No middleman conversion. Same-day delivery. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Here's the problem most business owners paying Chinese suppliers don't understand. You think you sent CNY. You didn't. Here's what actually happened. Step one. Your sender bank doesn't have a direct CNY rail. They convert your money to USD on day one. Step two. The correspondent bank in the middle, the one routing the wire, only carries USD. So the USD stays USD. Step three. Your supplier's bank receives USD and has to convert it to CNY locally because your supplier holds a CNY account. Three conversion points. Each one with a spread. Each one taking 0.5 to 1.5% silently. On 50,000 dollars, that's 1,500 dollars in invisible spread. Your supplier ends up holding USD they didn't ask for, or shorter CNY than expected. Stop. WorldFirst routes CNY directly to Chinese accounts. One rate. No middleman conversion. Same-day delivery. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

In this video: what actually happens when you wire CNY to a supplier in China. You think you sent CNY. You didn't. Here's what actually happened. Step one. Your sender bank doesn't have a direct CNY rail. They convert your money to USD on day one. Step two. The correspondent bank in the middle, the one routing the wire, only carries USD. So the USD stays USD. Step three. Your supplier's bank receives USD and has to convert it to CNY locally because your supplier holds a CNY account. Three conversion points. Each one with a spread. Each one taking 0.5 to 1.5% silently. On 50,000 dollars, that's 1,500 dollars in invisible spread. Your supplier ends up holding USD they didn't ask for, or shorter CNY than expected. WorldFirst routes CNY directly to Chinese accounts. One rate. No middleman conversion. Same-day delivery. Search WorldFirst.

In this video: what actually happens when you wire CNY to a supplier in China. You think you sent CNY. You did not. Here is what actually happens behind the scenes. Step one. Your sender bank does not have a direct line to send CNY into China. Their international system, what banks call a rail, mostly carries USD. So they take your CNY and swap it to USD on day one. Step two. The wire then goes through a correspondent bank, the bank in the middle that routes your money onwards. Most of these banks only hold USD. So the USD stays USD. Step three. Your supplier's bank in China gets USD. But your supplier holds a CNY account at that bank. So that bank does a local swap from USD back into CNY using their own rate. Three conversion points. Each one has a spread, the hidden gap inside the exchange rate. Spreads can be anywhere from 0.5 percent to 1.5 percent at each step. On a 50,000 dollar payment, that adds up to around 1,500 dollars quietly missing. Your supplier ends up holding USD they did not ask for, or shorter CNY than you both expected. We built WorldFirst on direct CNY rails into Chinese bank accounts. One rate at the start. No bank in the middle taking a slice. Same-day delivery in most cases. Search WorldFirst.

227 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Range better lah, 0.5 to 1.5%. But still where the proof?
  • Sender bank no CNY rail? Maybank can send CNY ah, I thought.
  • Who regulate WorldFirst? Important question. Wise got license here, WF got or not?
  • Cobi or who is on this video also dont know, no face also weird.

#8 PG1 Gary Vee Opinion-led 104 words

Classic Stop / Real-talk opener

Soc4.0 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Lost some of the original Gary Vee punch after polish. Still works but feels closer to baseline.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Stop. Real talk for every business owner sending CNY to China. You don't actually send CNY. You think you do. You don't. There are three places your money gets converted to USD on the way over. Your sender bank. The correspondent bank in the middle. Your supplier's receiving bank. Each one takes a spread. Each one eats your margin. By the time the money lands, your supplier is short or sitting on USD they didn't want. Listen. WorldFirst sends CNY directly. Same-day to Chinese accounts. One rate, no middleman conversion. Stop wasting money on currency hops nobody told you about. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Stop. Real talk for everyFor every business owner sending CNY to China. You don't actually send CNY. You think you do. You don't. There are three places your money gets converted to USD on the way over. Your sender bank. The correspondent bank in the middle. Your supplier's receiving bank. Each one takes a spread. Each one eats your margin. By the time the money lands, your supplier is short or sitting on USD they didn't want. Listen. WorldFirst sends CNY directly. Same-day to Chinese accounts. One rate, no middleman conversion. Stop wasting money onWorth checking if your bank is putting your CNY through currency hops nobody told you about. Search WorldFirst. Now.

deleted / added

Here's what really happens when you send a CNY wire from your bank. You don't actually send CNY. You think you do. You don't. There are three places your money gets converted to USD on the way over. Your sender bank. The correspondent bank in the middle. Your supplier's receiving bank. Each one takes a spread. Each one eats your margin. By the time the money lands, your supplier is short or sitting on USD they didn't want. WorldFirst sends CNY directly. Same-day to Chinese accounts. One rate, no middleman conversion. Worth checking if your bank is putting your CNY through currency hops nobody told you about. Search WorldFirst.

Here's what really happens when you send a CNY wire from your bank. You do not actually send CNY. You think you do. You do not. Here is what really happens. Your bank app shows CNY on the screen. But behind the scenes, your money can be swapped into USD up to three times before it lands. There are three places this can happen. Place one. Your own bank, the sender. Most banks carry USD on their international lines, not CNY. So they swap your money to USD before sending. Place two. The bank in the middle, the correspondent. It usually only holds USD. Your money sits and moves through there as USD. Place three. Your supplier's bank in China. It receives USD and has to swap it into CNY at its own rate. Each one takes a small spread, the hidden gap inside the exchange rate. Each spread eats into your margin. By the time the money lands, your supplier is either short of what they expected, or worse, sitting on USD they did not want and now have to convert themselves. We built WorldFirst to send CNY directly. Same-day to Chinese accounts in most cases. One rate at the front, no middle bank taking a slice. Worth checking if your bank is putting your CNY through currency hops nobody told you about. Search WorldFirst.

  • One rate sound nice but how I know is interbank rate? Show comparison la.

Stop using your bank to send CNY. I'm serious. Your bank doesn't carry CNY rails. It carries USD rails. So when you ask it to send CNY, it doesn't send CNY. Same-day to Chinese bank accounts. Your supplier receives CNY. Not USD. Not double-converted. Use the right tool for the job. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Here's why your bank might be the wrong tool for paying Chinese suppliers. Your bank doesn't carry CNY rails. It carries USD rails. So when you ask it to send CNY, it doesn't send CNY. Here's why your bank might be the wrong tool for paying Chinese suppliers. Here is why. Banks move international money on what they call rails, the pipes that carry currency between countries. Most banks have rails built for USD. They do not have direct rails for CNY. So when you ask your bank to send CNY, it does not actually send CNY end to end. It sends USD and hopes a swap into CNY happens somewhere down the line. There are three somewheres where that swap can happen. Place one. The sender bank, at the start. Place two. The correspondent bank in the middle, the one that routes the wire onwards. Place three. The beneficiary bank, your supplier's bank in China. Each swap takes a spread, the hidden margin inside the exchange rate. Each spread is a cost to you. Think of it like this. You would not use a fax machine to send a video file. Wrong tool. The pipe does not carry that kind of data. Same idea here. A USD-rail bank is not the right tool for a CNY job. We built WorldFirst on CNY-direct rails into Chinese bank accounts. Same-day in most cases. Your supplier receives CNY. Not USD. Not double converted. Use the right tool for the job. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst.

#10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 130 words

Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about international wires to China. The conversion doesn't happen where you think it happens. You assume your bank converts CNY at the front and sends CNY across. Wrong. Three different banks can convert your money at three different points. Sender bank. Correspondent bank in the middle. Beneficiary bank at the end. Each one books its own rate. Each one takes its own spread. You'll never see this on a statement. You'll just notice your supplier got less than expected. Or worse, your supplier received USD and had to convert again at their own bank. Now there's a fourth conversion. Four spreads on one wire. This is how it actually works. Every single time. WorldFirst doesn't play this game. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. Same-day. One rate. Period.

Here's the truthsomething nobody's telling you about international wires to China. The conversion doesn't happen where you think it happens. You assume your bank converts CNY at the front and sends CNY across. Wrong. Three different banks can convert your money at three different points. Sender bank. Correspondent bank in the middle. Beneficiary bank at the end. Each one books its own rate. Each one takes its own spread. You'll never see this on a statement. You'll just notice your supplier got less than expected. Or worse, your supplier received USD and had to convert again at their own bank. Now there's a fourth conversion. Four spreads on one wire. This is how it actually works. Every single time. WorldFirst doesn't play this gameis built differently. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. Same-day. One rate. Period.

deleted / added

Here are the silent FX moves banks pull on every China wire. Worth knowing. The conversion doesn't happen where you think it happens. You assume your bank converts CNY at the front and sends CNY across. Wrong. Three different banks can convert your money at three different points. Sender bank. Correspondent bank in the middle. Beneficiary bank at the end. Each one books its own rate. Each one takes its own spread. You'll never see this on a statement. You'll just notice your supplier got less than expected. Or worse, your supplier received USD and had to convert again at their own bank. Now there's a fourth conversion. Four spreads on one wire. This is how it actually works. Every single time. WorldFirst is built differently. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. Same-day. One rate.

Here are the silent FX moves banks pull on every China wire. Worth knowing. The currency swap does not happen where you think it happens. You probably assume your bank swaps your money into CNY at the front and then sends real CNY across the border. That is not how it actually works for most banks. Three different banks can each swap your money along the way. Place one. Your sender bank, where the money leaves. Place two. The correspondent bank in the middle, the bank that routes the wire from country to country. Place three. The beneficiary bank, your supplier's bank in China. Each one books its own exchange rate. Each one takes its own spread, the hidden gap inside the rate. You will not see these spreads as line items on your statement. You will just notice your supplier got less CNY than you thought you sent. Or worse. Your supplier's account receives USD because the receiving bank could not auto-convert. So your supplier then has to swap USD into CNY themselves at their bank. That is a fourth swap. Four spreads on one wire. We built WorldFirst differently. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. One rate at the start. Same-day delivery in most cases. Search WorldFirst.

208 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)

  • Sound like ad with the 'every single time' line. Real talk got nuance la.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Got Malaysia license or not, that's the main question.
  • Conspiratorial tone make me suspicious. If real, where the news article reporting this?
  • One rate is good but I want see the rate vs my Maybank rate. Just show la.

Here's why "fee waived" on your wire receipt doesn't mean the wire was free. Your bank isn't really your friend on cross-border wires. They send you a wire confirmation that says fee waived. You think they're doing you a favour. They're often not. Here's why. The bank may have already added a 3 percent markup into the FX rate they used. #3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 149 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Listen. If you're a business owner wiring money to Chinese suppliers, your bank has two ways to charge you. One you see. One you don't. Here's the deal. The wire fee is visible. 25 bucks. 40 bucks. Whatever your bank charges. That's the number you stare at. The rate margin is invisible. 1 to 3 percent of the wire amount. Buried inside the FX rate itself. On a 10K wire at 3 percent, that's 300 dollars gone. No line item. No receipt. Nobody itemises it because they don't have to. Your confirmation literally says fee waived while the rate is 3 percent off Google's mid-market. Both facts coexist legally. Stop trusting the fee line. Start checking the rate. WorldFirst gives you near mid-market rates. Same rate Google shows you. If you're sending money to China and you're still reading the fee line, you're missing where the cost actually lives. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Whatever your bank charges. That's the number you stare at. The rate margin is invisible. 1 to 3 percent of the wire amount. Buried inside the FX rate itself. On a 10K wire at 3 percent, that's 300 dollars gone. No line item. No receipt. Nobody itemises it because they don't have to. Your confirmation literally says fee waived while the rate is 3 percent off Google's mid-market. Both facts coexist legally. Stop trusting the fee line. deleted / added

Here's what every business owner sending money to China needs to know about hidden bank fees. One you see. One you don't. Here's the deal. The wire fee is visible. 25 bucks. 40 bucks. Whatever your bank charges. That's the number you stare at. The rate margin is invisible. 1 to 3 percent of the wire amount. Buried inside the FX rate itself. On a 10K wire at 3 percent, that's 300 dollars gone. No line item. No receipt. Nobody itemises it because they don't have to. Your confirmation says fee waived while the rate is 3 percent off Google's mid-market. Both facts coexist legally. Worth checking the rate, not just the fee line. WorldFirst gives you near mid-market rates. Same rate Google shows you. If you're sending money to China and you're still reading the fee line, you're missing where the cost actually lives. Search WorldFirst.

Here's what every business owner sending money to China needs to know about hidden bank fees. You see one of them. You don't see the other. Let's walk through it slowly. The first way is the wire fee. That's the flat charge on top of your transfer. Maybe 25 dollars. Maybe 40 dollars. Whatever your bank lists. This is the number you see on the receipt. The second way is hidden inside the exchange rate. This is the FX margin. FX means foreign exchange, which is just the rate to turn one currency into another. Your bank adds a small markup to that rate. Often 1 percent. Sometimes up to 3 percent. On a 10,000 dollar wire at a 3 percent markup, that's 300 dollars going to your bank. But it never shows up as a fee. It hides inside the rate they quote you. So your receipt can say fee waived, while your bank still kept 300 dollars from the rate. Both things are true. Both things are legal. This is why checking the rate matters, not just the fee line. We built WorldFirst to skip that hidden markup. We give you a rate close to the mid-market rate. That's the same number you see when you search USD to CNY on Google. So the 300 dollars stays in your business. Search WorldFirst.

232 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

  • Bro every video say 300 dollars 300 dollars. Show me actual receipt comparison la.
  • WorldFirst who? Never see them before, got Bank Negara license or not?
  • Google mid market also not the rate I get bro, even your WorldFirst sure got margin.
  • Ok so I save 300. Then customer support, app crash, transfer fail how?

If you wire money to China, you need to hear this. Real talk. Last time you sent 10K to a supplier, two things happened and you only checked one of them. One. Your bank waived the wire fee. You saw fee zero on the confirmation. Win. Two. Your bank quoted you a rate 3 percent off Google's mid-market rate and pocketed 300 dollars in the spread. You didn't see that anywhere. No line item. No receipt. The wire confirmation literally says fee waived next to a rate that just cost you 300 bucks. Both facts are legal. Airwallex surveyed business owners and found 35 percent already say hidden fees are their top frustration. Most still only check the fee line. We built WorldFirst to fix this. Near mid-market rates. #7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 146 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the problem most business owners don't realise when they pay Chinese suppliers. The fee is not the cost. You see fee waived on the wire confirmation. You think the transfer was free. Wrong. Banks have two charging mechanisms running in parallel. Mechanism one is the visible wire fee. 25 to 40 bucks. Sometimes waived as a promo. Mechanism two is the FX rate margin. 1 to 3 percent of the wire amount, embedded in the exchange rate. Not a fee. A margin. Same money. Different label. On a 10K wire at 3 percent margin, that's 300 dollars. Same outcome as a 300 dollar fee. No itemisation. No disclosure. The two facts coexist legally. The bank waived the fee. The bank also kept 300 in the rate. Stop. WorldFirst skips the second mechanism. Near mid-market rates. The rate Google shows when you check USD CNY. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Here's the problem most business owners don't realise when they pay Chinese suppliers. The fee is not the cost. You see fee waived on the wire confirmation. You think the transfer was free. Wrong. Banks have two charging mechanisms running in parallel. Mechanism one is the visible wire fee. 25 to 40 bucks. Sometimes waived as a promo. Mechanism two is the FX rate margin. 1 to 3 percent of the wire amount, embedded in the exchange rate. Not a fee. A margin. Same money. Different label. On a 10K wire at 3 percent margin, that's 300 dollars. Same outcome as a 300 dollar fee. No itemisation. No disclosure. The two facts coexist legally. The bank waived the fee. The bank also kept 300 in the rate. Stop. WorldFirst skips the second mechanism. Near mid-market rates. The rate Google shows when you check USD CNY. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

In this video: the two ways banks charge you when wiring money to China. The fee is not the cost. You see fee waived on the wire confirmation. You think the transfer was free. Wrong. Banks have two charging mechanisms running in parallel. Mechanism one is the visible wire fee. 25 to 40 bucks. Sometimes waived as a promo. Mechanism two is the FX rate margin. 1 to 3 percent of the wire amount, embedded in the exchange rate. Not a fee. A margin. Same money. Different label. On a 10K wire at 3 percent margin, that's 300 dollars. Same outcome as a 300 dollar fee. No itemisation. No disclosure. The two facts coexist legally. The bank waived the fee. In this video: the two ways banks charge you when wiring money to China. The fee is not the real cost. You see fee waived on the wire confirmation. You think the transfer was free. It usually isn't. Banks have two ways to charge you on every wire. They run side by side. Way one is the wire fee. The flat number on top. Maybe 25 dollars, maybe 40 dollars. Sometimes waived as a promo. This one is easy to see. Way two is the FX rate margin. FX stands for foreign exchange, the rate that converts one currency to another. Your bank adds a small markup to that rate. Often 1 to 3 percent of the wire amount, baked right into the rate they quote you. It's not called a fee. It's called a margin. Same money. Different label. On a 10,000 dollar wire at a 3 percent margin, that's 300 dollars. Same outcome as a 300 dollar fee. But no line item. No disclosure. Both facts can sit on the same receipt, legally. The bank waived the fee. The bank also kept 300 dollars in the rate. We built WorldFirst to skip the second way. We give you a rate close to the mid-market rate, the number Google shows when you check USD to CNY. Search WorldFirst.

  • Why I never see this 'two mechanisms' on Maybank app. Show evidence.
  • If both facts legal then WorldFirst also can do this when they grow big. Right?

Here's where the real cost of a wire to China lives, and it's not the fee column. Let us explain. Banks often waive the wire fee as a promo, because that's not where the main money is made. The main money is in the FX rate. FX means foreign exchange, the rate that converts your currency into your supplier's currency. Here's how it plays out. You send 10,000 dollars to China. The confirmation says fee waived. You feel like you won. But the rate the bank used is about 3 percent off the mid-market rate, which is the real rate Google shows. That 3 percent gap is 300 dollars. Your bank kept it without ever calling it a fee. Same outcome as a 300 dollar fee. Different column. So the fee column is the wrong tool to judge what a wire actually cost you. We built WorldFirst to fix that. Near mid-market rates. The rate you see on Google when you search USD to CNY. Same supplier. Same 10,000 dollar wire. The 300 dollars stays in your business. Worth reading the rate, not just the fee column. Search WorldFirst.

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about cross-border wires. The fee line is decoration. The rate is the business. Your bank can advertise no transfer fee on the front of the brochure and quietly mark up the FX rate by 3 percent on the back end. Both facts are legal. Both facts coexist on the same confirmation. On a 10K wire, that 3 percent is 300 dollars. No line item. No notification. The wire receipt literally says fee waived next to a rate that just cost you 300 bucks. 35 percent of business owners say this is their top frustration. Most still don't know it's 300 per 10K. WorldFirst doesn't play this game. Near mid-market rates. The rate Google shows when you search USD CNY. Same wire. The 300 stays with you. That's the difference. Period.

#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 142 words

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Watch this. Three business owners sent 50K to the same Chinese supplier on the same day. Supplier received three different amounts. 49,250. 49,700. 49,850. A 600 dollar spread on identical wires. Now compound it. 12 wires a year at 50K each. 600 thousand dollars in sourcing volume. The business owner who used the bank lost 9,000 dollars. The business owner who used the specialist lost 1,800. That's a 7,200 dollar gap in one year. On the same business. Run it across three years. 21,600 dollars. That's an employee. That's a container of inventory. That's the next product line. And it's not a fee. It's the exchange rate margin. Banks bake 1 to 2% into the rate so you never see the line item. Listen. WorldFirst sits at 0.3% on the same wires. Same day to Chinese bank accounts. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about how wire rate margin compounds across a year. Three business owners sent 50K to the same Chinese supplier on the same day. Supplier received three different amounts. 49,250. 49,700. 49,850. A 600 dollar spread on identical wires. Now compound it. 12 wires a year at 50K each. 600 thousand dollars in sourcing volume. The business owner who used the bank lost around 9,000 dollars. The business owner who used the specialist lost around 1,800. That's a 7,200 dollar gap in one year. On the same business. Run it across three years. 21,600 dollars. That's an employee. That's a container of inventory. That's the next product line. And it's not a fee. It's the exchange rate margin. Here's what every business owner needs to know about how wire rate margin compounds across a year. Three business owners sent 50,000 US dollars to the same Chinese supplier on the same day. The supplier got three different amounts. The first got 49,250. The second got 49,700. The third got 49,850. That is a 600 dollar gap on a wire that should have been identical. Now let us stretch that out over a year. Say you send 12 wires a year. Each one is 50,000 dollars. That is 600,000 dollars a year going to your Chinese suppliers. The business owner who used the major bank lost around 9,000 dollars to the hidden rate margin in those wires. The business owner who used the specialist provider lost around 1,800. That is a 7,200 dollar gap. In one year. On the same business doing the same work. Now run it across three years. That is 21,600 dollars. Think about what that money could be. A salary for a staff member. A full container of inventory. The next product line you have been waiting to launch. And here is the punchline. It is not a fee. There is no line item to point at. It is the exchange rate margin baked into your wire. Most banks add 1 to 2 percent into the rate so you never see it on paper. We sit closer to 0.3 percent. Same wires. Same day to Chinese bank accounts. Search WorldFirst.

In this video: why three identical 50K wires to one supplier land as three different amounts. Three business owners sent the same wire on the same day to the same Chinese supplier. Each one sent 50,000 US dollars. But the supplier got three different amounts. Let us walk through it. Business owner A used a major bank. The supplier got 49,250. Business owner B used a regional bank. The supplier got 49,700. Business owner C used a specialist payment provider. The supplier got 49,850. Same wire. Same day. Same supplier. A 600 dollar gap between the best and the worst. Now here is the part that catches people out. All three of them looked at their receipts and saw the same thing. Wire fee: zero. So all three thought the wire was free. It was not. The wire fee is not the real cost. The exchange rate is the real cost. FX, short for foreign exchange, is the rate your bank uses to convert your money into Chinese yuan. Your bank quietly adds a margin of about 1.5 percent into that rate. You never see it on a line item. On a 50,000 dollar wire, that 1.5 percent is 750 dollars. Now imagine you do 12 wires a year. That is 9,000 dollars going to your bank every year, just from the hidden rate margin. We do this differently at WorldFirst. We sit closer to 0.3 percent. Same wire. The supplier gets close to the full amount. Same day to Chinese bank accounts. Search WorldFirst.

The first got 49,250 landed. The third got 49,850. 600 dollar gap. Now compound it. 12 wires a year. That's 7,200 dollars gone to your bank's rate margin. WorldFirst sits at 0.3% margin. Same wire, same supplier, same day to Chinese accounts. The difference shows up on your P&L. Period.

#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 132 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the problem most business owners don't realize. Two wires to the same supplier on the same day can land at different amounts. Why? The exchange rate. Your bank picks the rate. They don't show you what they picked relative to the real market rate. They just show you the wire fee. Zero. Waived. Free. So you think the wire was free. Three business owners proved this last week. All sent 50K to the same Chinese factory. The bank customer's supplier got 49,250. The regional bank got 49,700. The specialist got 49,850. 600 dollar spread on identical wires. Same line item. Different reality. Stop assuming the fee line tells you the cost. The rate is the cost. WorldFirst sits at 0.3% margin on USD to CNY. Same day. Full amount lands. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Three business owners proved this last week. All sent 50K to the same Chinese factory. The bank customer's supplier got 49,250. The regional bank got 49,700. Here's what every business owner needs to know about why the wire fee is not the real cost. Two wires to the same supplier on the same day can land at different amounts. Why? Because of the exchange rate. Let us break it down. When you wire dollars to China, your bank has to swap your dollars into Chinese yuan, or CNY. Your bank picks the rate they use for that swap. They do not show you what they picked compared to the real market rate. They just show you the wire fee on the receipt. Zero. Waived. Free. So you think the wire was free. It was not. Three business owners proved this last week. All three sent 50,000 US dollars to the same Chinese factory on the same day. The one who used the major bank, their supplier received 49,250. The one who used a regional bank, supplier got 49,700. The one who used a specialist provider, supplier got 49,850. A 600 dollar spread on three identical wires. Same line item on the receipt. Three different realities on the supplier side. The fee line is not the cost. The rate is the cost. We sit closer to 0.3 percent rate margin on US dollar to Chinese yuan. Same day to Chinese accounts. Search WorldFirst.

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about international wires. Three business owners sent 50K each to the same Chinese supplier yesterday. Three different amounts arrived. 49,250. 49,700. 49,850. Why? Banks don't make their money on the wire fee. They make it on the rate. They pick a rate. You can't see what they picked. You can't compare. You just see the receipt that says no fee, wire complete. Meanwhile they pocketed 1 to 2% of the wire in the rate margin. Silently. Every single wire. WorldFirst doesn't do that. 0.3% margin. The rate you see is close to the real rate. Same day to Chinese accounts. That's the difference. Period.

In this video: how banks earn on the rate rather than the wire fee on cross-border payments. Three business owners sent 50,000 US dollars each to the same Chinese supplier yesterday. Three different amounts arrived on the other side. The first got 49,250. The second got 49,700. The third got 49,850. Why does that happen? Because banks do not earn most of their money on the wire fee. They earn it on the rate. Here is how. When you wire dollars to China, the bank has to convert your dollars into Chinese yuan. The bank picks the exchange rate they use. You cannot see what rate they picked compared to the real market rate. You cannot easily compare. You just see the receipt that says no fee, wire complete. Meanwhile, behind that simple receipt, the bank took 1 to 2 percent of the wire as their rate margin. Quietly. Every single wire. We are built differently at WorldFirst. Our margin sits closer to 0.3 percent. The rate you see is close to the real market rate. Same day to Chinese accounts. That is the difference. Search WorldFirst.

181 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Banks earn through rate margin, true, but they also got compliance cost. Not pure profit.
  • Most people don't realise this, but it's literally on the wire confirmation.
  • Quietly took, the rate is on the receipt bro just compare it.
  • Three different amount on same wire same supplier same day, source?
  • Built differently, like every fintech say. Need MAS or BNM license la.
Card P1-A2Pillar P1Short-form 60s · bilingualTopic 01Curiosity-led
#1 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 106 words

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China. You wire on Monday. Supplier says nothing's arrived. Tuesday, nothing. Wednesday, nothing. Thursday, you call your bank. They say it's with the correspondent. You call the correspondent. They say it's with the next correspondent. By Friday it lands. Production starts Monday. You just lost a week. Why? Because your wire is on the longest path. SWIFT through three correspondent banks. There are two faster paths. CIPS, one to two days. A dedicated CNY rail, same-day. Same money. Same destination. Different rails. Stop letting your bank pick the slowest one. WorldFirst routes you on the fastest available rail. Same-day. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Tuesday, nothing. Wednesday, still nothing. Thursday you finally call your bank. They tell you it is with the correspondent bank. So you call that correspondent. Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Watch this. You wire CNY to your supplier on Monday. They see nothing until Friday. That's four working days where production isn't moving. Now do that twelve times a year. That's forty-eight working days a year, almost ten weeks, gone. Why? Because your wire is on path one. SWIFT through correspondent banks. Three to five days, every single time. There are two other paths most business owners have never heard of. CIPS, one to two days. And a dedicated CNY rail, same-day. CIPS alone moved over twenty-four trillion in 2024, up forty-two percent in one year. This is real infrastructure. Most banks just don't tell you about it. Listen. The path you're on costs you weeks every year. Switch the path, get the weeks back. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

In this video: how slow CNY wires quietly cost a business almost ten weeks a year. You wire CNY to your supplier on Monday. They see nothing until Friday. SWIFT through correspondent banks. Three to five days, every single time. There are two other paths most business owners have never heard of. CIPS, one to two days. In this video: how slow CNY wires quietly cost a business almost ten weeks a year. Picture this. You send a CNY payment to your supplier on Monday. By Friday they still have not received it. That is four working days where nothing is happening. The factory is waiting. Your goods are not being made. Now let's do the math together. If you make twelve payments to China in a year, and each one takes four days like this, that adds up to forty-eight working days lost. That is almost ten full weeks gone. Just from waiting on payments. Why does this happen? Because your money is going on the slowest path, called SWIFT. It hops through a bunch of correspondent banks before it gets to your supplier. Three to five days every single time. But here is what most business owners don't know. There are two other paths. The first is called CIPS. That is China's own clearing system. It only takes one to two days. The second is a dedicated CNY rail. That lands the same day you send it. To show you how real this is, CIPS moved over twenty-four trillion CNY in 2024. That number was up forty-two percent from the year before. This is serious infrastructure. Your bank just isn't telling you about it. The path your wire is on is costing you weeks every year. We can help you switch the path. Search WorldFirst.

234 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • 48 days lost a year? Assume my wire always 4 days. Most of mine 2 days lah.
  • 24 trillion? Where this number from? Show source bro.
  • Even if CIPS exist, my bank still use SWIFT. So what to do?
  • Sound like ad. Show me real screenshot of same-day arrival.
  • WorldFirst regulated where? First time hearing the name.
#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 132 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers, your CNY can take three different paths to get there and only one is same-day. Here's the deal. Path one. SWIFT through correspondent banks. Three to five business days. Multiple banks in the chain. Each one takes a fee, each one takes its time. Path two. CIPS, China's own clearing system. One to two days. Cleaner, but most banks abroad don't route through it directly. Path three. A dedicated CNY rail straight into the supplier's account. Same-day when set up right. Most business owners don't know which one their bank is using. So you wire on Monday and your supplier sees nothing until Friday. Production never starts. You eat the delay. Stop. Your wire confirmation tells you which path you're on. Read it. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

SWIFT through correspondent banks. Three to five business days. Multiple banks in the chain. Each one takes a fee, each one takes its time. Path two. CIPS, China's own clearing system. One to two days. Cleaner, but most banks abroad don't route through it directly. Path three. A dedicated CNY rail straight into the supplier's account. Same-day when set up right. deleted / added

Here's what every business owner needs to know about the three paths CNY can take to China. Here's the deal. Path one. SWIFT through correspondent banks. Three to five business days. Multiple banks in the chain. Each one takes a fee, each one takes its time. Path two. CIPS, China's own clearing system. One to two days. Cleaner, but most banks abroad don't route through it directly. Path three. A dedicated CNY rail straight into the supplier's account. Same-day when set up right. Most business owners don't know which one their bank is using. So you wire on Monday and your supplier sees nothing until Friday. Production never starts. You eat the delay. Your wire confirmation tells you which path you're on. Read it. Search WorldFirst.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about the three paths CNY can take to China. If you pay suppliers in China, your money does not just go from your bank to their bank. It takes a path. And there are three paths it can take. Let me walk you through them. The first one is called SWIFT. Think of it like passing a note through a row of people. Your money hops from your bank, to another bank, to another bank, and finally to your supplier. Each bank takes a fee. Each one takes time. The whole trip takes three to five working days. The second one is called CIPS. That's China's own clearing system. It is faster because there are fewer stops along the way. One to two days. But here's the catch. Most banks outside of China don't even connect to it. The third one is a dedicated CNY rail. The money goes straight to your supplier's bank account. Same day. Like a direct flight instead of three layovers. Now here is the problem. Most business owners have no idea which path their bank is using. So you send the money on Monday. Your supplier checks their account. Nothing. Tuesday. Nothing. Friday. Finally there. That whole week is gone. Production has not started. Your shipment is late. We see this happen all the time. Here is what you can do. Look at the wire confirmation your bank sends you. It tells you which path your money is on. Read it. Then search WorldFirst.

250 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • My Maybank wire to China take 2 days only, not 5 lah. Don't generalise.
  • What is this 'dedicated CNY rail'? Got name or not? Sound vague.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Never heard. Got SC license in Malaysia?
  • My supplier got cash flow, don't need full amount land before production.
  • Read wire confirmation? Most banks don't show routing detail one.

A dedicated CNY rail. Same-day. Money lands the same business day. Almost no one uses it because almost no one knows it exists. So here's what happens. You wire Monday. Supplier sees nothing until Friday. Production stalls. Lead time blown. Customer waiting. Margin bleeding. All because you defaulted to path one. Stop. Pick the path. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Here's what anyone paying Chinese suppliers needs to know about the three CNY paths. Your CNY payments can take three different paths to get there. Most business owners only know about one of them. Let me show you all three. The first path is SWIFT through correspondent banks. It takes three to five days. The money hops through multiple banks. Each one takes a fee. This is the default at almost every bank you might use. The second path is called CIPS. It is direct clearing through China's own system. One to two days. Lower cost. The problem is, most banks outside China do not connect to it directly. The third path is a dedicated CNY rail. The money lands the same business day. Almost nobody uses this because almost nobody knows it exists. Here is what happens when you are on path one. You send the money Monday. Your supplier sees nothing all week. Friday it finally lands. The factory is behind. Your lead time is blown. Your customer is waiting on you. Your margins are bleeding. All because you used the default path without thinking about it. Pick the path yourself next time. Search WorldFirst.

Here's a plain look at why your bank picks one CNY path and not the others. They picked the slowest path on purpose to send your CNY to China. Let me explain how that works. There are three ways your money can reach a supplier in China. The first is SWIFT. That takes three to five days. The second is CIPS. That takes one to two days. The third is a dedicated CNY rail. That lands the same day. Now think about which one your bank uses. It is SWIFT. Pretty much always. Here is why. Every bank in the SWIFT chain takes a small cut as your money passes through. You might be thinking your bank just does not know any better. Trust us. They know. They picked the slow one anyway. So your supplier waits four days. The factory does not start. You miss your delivery date. You take the loss. Here is the thing. The path is a choice. Your bank picked one for you. You can pick a different one. Search WorldFirst.

#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 124 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the part most business owners don't realize when they pay China. There isn't one wire. There are three. Three different mechanisms, three different speeds, three different costs. Mechanism one. SWIFT. A messaging network. Your money hops bank to bank to bank. Three to five days, every hop takes a fee. Mechanism two. CIPS. China's own clearing system. Direct settlement, one to two days, fewer hops, lower cost. Mechanism three. A dedicated CNY rail built into the receiving bank. Same-day. The money lands in the supplier's account the day you send it. Most banks default you to mechanism one because it's their default. They don't pick the fastest one for you. They pick the easiest one for them. Stop accepting the default. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Here's the part most business owners don't realize when they pay China. There isn't one wire. There are three. Three different mechanisms, three different speeds, three different costs. Direct settlement, one to two days, fewer hops, lower cost. Mechanism three. A dedicated CNY rail built into the receiving bank. Same-day. The money lands in the supplier's account the day you send it. Most banks default you to mechanism one because it's their default. They don't pick the fastest one for you. They pick the easiest one for them. Stop accepting the default. PeriodWorth knowing the difference. Search WorldFirst. Period.

In this video: the three mechanisms a CNY payment can use, explained simply. There isn't one wire. There are three. Three different mechanisms, three different speeds, three different costs. Mechanism one. SWIFT. A messaging network. Your money hops bank to bank to bank. Three to five days, every hop takes a fee. Mechanism two. CIPS. China's own clearing system. Direct settlement, one to two days, fewer hops, lower cost. Mechanism three. A dedicated CNY rail built into the receiving bank. Same-day. In this video: the three mechanisms a CNY payment can use, explained simply. When you send a wire, you might think there is only one type of wire. That is wrong. There are actually three different ways the money can move. Three different systems. Three different speeds. Three different costs. Let me walk you through each one. The first system is called SWIFT. It is a messaging network. Your money hops from your bank to another bank to another bank. Like a relay race. Three to five days to land. Every stop along the way takes a small fee. The second system is called CIPS. That is China's own clearing system. The money settles more directly. One to two days. Fewer stops. Lower cost. The third one is a dedicated CNY rail. It is built straight into the bank where your supplier holds their account. The money lands the same day you send it. So why does your bank stick you with system one? Because that is what they default to. It is not the fastest one for you. It is the easiest one for them. Now you know the difference. Search WorldFirst.

192 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)

  • Same-day land really? Or land in WorldFirst account first then takes another day?
  • Easiest for them, fine. But how WorldFirst make money then?
  • Who regulate WorldFirst here? Got Malaysia license?
  • Need to see actual rate and fee comparison vs my bank, not just speed.

In this video: the three ways CNY can move to a Chinese supplier, explained simply. You probably think there is one way to send CNY to China. There are actually three. Let me walk through all of them. The first path is SWIFT. Your money goes through a chain of correspondent banks. Three to five days. The second path is CIPS. That is China's own clearing system. One to two days. The third path is a dedicated CNY rail. The money goes straight into your supplier's account. Same day. Here is the catch. Most banks outside China only use the first path. They do not even tell you the other two exist. So you wait. Your supplier waits. The factory waits. Production is sitting still. Here is where we come in. At WorldFirst, we route you onto the fastest path that is available for your supplier's bank. Same day to Chinese accounts. The money lands the same day you send it. Worth knowing your options. Search WorldFirst.

164 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • My bank actually mention SWIFT vs faster route before, just expensive.
  • WorldFirst routes me on fastest? What if no fast route exist for my supplier's bank?
  • Who is WorldFirst? Got license to operate Malaysia?
  • Same-day mean same calendar day or business day?
  • Three paths useful. But how WorldFirst handle compliance, AML check?
#9 PG3 Gary Vee Opinion-led 118 words

Stop using your bank / right-tool reframe

Soc4.0 Aud4.2 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Hammer/wood analogy is plain English and lands. 'Use the right tool for the job' is generic but clean.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Stop sending CNY through SWIFT. I'm serious. SWIFT is a tool. It's the wrong tool for China. SWIFT was built to move money between banks that don't have a direct relationship. So your money hops three, four banks before it lands. Three to five days. Multiple fees. That's not a payment, that's a relay race. China has its own clearing system called CIPS. One to two days, direct settlement. And there's a third path, a dedicated CNY rail, that lands same-day. You wouldn't drive cross-country when there's a flight. So why are you wiring CNY on the slowest route there is? WorldFirst picks the fast route for you. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Use the right tool. Search WorldFirst. Now.

In this video: why SWIFT is the wrong tool for paying China, explained simply. SWIFT is a tool. It's the wrong tool for China. SWIFT was built to move money between banks that don't have a direct relationship. So your money hops three, four banks before it lands. Three to five days. Multiple fees. That's not a payment, that's a relay race. China has its own clearing system called CIPS. One to two days, direct settlement. And there's a third path, a dedicated CNY rail, that lands same-day. In this video: why SWIFT is the wrong tool for paying China, explained simply. SWIFT is a tool. It is just not the right tool for paying China. Let me explain why. SWIFT was originally built to move money between banks that do not have a direct relationship with each other. That means your money has to hop from bank, to bank, to bank, sometimes three or four banks, before it finally lands at your supplier. That trip takes three to five days. Every stop charges a fee. That is not really a payment. That is a relay race. Now China has its own clearing system called CIPS. That handles payments directly. One to two days. Way cleaner. And then there is a third path. A dedicated CNY rail. That lands the same day you send it. Think of it this way. You would not drive across the country if there is a direct flight. So why are you sending CNY on the slowest route? At WorldFirst, we pick the fast route for you. Same day to Chinese accounts. Use the right tool. Search WorldFirst.

  • WorldFirst pick the fast route, but I still pay fee right? How much?
  • First time hearing WorldFirst, Got SC Malaysia license?

#10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 116 words

Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatHigh

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about paying China. There isn't one path. There's three. And your bank only uses the slowest one. Path one is SWIFT. Three to five days. Multiple banks in the chain, each one taking a fee. This is what your bank defaults you to. Path two is CIPS, China's own clearing system. One to two days. Cleaner. Cheaper. But it requires your bank to plug into China's rails, and most don't. Path three is a dedicated CNY rail straight into the supplier's bank. Same-day. The money lands the day you send it. The reason nobody talks about path two and three? They aren't the bank's default, and the default makes them money. WorldFirst is different. Period.

Here's the truthsomething nobody's telling you about paying China. There isn't one path. There's three. And your bank only uses the slowest one. Path one is SWIFT. Three to five days. Multiple banks in the chain, each one taking a fee. This is what your bank defaults you to. Path two is CIPS, China's own clearing system. One to two days. Cleaner. Cheaper. But it requires your bank to plug into China's rails, and most don't. Path three is a dedicated CNY rail straight into the supplier's bank. Same-day. The money lands the day you send it. The reason nobody talks about path two and three? They aren't the bank's default, and the default makes them moneythe default. WorldFirst is different. Periodbuilt around the faster ones.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about the three paths a CNY wire can take. There isn't one path. There's three. And your bank only uses the slowest one. Path one is SWIFT. Three to five days. Multiple banks in the chain, each one taking a fee. This is what your bank defaults you to. Path two is CIPS, China's own clearing system. One to two days. Cleaner. Cheaper. But it requires your bank to plug into China's rails, and most don't. Path three is a dedicated CNY rail straight into the supplier's bank. Same-day. The money lands the day you send it. Here's what every business owner needs to know about the three paths a CNY wire can take. When you send a wire, you probably think there is just one way the money goes. There are actually three. And your bank is only using the slowest one. Let me walk through all three. Path one is SWIFT. Three to five days. Your money goes through a chain of banks. Each bank takes a small fee along the way. This is what your bank defaults you to. Path two is CIPS. That is China's own clearing system. One to two days. Cleaner and cheaper. But for your bank to use it, they have to plug directly into China's rails. Most banks just do not. Path three is a dedicated CNY rail. The money goes straight into your supplier's bank. Same day. So why doesn't anyone talk about paths two and three? Because they are not the default. The default is what banks have always done. At WorldFirst, we built our system around the faster ones.
