#1 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 131 words

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China. You think your bank is moving your money. They're not. They're just sending a message through SWIFT. Real correspondent banks in the middle are moving it. 1, 2, 3 of them. Each one takes a $15 to $50 cut. You wire $50,000, supplier sees $49,800. You call the bank. They blame the intermediary. The intermediary blames the receiving bank in China. Nobody takes responsibility. Meanwhile your supplier won't ship until the gap is topped up. You're losing days. Your customer is waiting. Your margin is bleeding. And you're on the phone with a teller who's never heard the word correspondent before. I see this every week. Every single week. Stop. WorldFirst. Direct rail to Chinese accounts. Same-day. Full $50,000 lands. Supplier ships. You sleep. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Here's what happens when a China wire lands short, explained for any business owner. You think your bank is moving your money. They're not. They're just sending a message through SWIFT. Real correspondent banks in the middle are moving it. 1, 2, 3 of them. Each one takes a $15 to $50 cut. You wire $50,000, supplier sees $49,800. You call the bank. They blame the intermediary. The intermediary blames the receiving bank in China. Nobody takes responsibility. Meanwhile your supplier won't ship until the gap is topped up. You're losing days. Your customer is waiting. Here's what happens when a China wire lands short, explained for any business owner. You probably think your bank is moving your money to your supplier. They are not. Your bank is just sending a message through a system called SWIFT. The real work is done by other banks. They sit in the middle of the chain. There can be one, two, or three of them. Every one of them takes a small cut, usually fifteen to fifty dollars, off your wire as it passes through. So you wire fifty thousand. Your supplier sees forty nine thousand eight hundred. You call your bank. They blame the middle bank. The middle bank blames the receiving bank in China. Nobody wants to take responsibility. Meanwhile your supplier will not ship until you fill the gap. You are losing days. Your customer is waiting. Your profit margin is bleeding away. And you are on the phone with a teller who has never even heard the word correspondent before. This happens every single week. At WorldFirst, we use a direct rail to Chinese accounts. Same day. The full fifty thousand lands. Your supplier ships. You sleep. Search WorldFirst.

191 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Call the bank, they blame intermediary, intermediary blame CN bank. Story of my life bro.
  • Teller never heard of correspondent? Maybe try the priority banking line lah.
  • Direct rail to Chinese accounts. Including state banks like ICBC and Bank of China?
  • Full $50,000 lands. Including covering the CN bank receiving fees too or not?
  • Sleep at night? Sure. But also need to do KYC with WorldFirst first, that's also effort.
#2 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 142 words

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Watch this. You wire $50,000 to your Chinese supplier. They get $49,800. You just lost $200 and your bank gave you zero warning. Now do that twice a month. That's $400 a month. $4,800 a year. Over five years, three middle banks quietly took $24,000 from your business. That's a hire. That's a trade show. That's three months of inventory. And here's the part that makes it worse. The supplier won't ship until the full amount lands. So now you wire another $200. Plus another wire fee. Plus another week of delay. Plus your customer is waiting. The cause is one default setting on your SWIFT MT103. Field 71A. Set to SHA. Stop. WorldFirst routes payments through MYbank straight to Chinese accounts. No SWIFT chain. No middle banks. No deductions. Same-day. The full amount lands. Every wire. Every month. Every year. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Here's what every business owner should know about the real cost of wiring China. You wire $50,000 to your Chinese supplier. They get $49,800. You just lost $200 and your bank gave you zero warning. Now do that twice a month. That's $400 a month. $4,800 a year. Over five years, three middle banks quietly took $24,000 from your business. That's a hire. That's a trade show. That's three months of inventory. And here's the part that makes it worse. The supplier won't ship until the full amount lands. So now you wire another $200. Plus another wire fee. Plus another week of delay. Plus your customer is waiting. The cause is one default setting on your SWIFT MT103. Field 71A. Set to SHA. WorldFirst routes payments through MYbank straight to Chinese accounts. No SWIFT chain. No middle banks. No deductions. Same-day. The full amount lands. Every wire. Every month. Every year. Search WorldFirst.

Here's what every business owner should know about the real cost of wiring China. You wire fifty thousand dollars to your supplier in China. They only get forty nine thousand eight hundred. You just lost two hundred dollars. Your bank did not warn you. Now imagine you do that twice a month, because that is how often most business owners pay their factories. That is four hundred dollars gone every month. Four thousand eight hundred dollars gone every year. Now stretch that out over five years. Three middle banks have quietly taken twenty four thousand dollars from your business. Think about what that money could have been. It could have paid for a new hire. It could have paid for a trade show booth. It could have paid for three months of inventory. Here is what makes the story worse. Your supplier will not ship until the full amount lands. So now you have to wire another two hundred. Plus you pay another wire fee. Plus you wait another week. And your customer is waiting too. The cause of all this is one tiny setting on the form your bank fills out. The form is called a SWIFT MT103. The setting is in a box called field 71A. It is set to SHA by default. At WorldFirst, we route your payments through MYbank straight to Chinese bank accounts. No SWIFT chain. No middle banks. Same day. The full amount lands. Every wire. Every month. Every year. Search WorldFirst.

236 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • $24K over 5 years from $200? Sure or not. You assume every wire loss is $200, that's the max case.
  • I don't wire $50K twice a month bro. Math don't apply to me.
  • Three months of inventory? Mine $24K is more like one week of inventory. Numbers feel cherry-picked.
  • Field 71A SHA? You expect me to log into SWIFT myself? My bank handle this lah.
  • Got example from real importer or just math? Show me proof.
#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 145 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Listen. If you wire money to Chinese suppliers, your bank is robbing you in plain sight. Here's how. You send $50,000. Supplier WhatsApps you saying $49,800 landed. Where did the $200 go? Not a scam. Not a math error. Correspondent banks. SWIFT wires hop through 1 to 3 middle banks before they hit China. Each bank shaves $15 to $50 directly off the wire. Your bank never tells you. Supplier just sees a smaller number. Now they refuse to ship until you top up. You eat the $200. You eat the delay. You eat the next order too. Stop. The default code on your wire is SHA. SHA means the supplier eats every middle-bank cut. Change it to OUR and the supplier gets the full amount. Or skip SWIFT entirely. WorldFirst routes direct to China through MYbank. No middle banks. No deductions. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Listen. If you wire money to Chinese suppliers, your bank is robbing you in plain sightmay be quietly taking from you. Here's how. You send $50,000. Supplier WhatsApps you saying $49,800 landed. Where did the $200 go? Not a scam. Not a math error. Correspondent banks. SWIFT wires hop through 1 to 3 middle banks before they hit China. Each bank shaves $15 to $50 directly off the wire. Your bank never tells you. Supplier just sees a smaller number. Now they refuse to ship until you top up. You eat the $200. You eat the delay. You eat the next order too. Stop. The default code on your wire is SHA. SHA means the supplier eats every middle-bank cut. Change it to OUR and the supplier gets the full amount. Or skip SWIFT entirely. WorldFirst routes direct to China through MYbank. No middle banks. No deductions. Period. Worth a look. Search WorldFirst. Period.

deleted / added

In this video: why your China supplier receives less than you wire, explained simply. Here's how. You send $50,000. Supplier WhatsApps you saying $49,800 landed. Where did the $200 go? Not a scam. Not a math error. Correspondent banks. SWIFT wires hop through 1 to 3 middle banks before they hit China. Each bank shaves $15 to $50 directly off the wire. Your bank never tells you. Supplier just sees a smaller number. Now they refuse to ship until you top up. You eat the $200. You eat the delay. You eat the next order too. The default code on your wire is SHA. SHA means the supplier eats every middle-bank cut. Change it to OUR and the supplier gets the full amount. Or skip SWIFT entirely. WorldFirst routes direct to China through MYbank. No middle banks. No deductions. Worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

In this video: why your China supplier receives less than you wire, explained simply. You send $50,000 to your supplier in China. A few hours later, your supplier sends you a message. It says only $49,800 arrived. You read it twice. You sent fifty thousand. So where did the other two hundred go? It is not a scam. It is not a math mistake. The answer is something called correspondent banks. When you send money through SWIFT, your wire does not go straight to your supplier. It stops at one, two, or even three other banks first. These banks sit in the middle. Every single one of them is allowed to take a small slice off the top. Usually between fifteen and fifty dollars each. Your bank does not warn you. Your supplier just sees a smaller number land in the account. Now your supplier will not start production until you send the missing two hundred. You wait. Your customer waits. Your shipment is late. Here's the part that makes us pause. The reason this happens is one little code on your wire. The code is called SHA. SHA is the default. SHA means the supplier pays for every middle bank cut, not you. If you switch it to OUR, then you pay, and your supplier gets the full amount. Or you can skip the whole chain. At WorldFirst, we send your money straight to China through a partner called MYbank. No middle banks. No silent cuts. Worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

249 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • $50K wire one time only? Bro I never send that big lah. My orders maybe $5K.
  • SHA, OUR, what code? My bank app got no such thing meh.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Never hear before. Got Bank Negara license or not?
  • My supplier always confirm full amount one. Maybe got better banker?
  • MYbank? That's Alipay one right? How they connect to my Maybank account?

If you wire money to China, you need to hear this. Real talk. The last time you sent $50,000 to a supplier, four things happened and you saw zero of them. One. Your bank charged you a wire fee. Maybe $30. That you saw on the statement. Two. Correspondent bank A took $50 off the wire. You didn't see that. Three. Correspondent bank B took another $35. You didn't see that either. Four. Correspondent bank C took $80. Still invisible. Supplier received $49,835 instead of $50,000. WhatsApp ping. 'Short $165, please top up.' Now you wire another $200. Another fee. Another week. Your customer is waiting. Your supplier is annoyed. Your margin is bleeding. Stop wiring through SWIFT for China payments. WorldFirst routes direct to Chinese accounts through MYbank. No correspondent chain. Same-day. The number you send is the number that lands. If you're a business owner paying China, you need WorldFirst. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

#5 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 150 words

Accusation opener

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Three banks you've never heard of just stole $200 from your last wire to China. Here's how. You wired $50,000. Your supplier received $49,800. You assumed the gap was a fee. It wasn't. SWIFT moves your money through a chain of correspondent banks. 1, 2, sometimes 3 of them sit between your bank and the supplier's bank. Each one is allowed to deduct $15 to $50 directly from the wire amount before it moves to the next hop. Your bank never disclosed it because the default SWIFT code on your wire, SHA, says the supplier eats those deductions. Not you. So your supplier got short, refused to start production, and you lost a week. I'll say it. Banks have been running this default for decades. WorldFirst is different. Direct rail through MYbank into Chinese accounts. No correspondent chain. No silent deductions. The number you send is the number that lands. Stop signing off on the SHA default. Search WorldFirst. Period.

This video explains where the missing 200 on your China wire actually went. Here's how. You wired $50,000. Your supplier received $49,800. You assumed the gap was a fee. It wasn't. SWIFT moves your money through a chain of correspondent banks. 1, 2, sometimes 3 of them sit between your bank and the supplier's bank. This video explains where the missing 200 on your China wire actually went. Here is how it happened. You wired fifty thousand dollars. Your supplier only got forty nine thousand eight hundred. You probably thought the gap was just a regular fee. It was not. When you send money through SWIFT, your wire does not travel in one straight line. It hops through a chain of banks called correspondent banks. There can be one, two, or even three of these banks sitting between your bank and your supplier's bank. Each one is allowed to take fifteen to fifty dollars right off the wire before passing it along. Your bank did not tell you because there is a default code on your wire called SHA. SHA means your supplier pays for those cuts, not you. So your supplier got less money than they expected. They would not start making your order. And you lost a whole week of time. Banks have been running this default setting for decades. At WorldFirst, we do things differently. We use a direct rail through MYbank that goes straight into Chinese bank accounts. No chain of middle banks. No silent cuts. The amount you send is the amount that lands. Search WorldFirst.

212 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Took? Banks disclose in fine print what. Not theft if I tick the box.
  • Number you send equals number that lands? Sure or not? Chinese bank also got receiving fee meh.
  • Banks been doing decades, WorldFirst only how many years? Why I trust new player?
  • Three banks I never heard of? Show me the wire trace then. Don't just claim.
  • Ok so SHA bad. Why my bank not just default to OUR if it's better?
#6 PG2 Gary Vee Opinion-led 113 words

I'll say it opinion-as-truth + compound math

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

I'll say it. The most expensive part of your supply chain is the banks in the middle that you've never heard of. You wire $50,000 to your Chinese supplier. They get $49,800. That's $200 gone. One wire. Now run the math. 12 wires a year. That's $2,400. 24 wires. $4,800. Five years of that. $24,000. That's not a fee. That's correspondent banks deducting from the wire on the way through. SWIFT lets them. Your bank's default is SHA, which means the supplier eats every cut. So they short the order. You eat the gap. You eat the delay too. WorldFirst doesn't route through the SWIFT chain. Direct to Chinese accounts via MYbank. Same-day. Full amount lands. Period.

This video: the hidden bank costs every business owner paying China should understand. You wire $50,000 to your Chinese supplier. They get $49,800. That's $200 gone. One wire. Now run the math. 12 wires a year. That's $2,400. 24 wires. $4,800. Five years of that. $24,000. That's not a fee. That's correspondent banks deducting from the wire on the way through. SWIFT lets them. Your bank's default is SHA, which means the supplier eats every cut. So they short the order. You eat the gap. You eat the delay too. This video: the hidden bank costs every business owner paying China should understand. The most expensive part of your whole supply chain might be the banks in the middle that you have never even heard of. Let me show you what we mean. You wire fifty thousand dollars to your supplier in China. They only get forty nine thousand eight hundred. That is two hundred dollars gone. Just one wire. Now do the math with us. If you send twelve wires a year, that is two thousand four hundred dollars gone. If you send twenty four wires a year, that is four thousand eight hundred. Now stretch it out over five years. That is twenty four thousand dollars. Gone. That is not a fee on a bank statement. That is correspondent banks taking a piece of the wire as it passes through their hands. SWIFT lets them do it. Your bank's default setting is called SHA. SHA means your supplier pays for every cut, not you. So your supplier sends a short order. You eat the missing money. You also eat the delay. At WorldFirst, we do not route through the SWIFT chain. We go direct to Chinese accounts through MYbank. Same day. The full amount lands.

203 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Most expensive part of supply chain? Bro freight is more lah. By far.
  • $200 every wire compound to $24K? Only if you assume worst case 24 wires a year of exactly $200 short. Cherry-picked.
  • SHA default = supplier eats. Ok this part I learn something new actually.
  • MYbank direct, no SWIFT chain. Need to see Bank Negara approval before I switch.
  • WorldFirst sound like Wise. Same thing or different?
#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 143 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the problem most business owners don't realize when they wire money to China. SWIFT is not a payment network. It's a messaging network. SWIFT doesn't move your money. Real banks do. Real banks called correspondents sit between your bank and your supplier's bank. A US to China wire usually hits 1 to 3 of them. Each correspondent is allowed to deduct its own processing fee, usually $15 to $50, directly from the wire amount. Your bank never warns you because the default field on your SWIFT message, 71A, is set to SHA. SHA means the supplier eats the chain. So you send $50,000. Supplier gets $49,800. They refuse to ship. You eat a week. This is how the SWIFT default works. Stop. WorldFirst doesn't use the SWIFT chain for China payments. Direct MYbank routing. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Full amount lands. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Each correspondent is allowed to deduct its own processing fee, usually $15 to $50, directly from the wire amount. Your bank never warns you because the default field on your SWIFT message, 71A, is set to SHA. SHA means the supplier eats the chain. So you send $50,000. Supplier gets $49,800. They refuse to ship. You eat a week. This is how the SWIFT default works. Stop. WorldFirst doesn't use the SWIFT chain for China payments. Direct MYbank routing. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Full amount lands. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Here's what business owners need to know about how SWIFT wires to China actually work. SWIFT is not a payment network. It's a messaging network. SWIFT doesn't move your money. Real banks do. Real banks called correspondents sit between your bank and your supplier's bank. A US to China wire usually hits 1 to 3 of them. Each correspondent is allowed to deduct its own processing fee, usually $15 to $50, directly from the wire amount. Your bank never warns you because the default field on your SWIFT message, 71A, is set to SHA. SHA means the supplier eats the chain. So you send $50,000. Supplier gets $49,800. They refuse to ship. You eat a week. This is how the SWIFT default works. WorldFirst doesn't use the SWIFT chain for China payments. Direct MYbank routing. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Full amount lands. Here's what business owners need to know about how SWIFT wires to China actually work. SWIFT is not a payment network. SWIFT is a messaging network. That means SWIFT does not actually move your money. It just sends instructions. The real work is done by real banks. These real banks are called correspondent banks. They sit between your bank and your supplier's bank. When you send a wire from the US to China, it usually passes through one, two, or three of these correspondent banks. Each of them is allowed to take a small fee right off your wire. Usually somewhere between fifteen and fifty dollars. Your bank never warns you because there is a default setting on your wire form called field 71A. It is set to SHA. SHA means your supplier eats the cost of all those middle bank fees. So when you send fifty thousand dollars, your supplier might only get forty nine thousand eight hundred. They will refuse to ship until you make up the difference. You lose a whole week. That is just how the SWIFT default works. At WorldFirst, we do not use the SWIFT chain for China payments. We use a direct rail through MYbank that goes same day into Chinese accounts. The full amount lands. Search WorldFirst.

215 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Ok the SWIFT-is-messaging thing actually makes sense. But always 1 to 3? My direct wires to HSBC HK only one hop sometimes.
  • MYbank direct routing, is it legal for Malaysian SMB to use? Got AML clearance?
  • Sounds like the SHA default is the real problem. Can I just call my bank and switch to OUR?
  • Same-day to China? Even my Wise transfer take 1 day sometimes lah.
  • Looks like ad but the mechanism part is solid. Need pricing comparison to be convinced.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about why China wires arrive short. You wired fifty thousand dollars to your factory. A few hours later, your supplier sends you a WhatsApp. They say only forty nine thousand eight hundred landed. Your first thought is the supplier is lying. Your second thought is your bank made a mistake. Neither one is right. Here is what really happened. Your wire went through SWIFT. SWIFT wires hop through one to three middle banks called correspondent banks before they reach China. Each of those middle banks is allowed to take fifteen to fifty dollars right off the wire. You got no notification. There is no line item on any statement. It is just built into the default settings. Now your supplier will not ship until you top up the missing money. You lose a week. You lose the delivery. You might even lose the customer. At WorldFirst, we skip the SWIFT chain entirely. We use a direct rail through MYbank that goes straight to Chinese bank accounts. Same day. The amount you send is the amount that lands. Worth knowing if middle banks are taking a slice of every payment. Search WorldFirst.

#10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 127 words

Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about international wires to China. SWIFT is not one bank moving your money. SWIFT is a chain of 2, 3, sometimes 4 banks passing the message along. Each one in the chain is allowed to take a cut off the wire amount before passing it on. Your bank's default setting, the SHA code on field 71A, says the supplier eats those cuts. Not you. So you send $50,000. Three middle banks take a slice. Supplier sees $49,800. Refuses to start production. You're now scrambling. The bank won't help because technically nothing went wrong. This is how it works. Every single time. WorldFirst doesn't play this game. Direct rail to Chinese accounts through MYbank. No correspondent chain. The rate you see, the amount you send, is what lands. Period.

So you send $50,000. Three middle banks take a slice. Supplier sees $49,800. Won't start production. You're now scrambling. The bank can't reverse it because technically nothing went wrong. In this video: how SWIFT wires to China really work, for any business owner. SWIFT is not one bank moving your money from point A to point B. SWIFT is actually a chain of two, three, or sometimes four banks passing a message down the line. Every single bank in that chain is allowed to take a small cut off your wire amount before passing it on to the next one. Your bank has a default setting called SHA. It sits in a box on the form called field 71A. SHA means your supplier pays for all of those cuts, not you. So here is what happens. You send fifty thousand dollars. Three middle banks each take a slice. Your supplier sees forty nine thousand eight hundred land in the account. They will not start production until the missing money shows up. Now you are scrambling. You call your bank. They cannot reverse it because, on paper, nothing actually went wrong. This is how the system works. Every single time. At WorldFirst, we built things differently. We use a direct rail to Chinese accounts through MYbank. There is no chain of middle banks. The amount you send is the amount that lands.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China. You think your bank moves the money. They don't. They hand it to another bank. That bank hands it to another bank. By the time it reaches Shenzhen, three middlemen each took a day and 50 bucks. You call the bank to chase. They blame the correspondent. The correspondent blames the receiving bank. The receiving bank blames compliance. Compliance says the beneficiary name was missing Co Ltd. Nobody owns it. Meanwhile your supplier is texting you every day at 2am asking where the money is. Your factory slot is gone. Your customer is asking why. I see this every single week. Stop. WorldFirst. CIPS direct to Chinese accounts. Same-day. Full amount. One platform, one accountability line. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about who owns a missing China wire. You think your bank moves your money to your supplier. They don't. Your bank hands it to another bank. That bank hands it to another bank. And that bank hands it to a fourth bank. By the time the money reaches Shenzhen, three banks in the middle have each held it for a day and taken about 50 dollars off the top. So you pick up the phone and call your bank to chase. They tell you it's not their problem, the money is with the correspondent bank. You call the correspondent. They say no, we already passed it on to the receiving bank. The receiving bank says it's stuck in compliance. Compliance says the supplier's name on the wire was missing Co Ltd at the end, so we paused it. Nobody owns the problem. Meanwhile your supplier is texting you every day at 2am Shenzhen time, asking where the money is. Your factory slot is already gone, given to someone else. Your customer is asking why their order is late. This happens every single week to business owners we talk to. We built WorldFirst to fix this. CIPS rails go direct to Chinese accounts. Same day. Full amount lands. One platform, one accountability line. If something goes wrong, you call us, not four different banks. Search WorldFirst.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about the true cost of SWIFT wires to China. Your wire to China was supposed to clear in 2 days. It's day 5 now. Your supplier still hasn't seen a single dollar. The factory slot you booked is already gone. Here's exactly why this keeps happening. There are three middle banks in the wire chain. They're called correspondent banks. Each one takes a full working day to handle the money. So that's 3 working days lost, right there, before anything else. Now each of those three banks also takes a small fee. Usually 15 to 50 dollars each. That's 150 dollars gone from a 50,000 dollar wire. And you'll never spot it, because it doesn't show up as a fee on your statement. It just quietly disappears from the amount that lands. Now let's do the math. Say you pay suppliers in China twice a month. That's 300 dollars a month, every month, going to banks you've never heard of. Multiply that by 12 months. 3,600 dollars a year. Multiply that by 5 years of running your business. 18,000 dollars. Eighteen thousand. Quietly siphoned out of your supplier payments by banks in the middle. And that's just the money. You also burned 60 days of factory time over those 5 years, waiting for delayed wires. Sixty days. That's a holiday you could have taken. That's an extra inventory order. That's a new hire you could have made. We built WorldFirst to skip the whole SWIFT chain. We use CIPS rails that go straight to Chinese accounts. Same day landing. Full amount, no invisible cut. Search WorldFirst.

#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 160 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Listen. If you're a business owner wiring money to Chinese suppliers, your wire is sitting in a queue right now and you don't even know it. Here's the deal. Your bank quoted 2 days. It's day 5. Your supplier is still chasing you. Two things are happening. One. Correspondent banks. A US to China SWIFT wire hops through 1 to 3 of them. Each one takes a full business day to process. Each one shaves 15 to 50 dollars in fees before passing the money on. Two. Compliance review. Chinese banks check the beneficiary name letter for letter. If your wire says Shenzhen ABC Trading but the registered name is Shenzhen ABC Trading Co Ltd, the wire pauses for 3 to 10 days. Sometimes it gets returned. So on a 50K wire your supplier sees 49,850 land a week late. Stop using SWIFT for China. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Listen. If you're a business owner wiring money to Chinese suppliers, your wire is sitting in a queue right now and you don't even know it. Here's the dealHere's how it works. Your bank quoted 2 days. It's day 5. Your supplier is still chasing you. Two things are happening. One. Correspondent banks. A US to China SWIFT wire hops through 1 to 3 of them. Each one takes a full business day to process. Each one shaves 15 to 50 dollars in fees before passing the money on. Two. Compliance review. Chinese banks check the beneficiary name letter for letter. If your wire says Shenzhen ABC Trading but the registered name is Shenzhen ABC Trading Co Ltd, the wire pauses for 3 to 10 days. Sometimes it gets returned. So on a 50K wire your supplier sees 49,850 land a week late. Stop using SWIFT for China. Period. WorldFirst routes outside the SWIFT chain via CIPS and MYbank rails. Most payments clear same-day to Chinese accounts. Search WorldFirst. Period.

deleted / added

In this video: why a China wire lands late and short, explained simply. Here's how it works. Your bank quoted 2 days. It's day 5. Your supplier is still chasing you. Two things are happening. One. Correspondent banks. A US to China SWIFT wire hops through 1 to 3 of them. Each one takes a full business day to process. Each one shaves 15 to 50 dollars in fees before passing the money on. Two. Compliance review. Chinese banks check the beneficiary name letter for letter. If your wire says Shenzhen ABC Trading but the registered name is Shenzhen ABC Trading Co Ltd, the wire pauses for 3 to 10 days. Sometimes it gets returned. So on a 50K wire your supplier sees 49,850 land a week late. WorldFirst routes outside the SWIFT chain via CIPS and MYbank rails. Most payments clear same-day to Chinese accounts. Search WorldFirst.

In this video: why a China wire lands late and short, explained simply. You run a business. You pay suppliers in China. You sent a wire and your bank told you it would land in 2 days. It's been 5 days now. Your supplier is texting you, asking where the money is. You have no idea what to tell them. Here's what's actually going on behind the scenes. When you send a wire to China, the money doesn't fly straight from your bank to your supplier's bank. It hops through other banks first. Real banks, in the middle of the chain. Usually 1, 2, or 3 of them. Each bank takes a full working day to process the money before passing it on. So 3 banks means 3 days right there, before anything else happens. And here's the part nobody tells you. Each of those middle banks quietly takes 15 to 50 dollars in fees. They don't ask. They don't show it on your statement. They just take it off the top. So your 50,000 dollar wire shrinks before it lands. Then there's a second problem. When the money finally reaches a Chinese bank, the bank checks the supplier's name on the wire against the supplier's official registered company name. Letter by letter. If your wire says Shenzhen ABC Trading, but the registered name has Co Ltd at the end, the bank pauses the wire. Sometimes for 3 days. Sometimes for 10. Sometimes it gets sent back to you. So your supplier sees 49,850 instead of 50,000, and it lands a full week late. That's why we built WorldFirst differently. We skip the SWIFT chain. We route through CIPS and MYbank rails straight to Chinese accounts. Most of our payments land the same day. Search WorldFirst.

286 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)

  • 3 correspondent banks every time? My Maybank wire to China usually 2 days only.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Never heard. Got license in Malaysia?
  • CIPS rails what is that, sound very technical.
  • Same day to China? My bank also say same day for SWIFT one.
  • Name mismatch case ok fair, that one I see before.

In this video: why the bank's 2-day quote rarely matches reality, explained simply. That's not what actually happened. Let me walk you through it. They said 2 working days to Shenzhen. Sounds reasonable. What they didn't mention is that your money doesn't go straight there. First it goes through 3 middle banks. They're called correspondent banks. Each one holds the money for a full working day before passing it on. So day 2, day 3, and day 4 are already gone before the money even reaches a Chinese bank. Now it lands. But it's not over. It wasn't built for business owners paying suppliers in China. That's why we built WorldFirst. We use CIPS and MYbank rails. Money goes straight to Chinese accounts. Most wires land the same day. Full amount, no quiet fees. Search WorldFirst.

Then compliance review because the beneficiary name had Trading instead of Trading Co Ltd. Stop using SWIFT for China. WorldFirst uses CIPS and MYbank rails. No middlemen. In this video: how one late China wire creates four downstream problems, explained simply. Last week you sent 50,000 dollars to a supplier in Shenzhen. Four different things went wrong with that one wire. And here's the thing. You only noticed one of them, and you blamed the wrong people for the other three. Let me walk you through it. Problem one. The wire took 5 working days instead of the 2 days your bank promised. You called your bank and complained. Problem two. The amount that landed was 150 dollars short of what you sent. You assumed your supplier counted wrong, and you sent a frustrated message asking them to check. Problem three. The factory pushed your production start back by 5 days. You assumed it was a shipping or logistics issue. Problem four. A few weeks later, your customer asked why their delivery slipped. You blamed customs. Here's the truth. All four of those problems came from the same root cause. The wire went through 3 middle banks in the SWIFT chain. That's 3 hops, 3 days of delay, and 150 dollars in fees taken quietly along the way. Then on top of that, a Chinese bank paused it for a compliance check, because the supplier's name on the wire said Trading instead of Trading Co Ltd. One root cause, four downstream problems. We built WorldFirst to skip all of this. We use CIPS and MYbank rails, no middle banks, same day landing to Chinese accounts, full amount. If you're a business owner paying China, WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

I'll say it opinion-as-truth + compound math

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

I'll say it. SWIFT was built for banks talking to each other, not for business owners paying Chinese factories. Your wire goes through 3 correspondent banks. 3 business days. 150 dollars deducted invisibly. Now stack it. 24 wires a year. That's 72 days of delay across the year and 3,600 dollars eaten by middlemen. Across 5 years. 18,000 dollars. A full quarter of factory time lost in transit. And here's the kicker. Half of China-bound wires also get held at compliance because the beneficiary name has a missing Co Ltd or wrong character. Another week of delay. Another supplier waiting. WorldFirst uses CIPS and MYbank rails. No correspondent chain. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Period.

I'll say it. SWIFT was built for banks talking to each other, not for business owners paying Chinese factories. Your wire goes through 3 correspondent banks. 3 business days. 150 dollars deducted invisibly. Now stack it. 24 wires a year. deleted / added

In this video: what SWIFT really costs business owners over 5 years, explained simply. Your wire goes through 3 correspondent banks. 3 business days. 150 dollars deducted invisibly. Now stack it. 24 wires a year. That's 72 days of delay across the year and 3,600 dollars eaten by middlemen. Across 5 years. 18,000 dollars. A full quarter of factory time lost in transit. And here's the part most business owners miss. Half of China-bound wires also get held at compliance because the beneficiary name has a missing Co Ltd or wrong character. Another week of delay. In this video: what SWIFT really costs business owners over 5 years, explained simply. It was built for banks talking to other banks. Back in the 1970s. It was never built for business owners paying factories in China. Let's break down what happens when you use it. Your wire goes through 3 middle banks before it reaches your supplier. We call them correspondent banks. That's 3 working days of waiting, just for the money to pass between them. And those middle banks each take a small fee, usually adding up to about 150 dollars. The fees don't show on your statement. They just quietly come off the amount that lands. Now let's stack it up. Say you send 24 wires to China a year. That's 24 wires multiplied by 3 days each. 72 days of waiting, every year. And 24 wires multiplied by 150 dollars in fees. 3,600 dollars a year, gone. Now stretch that over 5 years of running your business. 18,000 dollars lost. And you lose a full quarter of factory time sitting in transit. And here's the part most business owners don't notice. Roughly half of China-bound wires also get paused at the compliance step, because the supplier's name on the wire has a tiny mismatch with their registered company name. Like a missing Co Ltd, or one wrong character. So that's another week of waiting added on. Another supplier sitting there, asking when the money will land. We built WorldFirst differently. We use CIPS and MYbank rails. No middle banks. Same day landings to Chinese accounts.

  • Half of China wires held at compliance? Where data come from?

  • Same day to China is the hook but no proof in the post.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the part most business owners never see when they wire money to China. SWIFT is not a bank. It's a messaging network. The actual money moves through correspondent banks in the middle, real banks, real people, real processing time. A US to China wire usually hops through 1 to 3 of them. Each hop is a business day. Each hop deducts 15 to 50 dollars from the amount in flight. Nobody tells you. The receipt shows what you sent. The supplier sees what landed. The gap goes to banks in the middle. Then Chinese bank compliance reviews the beneficiary name against the company registration. If anything is off by a single character, the wire pauses for days. So on a 50K wire your supplier gets 49,850, three days late. Stop. WorldFirst routes through CIPS and MYbank rails, no SWIFT correspondent chain. Direct to the Chinese bank account. Same-day on most wires. Full amount. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Each hop deducts 15 to 50 dollars from the amount in flight. Nobody tells you. The receipt shows what you sent. The supplier sees what landed. The gap goes to banks in the middle. Here's what every business owner needs to know about what SWIFT actually is. SWIFT is not a bank. It's a messaging network. The actual money moves through correspondent banks in the middle, real banks, real people, real processing time. A US to China wire usually hops through 1 to 3 of them. Each hop is a business day. Each hop deducts 15 to 50 dollars from the amount in flight. Nobody tells you. The receipt shows what you sent. The supplier sees what landed. The gap goes to banks in the middle. Then Chinese bank compliance reviews the beneficiary name against the company registration. If anything is off by a single character, the wire pauses for days. So on a 50K wire your supplier gets 49,850, three days late. WorldFirst routes through CIPS and MYbank rails, no SWIFT correspondent chain. Direct to the Chinese bank account. Same-day on most wires. Full amount. Search WorldFirst.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about what SWIFT actually is. SWIFT is not a bank. Let that sink in. SWIFT is a messaging system. It's a network that sends instructions, like a chat app for banks. The real money doesn't travel through SWIFT itself. The money moves through other banks in the middle. Real banks, with real people, doing real processing work. We call them correspondent banks. On a typical wire from the US to China, your money hops through 1, 2, or 3 of these middle banks. Each hop takes a full working day. So three hops means three days of waiting, just for the money to pass between banks. And here's the part most people miss. Each of those middle banks quietly takes 15 to 50 dollars off the top. Nobody warns you about this. Your bank receipt shows the amount you sent. Your supplier sees the smaller amount that arrived. The gap, those missing dollars, that went to the middle banks. You never get a line item. Now the money finally reaches a Chinese bank. But it's still not done. The Chinese bank checks the supplier's name on the wire against their official registered company name. Character by character. If a single letter is off, the wire pauses for days while compliance reviews it. So on a 50,000 dollar wire, your supplier ends up with 49,850 dollars, three days later than promised. We built WorldFirst to work differently. We don't use the SWIFT correspondent chain at all. We use CIPS and MYbank rails to send money straight to the Chinese bank account. Most wires land the same day. Full amount. Search WorldFirst.

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatLow

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about wires to China. The 2-day quote is a fiction. SWIFT isn't a network that moves money. It's just a messaging system that tells banks to move money. Real banks in the middle do the actual work. 1 to 3 of them on every US to China wire. Each one takes a day. Each one quietly takes 15 to 50 dollars off the top before passing it on. You'll never see those fees on a statement because they're not your bank's fees. They're the middlemen's. And the SHA setting your bank picks by default means your supplier eats every one of those deductions. WorldFirst doesn't play this game. CIPS rails. Same-day to Chinese accounts. The amount you send is the amount they receive. Period.

Here's the truth nobody's telling yousomething most business owners don't know about wires to China. The 2-day quote is a fictionoptimistic. SWIFT isn't a network that moves money. deleted / added

In this video: the invisible fees on every SWIFT wire to China, explained simply. The 2-day quote is optimistic. SWIFT isn't a network that moves money. It's a messaging system that tells banks to move money. Real banks in the middle do the actual work. 1 to 3 of them on every US to China wire. Each one takes a day. Each one quietly takes 15 to 50 dollars off the top before passing it on. You'll never see those fees on a statement because they're not your bank's fees. They're the middlemen's. In this video: the invisible fees on every SWIFT wire to China, explained simply. The 2 day quote your bank gave you? It's optimistic. Let me explain why. SWIFT isn't actually a network that moves money. Stop and think about that. SWIFT is a messaging system. It just sends instructions, like a chat app that tells banks to move money to each other. The real banks in the middle do all the actual work. Usually 1, 2, or 3 of these middle banks on every wire from the US to China. We call them correspondent banks. Each one holds the wire for a full working day before passing it on. So already, you're waiting days, not hours. And here's the part that quietly costs you. Each of those middle banks takes 15 to 50 dollars off the top before passing the money on. You'll never see those fees on your bank statement, because they aren't your bank's fees. They're the middle banks' fees. They just disappear. There's also a setting on every SWIFT wire called SHA. By default, your bank picks SHA. SHA means your supplier covers every one of those middle bank fees. They land short, and they assume you sent less. We built WorldFirst differently. We don't use the middle bank chain at all. We use CIPS rails. Same day to Chinese accounts. The amount you send is the amount they receive.

Stop using SWIFT to pay Chinese suppliers. I'm serious. SWIFT is a tool. It's the wrong tool for this job. You wouldn't fax a contract in 2026. So why are you running your supplier payments on a network designed in 1973? Here's what happens. You wire 50K. Three correspondent banks. Three business days lost. 150 dollars deducted invisibly. Then Chinese bank compliance pauses your wire because the beneficiary name was Trading instead of Trading Co Ltd. Day 7 your supplier sees the money. Day 8 production starts. Your customer already cancelled. WorldFirst is built for this job. CIPS and MYbank rails. Direct to the Chinese account. Same-day. Full amount. Use the right tool. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Stop using SWIFT to pay Chinese suppliers. I'm seriousSWIFT is not the right tool for paying Chinese suppliers. SWIFT is a tool. It's the wrong tool for this job. You wouldn't fax a contract in 2026. So why are you running your supplier payments on a network designed in 1973? Here's what happens. You wire 50K. Three correspondent banks. Three business days lost. 150 dollars deducted invisibly. Then Chinese bank compliance pauses your wire because the beneficiary name was Trading instead of Trading Co Ltd. Day 7 your supplier sees the money. Day 8 production starts. Your customer already cancelled. WorldFirst is built for this job. CIPS and MYbank rails. Direct to the Chinese account. Same-day. Full amount. Use the right tool. Search WorldFirst. Now.

deleted / added

Here's what every business owner needs to know about why SWIFT is the wrong tool for China. SWIFT is a tool. It's the wrong tool for this job. You wouldn't fax a contract in 2026. So why are you running your supplier payments on a network designed in 1973? Here's what happens. You wire 50K. Three correspondent banks. Three business days lost. 150 dollars deducted invisibly. Then Chinese bank compliance pauses your wire because the beneficiary name was Trading instead of Trading Co Ltd. Day 7 your supplier sees the money. Day 8 production starts. Your customer already cancelled. WorldFirst is built for this job. CIPS and MYbank rails. Direct to the Chinese account. Same-day. Full amount. Use the right tool. Search WorldFirst.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about why SWIFT is the wrong tool for China. It's a tool. And it's the wrong tool for paying suppliers in China. Think about it this way. You wouldn't send a contract by fax in 2026. The fax machine still technically works, but there are faster, cleaner ways to do it now. So why are you sending supplier payments through a network that was designed back in 1973? Let me show you what happens when you use SWIFT for China. You send a wire for 50,000 dollars. The money goes through 3 middle banks before it gets there. We call them correspondent banks. That's 3 working days of delay, right there. Each middle bank also takes a small fee, somewhere between 15 and 50 dollars. Together they take about 150 dollars off the top, and it doesn't show on your statement. Then the money finally reaches a Chinese bank. But the Chinese bank pauses it for a compliance check, because your wire said the supplier's name was Trading, when their official registered name is Trading Co Ltd. By day 7, your supplier finally sees the money. By day 8, the factory starts production. And by that point, your customer has already cancelled the order. We built WorldFirst specifically for this job. We use CIPS and MYbank rails, straight to the Chinese bank account. Same day. Full amount lands. Use the right tool for the job. Search WorldFirst.

233 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • SWIFT 1973 fair, but they update the system right? Not the same as fax.
  • Customer cancel from one payment delay? You move in tier 1 only is it.
  • Use the right tool... ok but who regulate WorldFirst?
  • Co Ltd thing real, happen to my friend once.
  • How long WorldFirst been around? New name to me.

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Watch this. You pay your Chinese supplier 20,000 dollars in USD because it feels safer. It just cost you 460 dollars extra and you didn't feel a thing. Here's why. Two conversions. Your bank does USD on the way out, 1.5% margin, 300 dollars. Supplier's bank does CNY on the way in, 0.8% margin, 160 dollars. Total leakage: 460 dollars. Now run that across the year. 12 orders. That's 5,500 dollars sitting inside someone else's spread. Run two years. 11,000. Run five. 27,500. That's a hire. That's a container. That's your kid's school fees. And it's not a fee line. It's the rate. Listen. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5% total. 60 to 100 dollars on the same 20K order. The other 360 stays in your business. Every order. Every month. Every year. Search WorldFirst. Period.

This is what 5 years of USD wires to China costs in lost margin. You pay your Chinese supplier 20,000 dollars in USD because it feels safer. It just cost you 460 dollars extra and you didn't feel a thing. Here's why. Two conversions. Your bank does USD on the way out, 1.5% margin, 300 dollars. Supplier's bank does CNY on the way in, 0.8% margin, 160 dollars. Total leakage: 460 dollars. Now run that across the year. 12 orders. That's 5,500 dollars sitting inside someone else's spread. Run two years. 11,000. Run five. 27,500. That's a hire. That's a container. That's your kid's school fees. And it's not a fee line. It's the rate. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5% total. 60 to 100 dollars on the same 20K order. The other 360 stays in your business. Every order. Every month. Every year. Search WorldFirst.

This is what 5 years of USD wires to China costs in lost margin. You just paid your Chinese supplier twenty thousand dollars for an order. You sent it in US dollars because that's what you always do. Feels normal. Feels safe. But that simple payment just cost you four hundred and sixty dollars extra. And you didn't notice a thing. Here's where it went. Two banks took a slice. Your bank changed your home money into US dollars. They took about one and a half percent on the exchange. That's three hundred dollars. Then your supplier's bank in China got the dollars and had to change them into Chinese yuan, because that's what they use day to day. They took another almost one percent. That's another hundred and sixty dollars. Two slices. Four hundred and sixty bucks. Gone. Now picture this happening every month. Twelve orders a year. That's five and a half thousand dollars in one year just sitting inside someone else's exchange rate. Stretch it out two years. That's eleven thousand. Five years. Twenty seven and a half thousand. Think about what that money could be. A new hire. A whole shipping container of stock. Your kid's school fees for a year. And here's the part that stings. It never shows up on a fee line. It's all baked into the rate itself. Invisible. At WorldFirst, we send Chinese yuan directly to your supplier. One exchange, not two. Half a percent or less. Sixty to a hundred dollars on that same twenty thousand dollar order. The other three sixty stays in your business. Every order. Every month. Every year. Search WorldFirst.

#2 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 135 words

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Real talk for every business owner who quotes in USD because it feels professional. The dollar feels safer. It costs you 2% more. Every single order. You wire 20K USD to your Shenzhen factory. Your bank takes 1% on the conversion out. Their bank takes 0.5% on the conversion in. That's 300 dollars gone before the goods touch a container. Then your supplier waits a week for SAFE compliance. They take the currency risk while it sits. They pad the next quote 2 to 5% to cover the wait. You're paying for safety that exists in your head, not in your books. I see this every week. Every single week. Stop. WorldFirst pays the supplier in CNY directly. Same bank account. Same supplier. One conversion at 0.3 to 0.5%. Half the cost. Same-day in 80% of cases. Search WorldFirst. Now.

deleted / added

More business owners need to hear this. Quoting in USD feels pro, costs 2%. The dollar feels safer. It costs you 2% more. Every single order. You wire 20K USD to your Shenzhen factory. Your bank takes 1% on the conversion out. Their bank takes 0.5% on the conversion in. That's 300 dollars gone before the goods touch a container. Then your supplier waits a week for SAFE compliance. They take the currency risk while it sits. They pad the next quote 2 to 5% to cover the wait. You're paying for safety that exists in your head, not in your books. This happens every week. Every single week. WorldFirst pays the supplier in CNY directly. Same bank account. Same supplier. One conversion at 0.3 to 0.5%. Half the cost. Same-day in 80% of cases. Search WorldFirst.

More business owners need to hear this. Quoting in USD feels pro, costs 2%. We hear you. The dollar feels safer. The dollar feels like the grown up currency. But it costs you about two percent more on every single order to a Chinese supplier. Let us walk you through why. Picture this. You wire twenty thousand dollars to your factory in Shenzhen. Your bank takes about one percent on the way out when they change your home currency into dollars. Then your supplier's bank takes another half a percent on the way in when they change those dollars into Chinese yuan, because that's what they actually use. That's three hundred dollars. Gone. Before the goods even touch a container. After that, your supplier has to wait about a week for the dollars to clear Chinese paperwork called SAFE compliance. While the money sits, they're carrying the currency risk. They don't know if yuan moves against the dollar in that week. To cover that risk and that wait, they quietly pad the next quote they send you. Two to five percent. So you're paying for a safety that exists in your head, not in your books. This happens every week. To real businesses. Every single week. At WorldFirst, we pay your supplier directly in Chinese yuan. Same bank account they already have. Same supplier. One exchange at half a percent or less. Half the cost. And in eighty percent of cases, the money lands the same day. Search WorldFirst.

#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 150 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers in USD because it feels safer, you're paying 2% extra for that feeling. Here's the math. Your USD wire to a Chinese factory goes through two exchange rate conversions. Your bank takes 0.5 to 1.5% turning your local currency into USD. Your supplier's bank takes another 0.3 to 0.8% turning USD back into CNY. That stack is invisible because it's baked into the rate, not the fee line. On a 20,000 dollar order, you've quietly given up 160 to 460 dollars before anything ships. Then your supplier waits 7 to 10 days for the USD to clear. So next quote, they pad your USD price by 2 to 5% to cover the wait. Stop paying twice for the same feeling. WorldFirst pays your supplier in CNY directly. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5% total. Same payment. Same destination. Half the cost. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Your USD wire to a Chinese factory goes through two exchange rate conversions. Your bank takes 0.5 to 1.5% turning your local currency into USD. Your supplier's bank takes another 0.3 to 0.8% turning USD back into CNY. That stack is invisible because it's baked into the rate, not the fee line. On a 20,000 dollar order, you've quietly given up 160 to 460 dollars before anything ships. Then your supplier waits 7 to 10 days for the USD to clear. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5% total. Same payment. Same destination. Half the cost. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

This is why paying suppliers in USD quietly costs a business owner 2% more. Here's the math. Your USD wire to a Chinese factory goes through two exchange rate conversions. Your bank takes 0.5 to 1.5% turning your local currency into USD. Your supplier's bank takes another 0.3 to 0.8% turning USD back into CNY. That stack is invisible because it's baked into the rate, not the fee line. On a 20,000 dollar order, you've quietly given up 160 to 460 dollars before anything ships. Then your supplier waits 7 to 10 days for the USD to clear. So next quote, they pad your USD price by 2 to 5% to cover the wait. Worth knowing before the next PI lands. WorldFirst pays your supplier in CNY directly. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5% total. Same payment. Same destination. Half the cost. Search WorldFirst.

This is why paying suppliers in USD quietly costs a business owner 2% more. You run a small business. You buy stuff from a factory in China. Every few months you send them money. Maybe twenty thousand dollars at a time. You send it in US dollars because that's what everyone does. It feels safe. It feels normal. But here's what's actually happening behind the scenes. Your bank doesn't just send the dollars. First, your bank turns your home currency into US dollars. They take a tiny cut of the exchange. About half a percent to one and a half percent. You never see this on your bill because it's hidden inside the exchange rate itself. Then the money lands in your supplier's bank in China. But here's the thing. Chinese banks don't really hold US dollars. So the Chinese bank flips it again into Chinese yuan. They take another cut. About a third of a percent to almost one percent. Two banks. Two cuts. On a twenty thousand dollar order, that's anywhere from one sixty to four sixty gone. Just gone. Before the goods even ship. And it gets worse. Your supplier waits seven to ten days for the dollars to clear customs and paperwork in China. That wait costs them cash flow. So next time they quote you, they quietly bump the price two to five percent to cover the wait. You're paying extra twice for the same order. Now at WorldFirst, we do this differently. We pay your supplier directly in Chinese yuan. One exchange. Half a percent or less. Same supplier. Same bank account. Same payment. Half the cost. Worth knowing before your next order. Search WorldFirst.

More business owners need to hear this. USD protects the bank, not you. Let us tell you the truth. It doesn't keep you safe. It keeps your bank rich. Here's how it actually works. Every time you wire dollars to a factory in China, two different banks take a slice. Your own bank goes first. They change your local money into US dollars and quietly skim half a percent to one and a half percent off the rate. You don't see this. It's not a fee on your statement. It's hidden inside the exchange rate itself. Now the dollars travel to China. They land at your supplier's bank. But Chinese banks don't really hold dollars day to day. Their accounts run on Chinese yuan. So the receiving bank changes the dollars into yuan and skims another third of a percent to almost one percent. Two slices. Two invisible cuts. On a twenty thousand dollar order, that adds up to as much as four hundred and sixty dollars. Gone before the goods even leave the warehouse. And it doesn't stop there. Your supplier has to wait about a week for the dollars to clear all the Chinese paperwork. While they wait, their own cash flow gets tight. So the next time they quote you, they quietly add two to five percent to the price to cover that wait. You just paid extra twice for the same order. Here's the honest reframe. The dollar feels safe because it's familiar. That's it. It's not cheaper. It's not faster. It's just the habit. At WorldFirst, we pay your supplier directly in Chinese yuan. One exchange. Half a percent or less. Same supplier. Same bank account. Lower cost. Search WorldFirst.

If you pay Chinese suppliers in USD, you need to hear this. You think you're paying one cost. You're paying four. One. Your bank's USD conversion margin, 0.5 to 1.5%, hidden in the rate. Two. Your supplier's bank CNY conversion margin, 0.3 to 0.8%, hidden in their rate. Three. Their 7 to 10 day wait for the USD to clear, which costs them cash flow. Four. The 2 to 5% padding they bake into the next USD quote to cover that wait. Add it up on a 20,000 dollar order. 460 dollars in spread. 700 dollars in next-quote padding. 1,160 dollars on one purchase order. And the USD price never even moved on paper. Real talk. The dollar feels safer because it's the currency you think in. It is not the cheapest currency to send to China. WorldFirst pays your supplier in CNY directly. One conversion at 0.3 to 0.5%. Same supplier. Same bank account. Half the cost. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Business owner paying Chinese suppliers in USD? You need to hear this. You think you're paying one cost. You're actually paying four. Let us count them out. Cost one. Your own bank changes your local currency into US dollars. They take a slice of the exchange rate. About half a percent to one and a half percent. You don't see this. It's hidden inside the rate. Cost two. The dollars arrive at your supplier's Chinese bank. Chinese accounts mostly hold Chinese yuan, so the bank changes the dollars again. They take another slice. About a third of a percent to almost one percent. Also hidden in the rate. Cost three. Your supplier has to wait seven to ten days for the dollars to clear all the Chinese paperwork. During that wait, their cash is stuck. They can't pay their own bills with it. Cost four. To make up for that wait, your supplier quietly adds two to five percent to the next quote they send you. That's eleven hundred and sixty dollars on a single purchase order. And the dollar number on your invoice never moved on paper. Here's the honest part. The dollar feels safer because it's the currency you think in. That's all. It is not the cheapest way to pay a country that runs on yuan. At WorldFirst, we pay your supplier directly in Chinese yuan. One exchange. Half a percent or less. Same supplier. Same bank account. Half the cost. Search WorldFirst.

The most expensive habit hiding in a business owner's supply chain. Paying your Chinese supplier in US dollars is the most expensive habit hiding in your supply chain. And almost nobody talks about it. Let us show you what's actually going on. Say you wire twenty thousand dollars to your factory. Two different banks each take a small cut of the exchange rate. Your bank takes about one percent on the way out. The supplier's bank takes about half a percent on the way in, because they have to change the dollars into Chinese yuan to actually use them. That's three hundred dollars gone on a single order. Doesn't sound like much. But run the math out. Say you place twelve orders a year, which is one a month. That's thirty six hundred dollars a year disappearing into bank exchange rates. Two years, seventy two hundred. Five years, eighteen thousand. Eighteen thousand dollars. Think about what that money could pay for. That's somebody's salary for a few months. That's a full shipping container of inventory. That's a new product you wanted to develop. And here's what your bank will never tell you. Both of those exchange slices are buried inside the rate. They never show up as a line item on your statement. You won't see them. Ever. The dollar price on your invoice feels locked in, but it really isn't. The real cost changes every time. At WorldFirst, we pay your supplier directly in Chinese yuan. One exchange. Half a percent or less. Same supplier. Same payment. The difference stays in your business. Search WorldFirst.

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the part most business owners miss about paying suppliers in USD. The USD price on the PI is not locked in. You see 20,000 USD and think that's the cost. Wrong. Two conversions happen. Your bank converts your local currency to USD at their card rate, not the rate on Google. That's 0.5 to 1.5% margin you never see. Then the wire lands in your supplier's Chinese bank. Chinese accounts hold CNY, not USD. So the receiving bank converts USD to CNY at their card rate. Another 0.3 to 0.8% margin. Stacked. Invisible. Baked into the rate, never the fee line. On a 20K order that's 160 to 460 dollars vanished into spread. Plus your supplier waits 7 to 10 days for clearance and SAFE compliance review. Stop. WorldFirst sends CNY direct over CIPS or MYbank rails. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5% all-in. 80% land same-day. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

deleted / added

Everyone wires USD to China. Nobody explains the two hidden conversions. The USD price on the PI is not locked in. You see 20,000 USD and think that's the cost. Wrong. Two conversions happen. Your bank converts your local currency to USD at their card rate, not the rate on Google. That's 0.5 to 1.5% margin you never see. Then the wire lands in your supplier's Chinese bank. Chinese accounts hold CNY, not USD. So the receiving bank converts USD to CNY at their card rate. Another 0.3 to 0.8% margin. Stacked. Invisible. Baked into the rate, never the fee line. On a 20K order that's 160 to 460 dollars vanished into spread. Plus your supplier waits 7 to 10 days for clearance and SAFE compliance review. WorldFirst sends CNY direct over CIPS or MYbank rails. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5% all-in. 80% land same-day. Search WorldFirst.

Everyone wires USD to China. Nobody explains the two hidden conversions. The price on the quote is not the real price. You see twenty thousand dollars on the invoice and you think that's what it costs. It isn't. Let us walk you through what actually happens. When you send the payment, your bank changes your home currency into US dollars first. But not at the rate you see on Google. They use their own rate, which is a bit worse. That gap is half a percent to one and a half percent of your money. You never see it. Then the dollars travel to China and land at your supplier's bank. Here's something most people don't know. Chinese bank accounts mostly hold Chinese yuan, not dollars. So the moment the dollars arrive, the bank has to change them again. They also use their own rate. Another third of a percent to almost one percent. Two changes. Two slices. Both buried inside the exchange rate, not on any fee line. On a twenty thousand dollar order, that's a hundred and sixty to four hundred and sixty dollars just gone. Plus there's a wait. Chinese rules require a compliance check on incoming dollar wires. Your supplier sits on the money for about a week before they can actually use it. At WorldFirst, we built a different pipe. We send Chinese yuan directly into your supplier's account using the local China rails. One exchange. Half a percent or less, total. Eighty percent of payments land the same day. Search WorldFirst.

261 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)

  • CIPS MYbank what? sounds technical, just tell me what to do
  • Got rate comparison vs Wise? Why WorldFirst?
  • How big is the company? Singapore got branch?
  • Bookmark, will check later

More business owners need to hear this. USD isn't safer when China runs CNY. You think USD is safer. It costs you 2% more. Every wire. Here's why. Two conversions stacked. Your bank, USD on the way out, 1% margin. Their bank, CNY on the way in, 0.5% margin. On a 20,000 dollar order that's 300 dollars gone. Quietly. Inside the rate. No fee line. Then your supplier waits a week for the USD to clear. Next quote, they pad it 2 to 5% to cover the wait. You just paid extra twice for the same order. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. More business owners need to hear this. USD isn't safer when China runs CNY. You think dollars feel safer. They actually cost you about two percent more on every single payment. Let us walk you through why. There are two exchange steps stacked on top of each other when dollars travel from you to a Chinese factory. First step, your own bank. They change your home currency into US dollars before they send the wire. They take about a one percent slice of the exchange rate. Second step, your supplier's bank in China. They get the dollars but they can't really keep dollars sitting around. So they change them into Chinese yuan, which is what factories actually use to pay workers and rent. They take another half a percent slice. On a twenty thousand dollar order, that's three hundred dollars. Gone. Quietly. None of it shows up as a fee on your statement. It's all inside the exchange rate. Then there's the wait. Your supplier sits there for about a week waiting for the dollars to clear all the Chinese paperwork. While they wait, their cash is stuck. So the next time they quote you, they bump the price two to five percent to cover that wait. You're paying extra twice for the same order. Here's the truth. At WorldFirst, we send Chinese yuan directly to your supplier. One exchange instead of two. Half a percent or less, total. Same supplier. Same bank account. Same payment. Half the cost. The dollar isn't safer when you're sending it to a country that doesn't use it day to day. Search WorldFirst.

269 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)

  • But china DOES accept usd, all my supplier quote in usd
  • WorldFirst safe ah? small company?
  • What about Wise? Same thing right?
  • Save first will read later

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about paying suppliers in USD. The dollar doesn't protect you. It just hides where the money goes. Your bank quotes USD. You think the price is locked. It isn't. They convert your local currency at their card rate, 1% margin. The USD lands at your supplier's Chinese bank. Chinese accounts hold CNY, not USD. So the receiving bank converts again, 0.5% margin. Two banks. Two spreads. Both invisible. On a 20K order, 300 dollars vanishes inside the rate. Then your supplier waits 7 to 10 days for the USD to clear, takes the currency risk, and pads the next quote to cover both. This is how it works on every USD wire to China. WorldFirst pays in CNY directly. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5%. The rate you see is the rate the supplier gets. That's the difference. Period.

Everyone wires USD to China. Nobody tells business owners where the money goes. The dollar doesn't really protect you. It just hides where your money actually goes. Let us show you. Your bank gives you a price in US dollars. You see that number and you think, okay, that's the price. Locked in. It is not locked in. The first thing they do is change your home currency into US dollars using their own internal rate. Not the rate you see online. Their rate is a tiny bit worse. About one percent worse. That gap, you never see. Now the dollars travel over to your supplier's bank in China. Here's something most people don't realise. Chinese bank accounts don't really hold US dollars day to day. They hold Chinese yuan. So the moment the dollars land, the bank changes them again. Another half a percent gap. Two banks. Two hidden gaps. Both completely invisible to you. On a twenty thousand dollar order, three hundred dollars just disappears inside the rate. Nobody points to it. Nobody charges you a fee for it. Then your supplier waits seven to ten days for the dollars to clear. During that week, they're carrying the currency risk. They don't know if the yuan rate will shift. So the next time they quote you, they pad the price to cover both the wait and the risk. This is how it works on every single dollar wire to China. At WorldFirst, we pay your supplier directly in Chinese yuan. One exchange. Half a percent or less. The rate you see really is the rate your supplier gets. That's the difference. Search WorldFirst.

Two conversions on one payment. 300 dollars gone on a 20K order. Plus a 7 to 10 day wait for clearance. Plus a padded quote next time. WorldFirst is built for this. Stop using USD for Chinese suppliers. Wrong tool for a country on CNY. Sending US dollars to China is the wrong default. We mean that. The US dollar is a tool. It's just the wrong tool when you're paying a country that runs on Chinese yuan. Think about it this way. If you bought from a factory in Vietnam, you wouldn't try to pay them in Thai baht. That would feel ridiculous. So why are you paying a factory in Shenzhen in US dollars? Here's exactly what happens when you do. Your own bank takes your home currency and changes it into US dollars. They take about one percent of the value as a slice of the exchange rate. Then the dollars travel to China. Your supplier's bank gets them and has to change them again into Chinese yuan, because that's the currency they actually use to pay their workers and rent. That bank takes about half a percent more. Two exchanges. Three hundred dollars gone on a twenty thousand dollar order. On top of that, your supplier waits seven to ten days for the dollars to clear Chinese paperwork. And on top of that, they quietly add a few percent to the next quote to cover that wait. At WorldFirst, we built this differently. We pay your supplier directly in Chinese yuan using the local China payment rails. One exchange. Half a percent or less. Same supplier. Same bank account they already use. Use the currency that the country actually uses. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst.

Here's where importers buying from China quietly lose margin every quarter. Your supplier is not your enemy. We want to be clear on that. But they're also not in the business of protecting your margin. That's not their job. So what happens? They quote you in US dollars because that's what you asked for. And then they often pad the price by 2 to 5% to cover what USD does to them on their side. Walk through what that means. The wire sits at their Chinese bank for 7 to 10 days. The bank charges a fee to change US dollars into Chinese yuan. The rate between the two currencies moves while the wire crosses oceans. None of that shows up on your invoice as a line item. But all of it is hiding inside the price you pay. This pattern shows up over and over. Importers wire USD. Supplier quietly collects the buffer. Importer keeps wondering why their margin shrinks every quarter, even when sourcing and orders look the same. Try this. Ask your supplier for the price in Chinese yuan on your next order. Then send the money via WorldFirst. We move Chinese yuan straight to your supplier's Chinese bank account. Same day. Near interbank rate. Watch the next quote come in lower. Search WorldFirst.

Watch this. Your last USD quote from your Chinese supplier was padded by 3%. On a 20K order, that's 600 dollars baked in. You never saw the line item. Now do that every order. Because USD hitting a Chinese bank means 7 to 10 days of waiting, a forex fee on conversion, and FX risk on their books. They priced that into your quote. Listen. Ask for the CNY price.
