Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers in USD, your supplier doesn't want your dollars. They just won't tell you. Here's why. When your USD wire lands in their bank, three things happen they hate. One. They wait 7 to 10 days for the bank to clear and convert it. Two. Their bank takes a forex fee on the conversion. Three. They eat the FX risk if the rate moves between your PI and settlement. So what do they do? They pad your USD quote by 2 to 5% to cover all of it. You pay the padding every single time and never see it. Stop. Ask for the CNY price on your next quote. Watch it come in 2 to 3% lower. WorldFirst lets you pay suppliers direct in CNY, same-day to their Chinese bank account. Supplier gets clean money. You get the lower price. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Three. They eat the FX risk if the rate moves between your PI and settlement. So what do they do? They pad your USD quote by 2 to 5% to cover all of it. You pay the padding every single time and never see it. Stop. Ask for the CNY price on your next quote. Watch it come in 2 to 3% lower. WorldFirst lets you pay suppliers direct in CNY, same-day to their Chinese bank account. Supplier gets clean money. You get the lower price. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

deleted / added

Here's what every importer needs to know about paying Chinese suppliers in USD. They just won't tell you. Here's why. When your USD wire lands in their bank, three things happen they don't love. One. They wait 7 to 10 days for the bank to clear and convert it. Two. Their bank takes a forex fee on the conversion. Three. They eat the FX risk if the rate moves between your PI and settlement. So what do they do? They pad your USD quote by 2 to 5% to cover all of it. You pay the padding every single time and never see it. Ask for the CNY price on your next quote. Here's what every importer needs to know about paying Chinese suppliers in USD. If you pay your Chinese supplier in US dollars, your supplier probably wishes you didn't. They likely won't say it to you, but it's true. Here's the story. You send a USD wire. It lands at their Chinese bank. The bank holds it for about 7 to 10 days. The bank then changes the dollars into Chinese yuan and takes a small fee for doing it. While your money is in the air, the rate between USD and CNY can move. If it moves the wrong way, your supplier loses money on the deal. So what does your supplier do? Simple. They add a little extra to your quote. About 2 to 5% extra. Just enough to cover the wait, the fee, and the risk. You never see this extra. It's just baked into the price you pay. Every order. So try this on your next order. Ask your supplier for the price in Chinese yuan. Not US dollars. Just see what number comes back. You'll often see a price that is 1 to 3% lower. That gap is the padding. We built WorldFirst for this exact moment. You send Chinese yuan straight to your supplier's Chinese bank account. Same day. No long wait. No mess. Your supplier gets clean money. You get a cleaner price. Search WorldFirst.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Pad 2 to 5%? My supplier already quote me thin lah. Got no room to drop.
  • Eh I tried ask CNY price last time, same lor. Not lower.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Never see in Maybank app or anywhere.
  • Got Malaysia license? Or this Hong Kong company?
  • OK the 7 to 10 day wait part actually true. My supplier complain before.
#4 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 162 words

Direct address + problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

If you pay Chinese suppliers in USD, you need to hear this. Real talk. Three things are happening on your supplier's side every time you send dollars, and you only see one of them. One. They wait. Your USD wire sits 7 to 10 days at their Chinese bank for compliance and conversion. Two. They pay. Their bank takes a 0.3 to 0.8% forex fee on the inbound conversion. Three. They gamble. If the rate moves between your PI and settlement, the loss is theirs. So they don't take that gamble. They pad the quote 2 to 5% upfront and pass it to you. Stop letting your supplier price your USD friction into your invoice. WorldFirst pays in CNY direct, same-day to Chinese accounts, no inbound conversion, no padding excuse. Send 20K CNY equivalent, supplier gets clean CNY today, your next quote drops 1 to 3%. If you're sourcing from China, you need WorldFirst. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Here's what happens on your supplier's side every time you wire USD. Three things are happening on your supplier's side every time you send dollars, and you only see one of them. One. They wait. Your USD wire sits 7 to 10 days at their Chinese bank for compliance and conversion. Two. They pay. Their bank takes a 0.3 to 0.8% forex fee on the inbound conversion. Three. They carry risk. If the rate moves between your PI and settlement, the loss is theirs. So they don't carry that risk. They pad the quote 2 to 5% upfront and pass it to you. There's no real reason your supplier should be pricing your USD friction into your invoice. WorldFirst pays in CNY direct, same-day to Chinese accounts, no inbound conversion, no padding excuse. Send 20K CNY equivalent, supplier gets clean CNY today, your next quote often drops 1 to 3%. If you're sourcing from China, WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

Here's what happens on your supplier's side every time you wire USD. Every time you send a USD wire, three things are happening on your supplier's side. You only see one of them. The first one is waiting. Your USD wire shows up at their Chinese bank and sits there for 7 to 10 days. The bank has to do a compliance check and change the money into Chinese yuan. The second one is paying. The bank takes a fee to do that change. About 0.3 to 0.8% of the wire. That money comes off your supplier's side. The third one is risk. The rate between USD and CNY moves all day, every day. If the rate shifts the wrong way while your wire is in flight, your supplier loses money on the deal. So they don't carry that risk for free. They add a buffer to your quote. About 2 to 5% extra. They pass the friction of your USD straight back to you. There's no good reason your supplier should be pricing your USD problem into your invoice. So we made WorldFirst. We send Chinese yuan straight to your supplier's Chinese account. Same day. No conversion on their side. Send the yuan version of 20K, your supplier gets clean money today, and your next quote often drops 1 to 3%. If you're sourcing from China, WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

  • Quote drop 1 to 3% only? Not worth the switch if account opening hassle.

I'll say it. The reason your Chinese supplier loves you when you pay USD has nothing to do with you. It's the padding. They quote you 20K USD on an order that should cost 19,400. That 600 dollars is their buffer against your wire taking 7 to 10 days to clear, their bank's forex fee, and the FX risk while the rate floats. Now run that across the year. Ten orders, that's 6,000 dollars in invisible padding. Twenty orders, 12,000. Money you handed over because of the currency on your invoice. Here's what nobody tells you. Pay in CNY direct and the buffer leaves. Supplier gets clean money, same-day, no conversion. WorldFirst routes CNY direct to Chinese accounts at near interbank rate. The padding stays in your business. Where it belongs. Period.

The reason your Chinese supplier doesn't push back when you pay in US dollars has less to do with you than you think. It's about the padding. Picture this. They quote you 20,000 dollars on an order that, in their head, should really cost around 19,400. That extra 600 dollars is their safety buffer. It covers a few things. Your USD wire sitting at the bank for 7 to 10 days. The bank's small fee to change USD into Chinese yuan. And the chance that the rate moves while the wire is crossing the world. All of that money walked out the door, just because of the currency printed on your invoice. Here's what most importers miss. When you pay in Chinese yuan directly, the buffer often eases off. Your supplier gets clean money. Same day. No conversion on their end. That's where we come in. We built WorldFirst to send Chinese yuan straight to Chinese supplier accounts, at near interbank rate. More of that hidden difference stays in your business. Where it belongs. Search WorldFirst.

Here's what every importer should know about the padding inside a USD supplier quote. Here's the story. They quote you 20,000 US dollars. But the price they actually wanted is closer to 19,400. So where does the other 600 dollars go? It's a buffer they add. Why do they add it? Because when your USD wire shows up at their Chinese bank, three things happen. It takes 7 to 10 days to clear. The bank charges a small fee to change it into Chinese yuan. And the rate between USD and CNY can move while the wire is on the way. If it moves the wrong way, that's on them. So they protect themselves. They build the 600 dollars into your quote. You pay it. They keep it as a buffer. That happens on every order. Every quote. Your supplier doesn't owe you a fixed USD price. They can quote you in their own currency. Just ask. You'll often see the quote drop 1 to 3%. We made WorldFirst to fix this. We send Chinese yuan straight to your supplier's Chinese bank account. Same day. Near interbank rate. Your supplier doesn't lose time. They don't pay a fee. They don't carry the rate risk. So the padding has no real reason to sit in your quote anymore. Search WorldFirst.

#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 156 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the mechanism most business owners miss when they pay Chinese suppliers. Your USD wire is not a clean payment. It's a problem the supplier has to solve. When 20,000 USD lands at their Chinese bank, the bank holds it for compliance review, sometimes 7 to 10 days. The bank converts USD to CNY at their own rate, taking a 0.3 to 0.8% forex fee on the way in. And if the USD CNY rate moved between PI and settlement, your supplier eats the loss. So they don't quote you their real price. They quote you their real price plus 2 to 5% padding to cover the friction your USD creates for them. Stop. Pay them in CNY direct. Same-day landing. No forex fee on their side. No FX risk for them. The padding comes off the next quote. WorldFirst routes CNY direct to Chinese supplier accounts at near-interbank rate. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

When 20,000 USD lands at their Chinese bank, the bank holds it for compliance review, sometimes 7 to 10 days. The bank converts USD to CNY at their own rate, taking a 0.3 to 0.8% forex fee on the way in. And if the USD CNY rate moved between PI and settlement, your supplier eats the loss. So they don't quote you their real price. They quote you their real price plus 2 to 5% padding to cover the friction your USD creates for them. Stop. Pay them in CNY direct. Same-day landing. In this video: how a USD wire actually works on the Chinese supplier's side. Your USD wire is not a clean payment. It's a problem the supplier has to solve. When 20,000 USD lands at their Chinese bank, the bank holds it for compliance review, sometimes 7 to 10 days. The bank converts USD to CNY at their own rate, taking a 0.3 to 0.8% forex fee on the way in. And if the USD CNY rate moved between PI and settlement, your supplier eats the loss. So they don't quote you their real price. They quote you their real price plus 2 to 5% padding to cover the friction your USD creates for them. Pay them in CNY direct. Same-day landing. In this video: how a USD wire actually works on the Chinese supplier's side. Your USD wire is not a clean payment for your supplier. It's actually a small problem they have to deal with. Walk through it with us. You send 20,000 US dollars to your supplier's Chinese bank. The first thing that happens? The bank doesn't just hand the money to your supplier. The bank holds the money for a compliance check. That can take 7 to 10 days. Next, the bank has to change the US dollars into Chinese yuan. The bank picks the rate and takes a small fee on the way in. About 0.3 to 0.8%. Then there's one more thing. The rate between USD and CNY moves all the time. While your wire is sitting at the bank, the rate could shift. If it shifts the wrong way, your supplier loses money on the deal. So your supplier protects themselves. They don't quote you their real price. They quote you their real price plus a buffer of about 2 to 5%. That covers all the friction your USD creates for them on their side. Now here's the simple fix. Pay them in Chinese yuan directly. Money lands same day. The bank doesn't take a fee to convert it. Your supplier doesn't carry the rate risk. So the buffer has no real reason to stay on your next quote. That's exactly what we built WorldFirst for. We send Chinese yuan straight to your supplier's Chinese account at a near interbank rate. Search WorldFirst.

Stop quoting in USD with your Chinese suppliers. I'm serious. USD is convenient for you and expensive for them, and they pass that bill straight back to you. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts, same-day, near interbank rate. The relationship gets cleaner. Period.

#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 132 words

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Watch this. 20,000 dollar order to your Chinese supplier paid in USD. Your bank takes 200 on the first conversion. Their bank takes 100 on the second. SWIFT chain takes 60. You just lost 360 dollars and you didn't see a single line item. Now do it again next month. And the month after. 12 orders a year, that's 4,320 dollars to the banks. Run that for 5 years. 21,600 dollars. That's a salary. That's inventory. That's a warehouse. And it's not a wire fee. It's two FX margins stacked on a route that nobody told you was optional. Pay in CNY direct. One conversion. 80 dollars total. Same-day. Listen. WorldFirst sends CNY direct via dedicated rails. 0.3 to 0.5% all-in. You keep the 280 every order. Every month. Every year. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Real talk for every business owner running orders into China. You think USD is the safe currency. It's not. You wire 20,000 in USD. Your bank converts your local to USD, 200 gone. SWIFT chain, 60 gone. Supplier's bank converts USD to CNY, 100 gone. Order lands 4 days late, 360 dollars short on the math. You call your bank. They blame the intermediary. The intermediary blames the receiving bank. Nobody owns it. Your supplier sees the short amount, pads the next quote 2 to 5% to cover the risk. Now you're paying twice. I see this every week. Every single week. Stop. WorldFirst. CNY direct, one conversion, 80 dollars, same day. The 280 per order stays with you. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Your bank converts your local to USD, 200 gone. SWIFT chain, 60 gone. Supplier's bank converts USD to CNY, 100 gone. Order lands 4 days late, 360 dollars short on the math. You call your bank. In this video: what really happens when a USD wire to China goes wrong, explained simply. You probably think US dollars is the safe currency. It feels universal. It feels stable. Here's why that's not actually true for your supplier payments. Say you wire 20,000 in US dollars. Your bank converts your local money into US dollars. 200 dollars gone. The international bank network in the middle takes 60. Your supplier's bank converts the US dollars into Chinese yuan when it lands. Another 100 gone. Your order lands 4 days late. 360 dollars short on the math. So you call your bank to ask what happened. They say it's the intermediary bank's fault. You call the intermediary. They blame the receiving bank in China. You call the receiving bank. They blame the original wire. Nobody owns it. Meanwhile your supplier sees the smaller amount that landed. They pad your next quote by 2 to 5 percent to cover the conversion risk. Now you're paying for the FX loss twice. Once on the wire. Once on the next quote. This happens every week. Every single week. The fix is simple. Send Chinese yuan from the start. At WorldFirst we send Chinese yuan directly. One conversion. About 80 dollars. Same day. The 280 dollars per order stays in your business. Search WorldFirst.

214 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • 'This happens every week' to who? You? You're not in my supply chain lah.
  • Supplier padding 2-5%, sure, but they pad for OTHER reasons too (RMB volatility, raw material). Not just USD.
  • Bank blame-game story is overdramatized. Most banks have decent support if you escalate.
  • WorldFirst same-day, 80 dollars, sounds too clean. Where's the small print?
  • If I'm doing 20K orders weekly I probably have a corporate FX desk already. This is more for newer importers.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about the math of paying suppliers in USD. Here's the math on a 20,000 dollar payment. Your bank converts your local currency to USD. Here's what every business owner needs to know about the math of paying suppliers in USD. Say you run a small business. You buy products from a factory in China. Every month you send them money. Let's walk through what happens to a 20,000 dollar order when you pay in US dollars. First, your own bank takes your local money and turns it into US dollars. They don't do this for free. They take about 1 percent. That's 200 dollars gone right there. Then the money travels through the international bank network. That network charges fees too. Another 60 dollars gone. Now the money lands at your supplier's bank in China. But your supplier needs Chinese yuan to spend, not US dollars. So their bank converts it again. Another half a percent. That's 100 dollars gone. Add it all up. 360 dollars vanished on one order. And the whole thing took 4 days. Now here's what's wild. If you send the money in Chinese yuan from the start, you skip one of those conversions. You skip a lot of those fees. One conversion. Around 80 dollars total. Lands the same day. The difference is 280 dollars per order. Run 12 orders a year and you just kept 3,360 dollars in your business. This isn't really a fee problem. It's a routing problem. You're using the wrong path. At WorldFirst we send Chinese yuan straight to Chinese supplier accounts, same day. Worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

239 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • 1% margin on USD conversion? My Maybank business rate is closer to 0.6%. Numbers feel cherrypicked.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Never heard la, got MAS license or not?
  • My supplier accept USD no problem, never charge me extra on next PI.
  • Same-day to China sounds nice but how my supplier verify the CNY actually came from me?
  • Show me the actual screenshot of the WorldFirst rate vs Maybank lah, not just say save 280.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about the 4 hidden costs on a USD wire to China. Your last 20,000 dollar order had four hidden costs. And you probably saw zero of them on your statement. Let me walk through all four. Cost number one. Your bank converted your local money into US dollars. They marked the rate up about 1 percent. That's 200 dollars gone. Cost number two. The international bank network in the middle took fees as the wire moved through it. That's 60 dollars gone. Cost number three. Your supplier's bank in China converted those US dollars into Chinese yuan. They marked that rate up about half a percent. That's 100 dollars gone. Cost number four. The whole wire took 4 days to clear. Your supplier was waiting for the full amount before they could start production. That delay cost you a slot in their factory schedule. Total invisible bill on one order. 360 dollars. Plus the time. Now here's the alternative. Pay in Chinese yuan straight from the start. One conversion. About 80 dollars. Same day clearing. At WorldFirst we send Chinese yuan directly to your supplier's account on a dedicated path. If you're a business owner running orders to China every month, this is worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

  • 4 days clearing is the worst case. My HSBC USD wire usually 2-3 days, sometimes next day.

  • I see the rate on my wire receipt though. 'Zero of them' is exaggerate.

Here's what every business owner should understand about where the wire fees actually go. You sent 20,000 dollars to your supplier. You paid in US dollars because that's how everyone does it. And the bank quietly pocketed about 280 dollars from you. You didn't see it happen. Here's exactly how. Step one. Your bank took your local money and turned it into US dollars. They marked the rate up by about 1 percent. That's 200 dollars taken before the wire even left your country. Step two. The US dollars traveled through the international bank network. Each bank in the middle took a small slice. About 60 dollars in those fees. Step three. The money landed at your supplier's bank in China. Their bank turned those US dollars into Chinese yuan. Another half a percent. Another 100 dollars gone. Total cost. 360 dollars on a 20,000 dollar order. No line item on the bill. No notification. Now here's the right way to do it. Send Chinese yuan directly from the start. One conversion only. About 80 dollars total. Lands the same day. Why does the old way exist? Because two banks both earn money on it. That's why nobody mentions there's an alternative. At WorldFirst we send Chinese yuan straight to your supplier's account on a direct path. No double dip. Search WorldFirst.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about the line item you've never seen on a statement. Let me explain what I mean. Say you send a 20,000 dollar order to your supplier in China and pay in US dollars. Two banks touch that money. Your bank on the way out. Your supplier's bank on the way in. Each one takes a small slice on the currency conversion. Your side marks the rate up about 1 percent. Their side marks it up about half a percent. That's 300 dollars right there. Add another 60 dollars in international bank fees and you're at 360 dollars on one order. Now let's run that out. Twelve orders a year. That's 4,320 dollars. Three years. Almost 13,000 dollars. Five years. 21,600 dollars. Think about what that money could do in your business. That's a person you could hire. That's three months of warehouse rent. That's a real cushion for slow months. And here's the part most people miss. It's not a fee. Nobody charged you. It's a routing choice you made without knowing it was a choice. Pay in Chinese yuan from the start instead. About 80 dollars total. One conversion. Same day. At WorldFirst we send Chinese yuan directly. The 280 dollars you save per order goes back into your business. Where it belongs.

221 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • 5-year 21,600 assumes I never change bank, never grow volume, never negotiate. Real life never that static.
  • Where the catch with WorldFirst? Subscription? Minimum balance? Inactive fee?
  • 'Three months warehouse rent' is emotional manipulation la. Just show the rate comparison straight.
  • WorldFirst regulated in MY? My accountant won't approve a tool without local license.
  • 0.3-0.5% on WorldFirst, what's the actual mid-market spread? Wise also claim near-mid.

In this video: how a USD wire to China actually works, two conversions in plain English. You think a wire is one thing. One payment. One cost. It's not. A single wire is actually two separate currency conversions stacked together. Let me show you what happens with a 20,000 dollar invoice. The first conversion happens at your own bank. They take your local money and turn it into US dollars. They mark the rate up by about 1 percent. That's 200 dollars taken quietly. No line item shows up. The second conversion happens at your supplier's bank in China. They take the US dollars and turn them into Chinese yuan. They mark that rate up by about half a percent. Another 100 dollars gone on their end. And there's a middle step too. The international bank network charges fees along the way. About 60 dollars. Add it all up. 360 dollars on a 20,000 dollar order. Plus 4 days of waiting for it to clear. Now here's the simpler path. Send the same 20,000 dollars but pay in Chinese yuan from the start. One conversion only. About 80 dollars total. Lands the same day. This is how banks earn quietly on a route they don't really explain to you. At WorldFirst we send Chinese yuan straight to your supplier's account. Search WorldFirst.

Two conversions happened and nobody told you. Your bank turned your local currency into USD at 1% margin. 200 dollars. Then their bank turned the USD into CNY at 0.5%. Another 100 dollars. Add 60 in SWIFT fees. 360 dollars vanished on a single 20K order. Now multiply by 12 orders a year. 4,320 dollars sitting in a bank's pocket. Pay in CNY direct and the math collapses. In this video: what two banks actually take from a 20K USD wire to China, explained simply. You wire 20,000 US dollars to your supplier. But the amount that actually hits their account is a little smaller. Two currency conversions happened in the middle. Nobody mentioned them. Here's how it breaks down. Your bank turned your local money into US dollars. They marked the rate up by 1 percent. That's 200 dollars taken. Then your supplier's bank in China turned those US dollars into Chinese yuan. They marked that rate up by half a percent. Another 100 dollars taken. And the international bank network in the middle took 60 dollars in fees as the money passed through. Add it all up. 360 dollars vanished on a single 20,000 dollar order. Now picture this happening every month. Twelve orders a year. That's 4,320 dollars sitting quietly in bank pockets. Now picture paying in Chinese yuan from the start. The math collapses. One conversion. About 80 dollars. Same day. At WorldFirst we send Chinese yuan directly to Chinese accounts on a dedicated path. Worth knowing if two banks have been splitting your money this whole time. Search WorldFirst.

In this video: why paying a Chinese supplier in CNY beats USD, explained simply. Let me explain why with a simple comparison. Imagine you live in Singapore and you rent a flat in London. You wouldn't try to pay your London landlord in Japanese yen. That'd be weird. They'd need to convert it to British pounds and you'd lose money on the conversion. Same logic applies here. Your supplier in Shenzhen lives and works in Chinese yuan. So why are you sending them US dollars? Here's what happens when you do. Your bank converts your local money into US dollars. They mark the rate up about 1 percent. That's 200 dollars on a 20,000 dollar order. Then the international bank network in the middle takes 60 dollars in fees. Then your supplier's bank in China converts those US dollars into Chinese yuan. Another half a percent. Another 100 dollars. Total leakage. 360 dollars. Plus 4 days of waiting. Now do it the right way. Send Chinese yuan from the start. One conversion. About 80 dollars total. Same day. At WorldFirst we built a direct path for this exact job. Dedicated lanes straight to Chinese accounts. Use the right tool for where the money is going. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst.

Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatHigh

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about USD payments to China. They route through two banks that both make money on the conversion. Your 20K order in USD. Bank one, your side, marks the local-to-USD rate 1% off the real one. 200 dollars. Silently. SWIFT chain shaves 60 in transit. Bank two, supplier's side, marks the USD-to-CNY rate 0.5% off. Another 100 dollars. The order arrives 4 days later, 360 dollars lighter. This is how it works on every USD wire to China. Every single time. WorldFirst doesn't play this game. CNY direct, one conversion, 0.3 to 0.5% all-in, same day. The 280 dollars you save per order is the difference. Period.

Bank one, your side, marks the local-to-USD rate 1% off the real one. 200 dollars. Quietly. SWIFT chain shaves 60 in transit. Bank two, supplier's side, marks the USD-to-CNY rate 0.5% off. Here's what every business owner needs to know about how two banks earn on a USD wire to China. Your payment passes through two banks. And both of those banks make money on the currency conversion. Let me walk you through one. You send a 20,000 dollar order in US dollars. The first bank is your own bank. They take your local money and turn it into US dollars. But they don't use the real exchange rate. They mark it up about 1 percent. That's 200 dollars taken quietly. Then the money travels through the international bank network. Each bank in the middle takes a little. About 60 dollars. Now the money arrives at the second bank. Your supplier's bank in China. They take those US dollars and turn them into Chinese yuan. They also don't use the real exchange rate. They mark it up about half a percent. Another 100 dollars taken. Four days later the order lands. 360 dollars lighter than what you sent. This is how it works on every single USD wire to China. Every time. At WorldFirst we built it differently. We send Chinese yuan directly. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5 percent all in. Same day. The 280 dollars you save per order is the difference.

#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 164 words

Cold open plus compounding

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatLow

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

S H A. Three letters that quietly cost business owners hundreds of dollars per wire. Here's what it actually is. Every wire to China rides on SWIFT, which is just a messaging system between banks. On every SWIFT message there's a field called 71A, and it picks who pays the intermediary banks in the middle of the chain. SHA means shared, but in practice it means your supplier pays. Each intermediary takes fifteen to fifty bucks. A typical US to China wire hits one to three of them. On one wire, that's maybe a hundred and fifty short. On twelve wires a year, you're looking at almost two grand your supplier had to absorb or chase you for. And every chase costs you trust. The good news is you can switch the code. Tell your bank OUR. Sender pays the whole chain. Supplier gets the full amount. Suddenly nobody's negotiating who lost what.

S H A. Three letters that quietly cost business owners hundreds of dollars per wire. Here's what it actually is. Every wire to China rides on SWIFT, which is just a messaging system between banks. On every SWIFT message there's a field called 71A, and it picks who pays the intermediary banks in the middle of the chain. SHA means shared, but in practice it means your supplier pays. Each intermediary takes fifteen to fifty bucks. A typical US to China wire hits one to three of them. On one wire, that's maybe a hundred and fifty short. On twelve wires a year, you're looking at almost two grand your supplier had to absorb or chase you for. And every chase costs you trust. The good news is you can switch the code. Tell your bank OUR. Sender pays the whole chain. Supplier gets the full amount. Suddenly nobody's negotiating who lost what. Or use WorldFirst and bypass the intermediary chain entirely. Search WorldFirst, send CNY direct, no 71A roulette.

In this video: the three letters on every China wire that decide who eats the fees. Three letters that quietly cost business owners hundreds of dollars per wire. Here's what it actually is. Every wire to China rides on SWIFT, which is just a messaging system between banks. On every SWIFT message there's a field called 71A, and it picks who pays the intermediary banks in the middle of the chain. SHA means shared, but in practice it means your supplier pays. Each intermediary takes fifteen to fifty bucks. A typical US to China wire hits one to three of them. On one wire, that's maybe a hundred and fifty short. On twelve wires a year, you're looking at almost two grand your supplier had to absorb or chase you for. And every chase costs you trust. The good news is you can switch the code. Tell your bank OUR. Sender pays the whole chain. Supplier gets the full amount. Or use WorldFirst and bypass the intermediary chain entirely. Search WorldFirst, send CNY direct, no 71A roulette.

In this video: the three letters on every China wire that decide who eats the fees. S. H. A. Three letters. They sit on every wire you send to China. And they quietly cost you hundreds of dollars every time. Here is what is really going on. When you wire money to China, the wire travels on a system called SWIFT. SWIFT is just how banks talk to each other across countries. Every SWIFT wire has a small field called 71A. That field picks who pays the middle banks. Yes, there are middle banks. The money does not go straight from your bank to your supplier's bank. It hops through one to three other banks on the way. Each one takes a small fee. SHA tells the chain that your supplier pays those middle fees. Each middle bank takes about fifteen to fifty dollars. So one wire might land short by a hundred and fifty. Do twelve wires a year and that is nearly two thousand dollars your supplier had to absorb or chase you about. Every chase is a tiny crack in trust. Here is the good news. You can change the code. Ask your bank to send the wire as OUR. OUR means you pay every middle bank. Your supplier gets the full amount. No chasing. Or skip the whole game. Use WorldFirst. We send Chinese yuan direct to your supplier on local rails inside China. No SWIFT. No 71A. No middle banks. Search WorldFirst and see for yourself.

#2 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 136 words

Real-talk plus escalating

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Real talk. If you've been paying suppliers in China for years and you don't know what SHA OUR BEN means, you've been getting clipped. That's not me being dramatic. That's the system. Every SWIFT wire has a field, 71A, and your bank picks SHA by default. SHA means the supplier pays every middleman fee in the chain. Each middleman, fifteen to fifty bucks. One to three middlemen per wire. So your supplier's seeing one fifty, two hundred bucks off every order. And they don't push back, because in China the relationship matters more than the hundred bucks. So they swallow it. And then they add three percent to the next quote. So actually, you're paying it. Just on the second invoice. Switch to OUR. Or stop using your bank for China wires altogether. Your call.

One to three middlemen per wire. So your supplier's seeing one fifty, two hundred bucks off every order. And they don't push back, because in China the relationship matters more than the hundred bucks. So they swallow it. And then they add three percent to the next quote. So actually, you're paying it. Just on the second invoice. Switch to OUR. Or stop using your bank for China wires altogether. Your call. Or use WorldFirst, CNY direct, no SWIFT chain, no 71A. Search WorldFirst.

Here is what every business owner needs to know about the three percent hidden in China quotes. If you have been paying suppliers in China for years and you do not know what SHA, OUR, and BEN mean, you have been quietly losing money the whole time. That is not us being dramatic. That is just how the system works. Here is the story. Every wire that crosses borders travels on a system called SWIFT. SWIFT is how banks talk to each other. Every SWIFT wire has a tiny box called 71A. Your bank picks SHA in that box by default. SHA means your supplier pays every middle bank that touches the money on the way. Each middle bank takes fifteen to fifty dollars. There are usually one to three middle banks per wire. So your supplier is quietly seeing one fifty, maybe two hundred dollars taken off every order. They do not push back. In their world, the relationship matters more than a hundred dollars. They swallow it. Then they add three percent to the next quote. So in the end, you pay it anyway. Just on the next invoice. The fix is one word. Ask your bank for OUR on the next wire. Or come to us. We are WorldFirst. Chinese yuan goes direct to your supplier. No SWIFT chain. No middle banks. Search WorldFirst.

#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 181 words

Classic PAS

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatLow

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

There's a three-letter code on every wire you send to China, and your bank picks it for you. It's called field 71A on the SWIFT message, and it has three options. SHA, OUR, or BEN. Almost every bank defaults you to SHA. SHA does not mean shared evenly. It means you pay your bank's send fee, and your supplier eats every single deduction from every intermediary bank in the chain. There are usually one to three intermediaries on a China wire, and each one takes fifteen to fifty dollars straight off the amount before passing it on. So on a fifty thousand dollar wire, your supplier can land short by a hundred and fifty bucks, and you never see why. The fix is one word. OUR. That means the sender pays the whole chain, and the supplier gets the full amount. Costs you maybe thirty more upfront. Saves you the awkward top-up message. Open your last wire confirmation and check what's in that field right now.

There's a three-letter code on every wire you send to China, and your bank picks it for you. It's called field 71A on the SWIFT message, and it has three options. SHA, OUR, or BEN. Almost every bank defaults you to SHA. SHA does not mean shared evenly. It means you pay your bank's send fee, and your supplier eats every single deduction from every intermediary bank in the chain. There are usually one to three intermediaries on a China wire, and each one takes fifteen to fifty dollars straight off the amount before passing it on. So on a fifty thousand dollar wire, your supplier can land short by a hundred and fifty bucks, and you never see why. The fix is one word. OUR. That means the sender pays the whole chain, and the supplier gets the full amount. Costs you maybe thirty more upfront. Saves you the awkward top-up message. Open your last wire confirmation and check what's in that field right now. Or skip SWIFT entirely and use WorldFirst, which routes CNY direct to your supplier's account with no intermediary chain to deduct from. Search WorldFirst and see the rail.

deleted / added

Here is what every business owner needs to know about the code on every China wire. It's called field 71A on the SWIFT message, and it has three options. SHA, OUR, or BEN. Almost every bank defaults you to SHA. SHA does not mean shared evenly. It means you pay your bank's send fee, and your supplier eats every single deduction from every intermediary bank in the chain. There are usually one to three intermediaries on a China wire, and each one takes fifteen to fifty dollars straight off the amount before passing it on. So on a fifty thousand dollar wire, your supplier can land short by a hundred and fifty bucks, and you never see why. The fix is one word. OUR. That means the sender pays the whole chain, and the supplier gets the full amount. Costs you maybe thirty more upfront. Saves you the awkward top-up message. Or skip SWIFT entirely and use WorldFirst, which routes CNY direct to your supplier's account with no intermediary chain to deduct from. Search WorldFirst and see the rail.

Here is what every business owner needs to know about the code on every China wire. You run a business. You send money to China to pay your supplier. The bank takes your money, sends it, and your supplier writes back a few days later. They say, hey, the money came short. You check. You sent fifty thousand dollars. They got forty nine thousand eight hundred. Where did the two hundred go? Here is what happened. Every wire to China rides on a system called SWIFT. SWIFT is just a way banks talk to each other. On every SWIFT wire, there is a tiny three letter code your bank picks for you. The options are SHA, OUR, or BEN. Almost every bank picks SHA on its own. They never ask you. SHA sounds like it means shared, like the cost gets split. It does not. SHA means you pay your bank to send. Your supplier pays every other bank that touches the money on the way to China. There are usually one to three middle banks. Each one takes a small bite, maybe fifteen to fifty dollars. By the time the money lands, your supplier is short. The fix is one word. Ask your bank to use OUR instead. OUR means you pay the whole chain. Your supplier gets the full amount. It costs you maybe thirty dollars more up front. No awkward top up message later. Or here is what we suggest. Skip SWIFT for China altogether. We are WorldFirst. We send Chinese yuan straight to your supplier's bank account using local rails inside China. No middle banks. No three letter code. No bite out of the amount. Search WorldFirst and see how the wire moves.

#4 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 149 words

Accusation

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Your bank set you up. Every wire you've ever sent to China was tagged SHA in field 71A of the SWIFT message, and nobody told you what that meant. SHA means your supplier pays the intermediary banks, not you. Sounds technical. Here's what it actually does. The wire leaves your account, hits one to three correspondent banks on the way to China, and each one quietly takes fifteen to fifty bucks from the amount before passing it forward. By the time it lands, your fifty thousand dollar wire might be forty-nine thousand eight hundred. Your supplier emails. You panic. You wire again. Now you've paid the bank's fee twice. The simple fix your bank didn't tell you. There's an OUR option. Sender pays everything. Supplier gets the whole amount. One word change, problem solved. Your bank doesn't sell it because OUR generates fewer second wires. Less revenue for them.

#5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 154 words

Direct address plus stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Look at your last China wire. Find the SWIFT confirmation. Scroll down to field 71A. Bet you it says SHA. SHA is the default at almost every bank in Southeast Asia, and it's costing you on every single transfer. Here's the stack. Problem one, SHA means your supplier pays intermediary deductions. Problem two, China wires usually pass through one to three intermediary banks. Problem three, each one shaves fifteen to fifty bucks off the amount before passing it on. Problem four, your supplier sees the shortfall and asks you to top up. Problem five, you wire again, paying your bank's send fee a second time. Stack it up on a fifty thousand dollar wire, you've quietly leaked around three hundred bucks per round. Twelve rounds a year, that's three and a half thousand gone. The fix is OUR. Three letters. Goodbye stack.

Problem two, China wires usually pass through one to three intermediary banks. Problem three, each one shaves fifteen to fifty bucks off the amount before passing it on. Problem four, your supplier sees the shortfall and asks you to top up. Problem five, you wire again, paying your bank's send fee a second time. Stack it up on a fifty thousand dollar wire, you've quietly leaked around three hundred bucks per round. Twelve rounds a year, that's three and a half thousand gone. The fix is OUR. Three letters. SHA is the default at almost every bank in Southeast Asia, and it's costing you on every single transfer. Here's the stack. Problem one, SHA means your supplier pays intermediary deductions. Problem two, China wires usually pass through one to three intermediary banks. Problem three, each one shaves fifteen to fifty bucks off the amount before passing it on. Problem four, your supplier sees the shortfall and asks you to top up. Problem five, you wire again, paying your bank's send fee a second time. Stack it up on a fifty thousand dollar wire, you've quietly leaked around three hundred bucks per round. Twelve rounds a year, that's three and a half thousand gone. The fix is OUR. Or use WorldFirst, which uses local CNY rails, no SWIFT, no 71A, no stack. Search WorldFirst.

Here is what every China-wiring business owner needs to know about the SHA default. Pull up your last wire to China. Find the SWIFT confirmation slip your bank sent you. Scroll down and look for a row called 71A. We bet you a coffee it says SHA. SHA is the default at almost every bank in Southeast Asia. And it costs you on every single wire. Let us stack the problem so you can see it whole. One. SHA tells the chain your supplier pays the middle banks, not you. Two. A wire to China usually passes through one to three middle banks before it lands. Three. Each one of those banks quietly shaves fifteen to fifty dollars off the amount. Four. Your supplier sees the wire came short and asks you for a top up. Five. You wire again, and now you have paid your bank's send fee twice. Stack all five together on a fifty thousand dollar wire and you have leaked around three hundred dollars in one round. Do that twelve times a year and you have lost three and a half thousand dollars without seeing it. The simple fix is the word OUR. Or skip it all with us. We are WorldFirst. We send Chinese yuan to your supplier through local rails inside China. No SWIFT. No 71A. No stack. Search WorldFirst.

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)

I'll say it. Your bank is hiding things from you. Not in a dramatic way, just in a quiet, slow, expensive way. Every wire you send to China has a tiny field called 71A. Three options. SHA, OUR, BEN. Your bank checks SHA by default, every time, and SHA means your supplier eats the fees from every middleman bank between you and them. There are usually one to three middlemen. Each one takes fifteen to fifty bucks. Your supplier loses. You don't know. They ask for the missing amount, you wire again, pay another fee, and you think wire fees are just like that. They're not. They're a choice. The bank made the choice for you. Take the choice back.

#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 140 words

Mechanism-first

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the mechanism nobody explains. When you send a wire to China, your bank generates a SWIFT message called MT103. Inside that message, there's a field labeled 71A. It accepts three values. SHA, OUR, or BEN. SHA tells the chain you'll pay your sending bank's fee, and the supplier covers every other deduction along the route. OUR tells the chain you pay everything top to bottom. BEN tells the chain the supplier pays the entire bill, including your sending fee. Your bank's system defaults to SHA. That's not a coincidence. SHA generates more frequent top-up requests, which generate more wires, which generate more fees. The mechanism is hidden. The choice is yours. Walk to your bank's wire desk, ask for 71A to be OUR on the next China wire, and watch the shortfall problem disappear. One field. Three letters. Hundreds saved per wire.

BEN tells the chain the supplier pays the entire bill, including your sending fee. Your bank's system defaults to SHA. That's not a coincidence. SHA generates more frequent top-up requests, which generate more wires, which generate more fees. The mechanism is hidden. The choice is yours. In this video: the tiny field on the wire form that decides who pays the bank chain. Let us walk through it. You tell your bank, please send fifty thousand dollars to my supplier in China. Behind the scenes, your bank writes a tiny message. The message has a name. MT103. Think of it like a small instruction slip the banks pass to each other. Inside that slip, there is a box called 71A. That box has three possible answers. SHA, OUR, or BEN. SHA means you pay your bank's send fee, your supplier covers every other deduction on the way. OUR means you pay everything, top to bottom. BEN means your supplier pays everything, even your bank's send fee. Your bank fills the box with SHA on its own. They never ask you. The box stays hidden. But the choice was always there. You can call your bank, or message them online, and say, please mark 71A as OUR on my next China wire. They will. Or you can skip the whole MT103 game with us. We are WorldFirst. We send Chinese yuan straight to your supplier using local rails inside China. No SWIFT. No MT103. No middle banks. Search WorldFirst and look at how the money actually moves.

Stop. Look. If you're a business owner sending wires to China and you don't know what SHA, OUR, and BEN mean, your bank is winning and you don't even know there was a game. These are three letters on a SWIFT form. Just three letters. And your bank picks one for you, every single time. They pick SHA. SHA means your supplier eats the fee from every intermediary bank in the chain. Could be one bank, could be three. Each one takes fifteen to fifty bucks off the wire before passing it on. Real money. Real loss. And nobody tells you. So next time, ask your bank to send the wire as OUR. You pay the whole chain. Supplier gets the full amount. Done. Easy. Why is nobody talking about this?

Stop using X

Soc4.0 Aud4.2 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

Stop using SHA on your wires. I'm serious. Look at your last wire to China. Pull up the SWIFT confirmation. There's a field called 71A. If it says SHA, you just told the bank to charge your supplier for every middleman bank between you and them. That's not shared. That's outsourced. Each middleman shaves fifteen to fifty bucks off the wire. On a fifty thousand dollar order, your supplier sees forty-nine eight, maybe forty-nine seven. They ping you. Awkward. The fix is the word OUR. You pay the whole chain. Supplier gets the full number. It costs you maybe thirty bucks more, saves the relationship, saves the chase. Stop using SHA. Change it today.

#10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 117 words

Conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Want to know what your bank doesn't want you to ask? Try this. Walk in next week and say, I want to send my next China wire as OUR not SHA. Watch the teller's face. Half of them won't know what you're talking about. The other half will tell you it costs more, but won't say how much. Here's the truth. SHA is the default because it generates short wires. Your supplier complains. You wire again. Bank collects another fee. OUR breaks the loop. You pay the chain upfront, maybe thirty more bucks, and your supplier gets the full amount. One wire. No follow-up. So why is OUR not the default? Think about who profits when wires go short.

Cold open + compounding stakes

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Watch this. Your bank app shows sent. Your supplier's WhatsApp shows nothing. You haven't lost the money. You've lost the day. Then the week. Then the order. Because your supplier won't load raw materials until the CNY hits their account. So every hour the wire sits at an intermediary bank, your delivery date slips. Three intermediaries, two days of conversion windows, one AML hold above a threshold nobody told you about, plus a Friday public holiday in Shenzhen. That's a five-day silence. On a Q4 order, five days is your container. On a launch SKU, five days is your competitor beating you to the shelf. Listen. The wire isn't lost. The visibility is. WorldFirst removes the correspondent chain entirely. CNY straight to Chinese bank accounts, same-day, with a tracking reference your supplier can match. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Is it possible to lose a container in five days without losing the money? Your bank app shows the word sent. Your supplier's WhatsApp shows nothing at all. You have not lost the money. But you have lost the day. Then the week. Then the whole order. Why? Because your supplier will not start cutting fabric or pouring plastic until the yuan lands in her account. So every single hour the wire sits at some other bank in between, your shipping date slides further. Picture it stacked up. Three other banks in the middle. Two whole days for the money to swap currency. One pause because the amount is over a limit no one told you about. Then a Friday holiday in Shenzhen. That gives you five full days of silence. If this is a Q4 order, five days is your whole container. If this is a new product you wanted on the shelf first, five days is your rival winning the race. The wire is not lost. The view of it is lost. We built WorldFirst to fix this. We send yuan straight into Chinese bank accounts, on the same day. We give you one tracking number your supplier can check against her statement. No middle banks. Just a clear line from us to her. Search WorldFirst.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China. You wire $30K. Bank app says sent. Supplier says nothing. You call the bank. They blame the correspondent. The correspondent blames the receiving bank. The receiving bank says it's in conversion. Conversion says there's a holiday. Holiday says wait Monday. Monday says AML flagged it. AML says give us a reason. Meanwhile your supplier is asking every two hours. Your delivery slot is gone. Your customer is asking. And the bank app still says sent. I see this every single week. Stop. WorldFirst. CNY direct to Chinese accounts, same-day, one tracking reference. Supplier sees it land. You sleep. Search WorldFirst. Now.

#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 140 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Listen. Your bank says the wire is sent. Your supplier says nothing arrived. Both are telling the truth. Here is how that's possible. When your bank says sent, they mean the wire left their system. They don't mean it landed. Between you and your supplier, there are correspondent banks. Sometimes three. Sometimes five. Each one holds the wire while they queue it, check it, and convert it. A conversion window alone is up to two working days. Add a CN bank holiday or an AML threshold hold and you're at four. So now your supplier is asking where the money is and you're staring at a bank app that says sent. You look like the problem. Stop. Track the wire by SWIFT reference, not by the word sent. WorldFirst pays directly to Chinese accounts in CNY same-day. No correspondent chain. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Listen. Your bank says the wire is sent. Your supplier says nothing arrived. Both are telling the truth. Here is how that's possible. When your bank says sent, they mean the wire left their system. They don't mean it landed. Between you and your supplier, there are correspondent banks. Sometimes three. Sometimes five. Each one holds the wire while they queue it, check it, and convert it. A conversion window alone is up to two working days. Add a CN bank holiday or an AML threshold hold and you're at four. So now your supplier is asking where the money is and you're staring at a bank app that says sent. You look like the problem. Stop. Track the wire by SWIFT reference, not by the word sent. WorldFirst pays directly to Chinese accounts in CNY same-day. No correspondent chain. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

deleted / added

Stop using the word sent as proof a supplier got paid. Your supplier says nothing arrived. Both are telling the truth. Here is how that's possible. When your bank says sent, they mean the wire left their system. They don't mean it landed. Between you and your supplier, there are correspondent banks. Sometimes three. Sometimes five. Each one holds the wire while they queue it, check it, and convert it. A conversion window alone is up to two working days. Add a CN bank holiday or an AML threshold hold and you're at four. So now your supplier is asking where the money is and you're staring at a bank app that says sent. You look like the problem. Track the wire by SWIFT reference, not by the word sent. WorldFirst pays directly to Chinese accounts in CNY same-day. No correspondent chain. Search WorldFirst.

Stop using the word sent as proof a supplier got paid. You sit at your desk on a Tuesday morning. You wire money to your supplier in China. Your bank app pings. It says one word. Sent. You feel good. You message your supplier. You tell her the money is on the way. She writes back an hour later. She says nothing has come in. You open the app again. It still says sent. So who is right? Here is the thing. Both of you are right. When your bank says sent, all they mean is the wire left their building. They do not mean it landed in your supplier's account. Between you and her, there is a long chain of other banks. Sometimes three. Sometimes five. Each one holds the wire for a while. They check it. They line it up. They swap the money from your dollars into Chinese yuan. That swap alone can take up to two working days. Now add a bank holiday in China. Add a money check that pauses the wire because the amount is over some limit. Suddenly you are on day four. Your supplier is asking. Your app still says sent. You look like the one who messed up. We have a fix. We are WorldFirst. We pay straight into Chinese bank accounts in yuan, on the same day. No long chain of other banks. Just one step. Search WorldFirst.

229 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Three to five correspondent banks? My MBB wire usually one hop only lah.
  • SWIFT reference can track? Most bank app don't even show it nicely.
  • WorldFirst who? Never hear before. Got Bank Negara license?
  • Same day to Chinese account? Got cut off time or not?
  • Sound like ad. Show real screenshot of WorldFirst paying same day.
#4 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 134 words

Direct address + problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

If you wire money to China, you need to hear this. Real talk. Last time your supplier said nothing arrived, four things happened and your bank only told you about one. One. The wire left your bank. They called this sent. Two. It hit a correspondent bank in a different timezone and queued for the next clearing cut-off. Three. The currency conversion sat in a window of up to two working days. Four. An AML check above an undisclosed threshold paused it for review. Your supplier sees zero. Your bank says sent. You look like the problem. You lose a week. You lose the order. Stop wiring through chains you can't track. WorldFirst pays direct to Chinese accounts in CNY same-day. One reference. One status. One person to ask. If you're a business owner paying China, you need WorldFirst. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

When a wire goes silent, four things happened. The bank told you one. The last time your supplier said nothing has come in, four different things happened to your wire. Your bank only told you about one of them. Let me walk you through the whole thing. Number one. The wire left your bank. They called this sent and they closed their side of the job. Number two. It landed at another bank in a different time zone and got in line for the next cut-off window. So it sat there waiting its turn. Number three. The money then had to swap from your currency into yuan. That swap on its own can sit for up to two working days. Number four. The amount tripped a money check at a hidden limit no one told you about, and it got paused for a review. So your supplier sees nothing in her account. Your bank app says sent. You look like the one who messed up. You lose a week. Maybe you lose the whole order. We made WorldFirst because this is fixable. We pay yuan straight into Chinese bank accounts on the same day. One tracking number. One status that means what it says. One team you can actually ask. If you are paying suppliers in China, WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

  • AML threshold undisclosed, ok that's actually a fair point. Mine got held last year.
  • Lose a week, lose the order, bit stacked. Most supplier wait few days.
  • One person to ask, who? Got Malaysia support? English speaker?
  • Worth a look ok but who is WorldFirst leh. Ant Group? Trust them or not?
#5 PG2 Gary Vee Opinion-led 109 words

I'll say it / opinion-as-truth

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

I'll say it. The word sent is the most expensive word your bank uses and nobody warns you about it. You hit send. App says sent. Done, right? Wrong. Sent means the wire left their system. It does not mean it arrived. Between sent and arrived there are correspondent banks, conversion windows of up to two working days, AML holds nobody told you about, and the occasional CN bank holiday. So your supplier is staring at an empty account and you're staring at the word sent and a week disappears. WorldFirst is direct CNY to Chinese bank accounts, same-day, no chain. One status. The word arrived means arrived. That's the difference.

Took business owners years to learn one word costs the most. Here it is. You hit send. App says sent. Done, right? Not quite. Sent means the wire left their system. It does not mean it arrived. Took business owners years to learn one word costs the most. Here it is. Here is how it usually goes. You tap send on the wire. The app pings back with the word sent. You think great, that's done. Not quite. Sent only tells you that the money left the bank's own system. It does not tell you the money arrived anywhere. Between the moment your bank says sent and the moment your supplier sees the money arrive, a lot of stuff happens. Your wire passes through other banks. The cash has to swap from your currency into yuan, which can sit for up to two working days. A money check might pause it because of a limit no one told you about. There might even be a Chinese bank holiday. So your supplier is staring at an empty account. You are staring at the word sent. And a whole week is gone. We built WorldFirst to close that gap. Yuan straight to Chinese bank accounts, same day, no middle banks. One status. When we say arrived, we mean arrived. That is the difference.

189 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)

  • Conversion two working days? Mostly one day for me. Where you get two?
  • WorldFirst arrived means arrived, ok confident statement. Show me proof.
  • Cut off time how? What if I send Friday 5pm Malaysia time?
  • Who is WorldFirst. First time hearing leh.

#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 136 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here is what most business owners don't understand about international wires. Sent is not a status. Sent is a sentence your bank uses to close the ticket. The actual wire is now somewhere inside a chain of correspondent banks. Two. Three. Sometimes five. Each one holds it while they check sanctions, queue it for cut-off, and convert the currency. Currency conversion alone can sit two working days. AML flags above unannounced thresholds add another day. A Chinese bank holiday adds another. So when your supplier says nothing has arrived, they're right. And when your bank says sent, they're also right. The problem isn't a lie. The problem is the word means two different things. Stop. WorldFirst pays Chinese accounts directly in CNY same-day. No correspondent banks between you and your supplier. One status. One number. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Here's exactly what sent means on a bank app. Spoiler. Not arrived. The word sent is not really a status. Sent is just a sentence your bank uses to close out the job on their end. The actual wire is now somewhere inside a long chain of other banks. Maybe two of them. Maybe three. Sometimes as many as five. Each one is holding your money for a while. They check it against a list of rules. They line it up for their cut-off time. They swap your dollars into yuan. The currency swap on its own can sit for up to two working days. Then add a pause if the amount sets off an alert at some hidden level no one told you about. That is another day on the clock. Then add a Chinese bank holiday you did not know was coming. That is one more day. So when your supplier tells you nothing has come in, she is telling you the truth. And when your bank tells you the wire is sent, your bank is also telling you the truth. Nobody is lying to you. The problem is the word sent means two completely different things to two different people. We built WorldFirst to fix this. We pay yuan straight into Chinese accounts on the same day, with no other banks in between you and your supplier. One status. One number. Search WorldFirst.

#9 PG3 Gary Vee Opinion-led 112 words

Soc4.0 Aud4.2 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Stop using your bank app as a tracking tool. I'm serious. The bank app gives you one word. Sent. That word ends their job and starts yours. Now you have to chase three correspondent banks, a conversion desk, an AML team in a different country, and a public holiday calendar in Shenzhen. Meanwhile your supplier won't start production because nothing has landed. The bank app was built to tell their teller the wire left. It was not built to tell you when your supplier can start. Wrong tool. WorldFirst was built for the second job. Direct to Chinese accounts in CNY same-day. One reference. One status that means arrived. Use the right tool. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about international wires. Your bank's status doesn't mean what you think it means. Sent doesn't mean arrived. Sent means it left their system. After that, the wire enters a black box of correspondent banks, conversion windows, AML thresholds, and bank holidays in cities you've never been to. Your bank closes the ticket. You're left holding the line with your supplier. And nobody owns the gap. WorldFirst doesn't have a gap. CNY direct to Chinese accounts, same-day, one tracking reference your supplier can match against their account. Sent and arrived become the same word. Period.

Real talk for every business owner running sourcing out of China. The 2% rate gap is the thing you're not auditing. You'd renegotiate a supplier over a 2% price hike. You'd switch a 3PL over a 2% margin shift. But the 2% your bank takes on every wire? You don't even see it. Twelve wires a year. $50K each. $12,000 quietly absorbed. That's a hire. That's an extra container. That's your fourth-quarter bonus. And it's recurring. Every year. Stop. Audit the rate. Compare your bank to a specialist FX rail at 0.3 to 0.5%. WorldFirst runs that rail. Here's what every business owner needs to know about how a small FX gap adds up. You run a small business. You buy stuff from suppliers in China. Every month or so, you send them money. Let's say you send fifty thousand dollars at a time. Twelve times in a year. So in one year, you've sent six hundred thousand dollars to your suppliers. Now here's the thing most business owners miss. When your regular bank changes your dollars into Chinese money, they don't give you the real exchange rate. They give you a rate that's about 2% worse. That sounds tiny, right? But on one wire of fifty thousand dollars, 2% is one thousand dollars. And you do that twelve times. So at the end of the year, the bank has quietly kept twelve thousand dollars of your money. You never saw it as a fee. There was no receipt. It was just baked into the rate. Now compare that to a specialist money-moving service. They charge way less, around zero point three to zero point five percent. We at WorldFirst run on that kind of rail. Same-day delivery to accounts in China. So if you're sending money to China every month, it's worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

The gap is 1.5 to 1.7 percentage points per wire. Multiply by twelve. That's the difference between a hire and not. A container and not. A bonus and not. WorldFirst runs on the specialist rail. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Search WorldFirst.

In this video: why the rate, not the fee, is the real cost on every supplier wire. The wire fee is not the cost. The exchange rate is. Let me show you. Your bank does not give you the actual dollar-to-Chinese-yuan rate. They give you a rate that's about 2% worse than what the real market rate is. On a fifty thousand dollar wire, that's one thousand dollars. Silently. There is no line on your statement that says this. Now do it twelve times in a year. That's twelve thousand dollars. On six hundred thousand dollars of supplier payments, you've quietly lost 2% of your whole sourcing budget every single year. That's the gap. Specialist services for moving money work differently. They use a rate that's much closer to the real market rate. Around zero point three to zero point five percent instead of 2%. So the gap between a bank and a specialist is one point five to one point seven points per wire. Now multiply that by twelve wires. That's the difference between hiring someone or not. Funding another container or not. Paying yourself a bonus or not. We at WorldFirst run on the specialist rail. Same-day to accounts in China. Search WorldFirst.

In this video: where 50,000 dollars of sourcing money goes over five years, explained. This year you sent 500,000 dollars to suppliers in China. In your books, your sourcing cost was 500,000 dollars. But it wasn't. The real number was 510,000 dollars. Your bank took the extra 10,000 dollars and never said a word. Here's how. That's the gap. That's the leak. We're WorldFirst. We move money to China at near the real bank-to-bank rate. Same 500K. Same suppliers. You keep the 10,000 dollars. Every year. Every wire. Search WorldFirst.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about the 10K leaving silently each year. You own a small business and you buy stuff from China to sell. This year you sent 500,000 dollars to your suppliers over there. You did everything right. You paid on time. You got your goods. But here's something strange. Your bank kept 10,000 dollars of your money and never told you. We'll show you how. Every time you send money to China, your bank changes your dollars into Chinese yuan. They give you a rate. Other money services do this for way less. Around 0.3 to 0.5 percent. That's the gap. That's the money leaving your business every year. We're WorldFirst. We move money to China at near the real bank-to-bank rate. Same supplier. Same wire. You just stop losing 10,000 dollars a year. Worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

If you wire money to China for sourcing, you need to hear this. Real talk. Three things happened on every wire you sent this year and you only saw one of them. One. Your bank charged you a wire fee. Maybe 25 dollars, maybe 40. You saw that. Two. Your bank marked up the exchange rate 2% and pocketed your money silently. You didn't see that. Three. Across 500K of annual sourcing, that 2% became 10,000 dollars sitting in your bank's pocket instead of yours. You didn't see that either. Specialist FX rails run at 0.3 to 0.5%. The gap is real and it compounds every year. Stop wiring through banks for China supplier payments. WorldFirst gives you near-interbank rates on every CNY wire. Same suppliers. Same money. You keep the 10K. If you're a business owner sourcing from China, you need WorldFirst. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Over a whole year of sourcing, say 500,000 dollars in supplier payments, that 2 percent quietly grows into 10,000 dollars sitting in the bank's pocket instead of yours. You don't see that either. Now compare. Specialist money services do this for around 0.3 to 0.5 percent. The gap is real. The gap shows up year after year. We're WorldFirst. We move money to China at near the real bank-to-bank rate on every wire. Same suppliers. Same money out. You keep the 10,000 dollars. If you source from China, WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about the gap between wire fees and rates. This year, your bank took 10,000 dollars from your business. And you agreed to it. Every single time. Let us walk you through it. You sent 500,000 dollars to your suppliers in China over the past twelve months. Could be twelve wires. Could be twenty wires. Doesn't matter how many. On every single one, the bank added about 2 percent to the exchange rate. They didn't show it as a fee. They didn't put it on your statement. They just baked it into the rate. So when you opened your books, you saw 500,000 dollars going out. The real number going out of your account was 510,000 dollars. The bank simply decided not to tell you. Now, here's the part that might sting. Other money services do this exact same thing for around 0.3 to 0.5 percent. So you weren't really paying for the service. You were paying because nobody sat down with a calculator. Banks have done this for years and years. Most owners never check the rate. They only check the fee. We're WorldFirst. We move money to China at near the real bank-to-bank rate. Same 500K. Same suppliers. You keep the 10,000 dollars. Search WorldFirst.

206 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Pocketed? Bank disclose in T&C. Not theft if I agree at sign-up.
  • You call me stupid for not reading the rate? Ok thanks for the lecture.
  • 510K real number, where you get that exact? Show me bank statement.
  • Banks decades doing this, but WorldFirst how many years? Trust who?
  • Got Malaysia regulatory license? BNM approved or not?

Here's what every business owner needs to know about the P&L line their bank never prints. Your bank is one of the biggest costs in your sourcing budget. And the strange part is, you can't even see it. Here's the story. This year, you spent 500,000 dollars on Chinese suppliers. Next year, you'll probably spend another 500,000 dollars. The year after, the same again. On every single wire, your bank quietly adds about 2 percent inside the exchange rate. So 10,000 dollars a year goes to the bank instead of staying in your business. Stretch that across five years. That's 50,000 dollars. Now, this isn't a fee. There's no fee column showing this. It's just the gap between the real rate that banks use with each other and the rate they sell to you. And the part most business owners miss. You'll never see a line item for it anywhere. Not on a receipt. Not on a statement. Not on the bank app. We're WorldFirst. We move money to China at near the real bank-to-bank rate on every payment. Same suppliers. The 10,000 dollars stays in your business. Where it belongs.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)

Here's the problem most business owners don't see when they review their sourcing P&L. The fee is not the cost. You see a 25 dollar wire fee on the bank statement, you think that's the price of moving money. Wrong. The real cost is the exchange rate margin. Your bank doesn't give you the actual USD to CNY rate. They mark it up 2%. On 500K of annual supplier payments, that's 10,000 dollars taken silently. You will never see it on a statement because it's baked into the rate itself. Specialist FX rails run at 0.3 to 0.5% margin. Same wire. Same destination. One-quarter the cost. This is how banks make billions off business owners who only check the fee column. Stop. WorldFirst uses near-interbank rates on every CNY payment. Same 500K. You save the 10K. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

In this video: why the real cost of a China wire lives in the rate, not the fee. The fee is not the cost. You see a 25 dollar wire fee on the bank statement, you think that's the price of moving money. Wrong. The real cost is the exchange rate margin. Your bank doesn't give you the actual USD to CNY rate. They mark it up 2%. On 500K of annual supplier payments, that's 10,000 dollars taken silently. You will never see it on a statement because it's baked into the rate itself. Specialist FX rails run at 0.3 to 0.5% margin. Same wire. Same destination. One-quarter the cost.
