In this video: why the real cost of a China wire lives in the rate, not the fee. When you wire money to China, you look at the bank statement and you see a wire fee. Maybe 25 dollars. You think, that's the price of moving the money. We're sorry to tell you. That's not the cost. Here's the real story. To send money to China, your bank has to swap your dollars into Chinese yuan. They need an exchange rate to do that swap. But here's the trick. They don't give you the actual rate that banks use with each other. They quietly add around 2 percent on top. So when you send 500,000 dollars to suppliers over a year, your bank takes 10,000 dollars in the rate. You will never see that 10,000 dollars on your statement. Not as a fee. Not as a charge. Nothing. It's just baked into the rate. Now compare. Specialist money services that do the same wire to the same supplier charge around 0.3 to 0.5 percent. That's about one-quarter of what your bank takes. This is how banks make billions of dollars every year from business owners who only check the fee column. We're WorldFirst. We use near the real bank-to-bank rate on every China payment. Same 500K. You keep the 10,000 dollars. Search WorldFirst.

217 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Ok the fee vs rate distinction actually make sense, fair.
  • But 2% always? My bank not so bad. Maybe US bank different.
  • One-quarter the cost on paper, but how much WorldFirst really charge me?
  • Banks make billions, sure. But how WorldFirst make money then? Free?
  • Who regulate WorldFirst in Malaysia? Need to check before move money.

In this video: where 10,000 dollars goes silently every year on China sourcing, explained. We want to share something quiet that's happening to your money. This year, you spent 500,000 dollars paying suppliers in China. You think that's the full cost. It's not. Your bank quietly kept 10,000 dollars of that money inside the exchange rate. Here's what we mean. On every wire you send, your bank needs to change your dollars into Chinese yuan. They use a rate to do that. But the rate they give you is about 2 percent worse than the real one. They keep the difference. On a single wire, it doesn't feel like much. But across a whole year of payments, that 2 percent adds up to 10,000 dollars. And the tricky part is, you'll never spot it. Banks don't print the rate margin as a separate line. There's no receipt. There's no notification on your statement. It's all hidden inside the rate. We're WorldFirst. We move money to China at near the real bank-to-bank rate. That's the rate banks use with each other. Same supplier. Same 500,000 dollars of sourcing. You keep the 10,000. Worth knowing if your bank is quietly taking a slice on every wire. Search WorldFirst.

#1 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 124 words

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China with PayPal. You think PayPal is convenient. It is. It's also expensive. You send $20,000 to your supplier. PayPal takes $880 on the cross-border fee. Then $700 more on the FX margin nobody warned you about. Then your supplier hands you a PI with another 4 to 5% padded in because PayPal charges them too. By the time the goods ship, that $20K order cost you $22,380. Plus a 21-day fund hold on the supplier side that they price in next time. I see this every single week. SMEs paying retail prices for B2B volume because the app is familiar. Stop. WorldFirst. Specialist FX. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. Full $20K lands. Supplier doesn't pad. You sleep. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Here's exactly how much a 20K PayPal order costs once supplier-side fees stack. You think PayPal is convenient. You are right. It is convenient. It is also expensive. Picture this. You send twenty thousand dollars to your supplier. PayPal takes eight hundred and eighty dollars as a fee for sending money to another country. Then PayPal adds another seven hundred dollars on top of the exchange rate when they turn your dollars into Chinese money, and nobody warned you about this one. Then your supplier hands you an invoice with another four or five percent quietly padded in, because PayPal charges them a fee on their side too. By the time your goods are ready to ship, that twenty thousand dollar order actually cost you twenty two thousand three hundred and eighty dollars. And on top of that, PayPal holds your supplier's money for twenty one days, so they price that delay into your next order too. This is happening every single week to business owners. Small business owners paying everyday shopping prices on wholesale volume, just because the app feels familiar. We built WorldFirst for this. Specialist exchange rates. Chinese money sent straight to Chinese bank accounts. The full twenty thousand dollars lands. Your supplier does not need to pad the invoice. You sleep better. Just search WorldFirst.

#2 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 136 words

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Watch this. You pay your Chinese supplier 20K through PayPal once a month. The receipt says 20K. The real cost is closer to 22K. Why? 4.4% cross-border fee, that's 880 dollars. 3.5% FX margin, that's another 700. Plus a 4 to 5% supplier markup because they're absorbing PayPal's receiving fee. Add it up: 2,380 dollars per order. Now run it for a year. Twelve orders. That's 28,560 dollars handed to a payments app and a padded invoice line. Three years? Over 85,000 dollars. That's a hire. That's inventory. That's your kid's school fees. And it's not theft. It's just PayPal pricing built for retail volume, not 20K supplier payments. Listen. WorldFirst uses specialist FX rates and pays Chinese suppliers in CNY directly. Same 20K. The leak shrinks to hundreds, not thousands. Every order. Every year. Search WorldFirst. Period.

This is what three years of PayPal supplier payments actually looks like. You run a small business that buys from China. Every month, you pay your supplier twenty thousand dollars through PayPal. Easy, right? The receipt says twenty thousand. But the real cost is closer to twenty two thousand. Let us show you why. PayPal charges a four point four percent fee for sending money to another country. That is eight hundred and eighty dollars. PayPal also adds three and a half percent to the exchange rate when it changes your dollars into Chinese money. That is another seven hundred dollars. And here is the sneaky part. Your supplier also gets charged a fee when they receive PayPal money, so they add four or five percent to your invoice without telling you. That is another eight hundred dollars hidden in the price. Add it up and you are losing two thousand three hundred and eighty dollars on every single order. Now imagine that happens twelve times a year. That is twenty eight thousand five hundred and sixty dollars going out the back door. Over three years, that adds up to more than eighty five thousand dollars. That is a salary you could pay someone. That is more inventory. That is your kid's school for the year. Nobody is stealing from you. PayPal was built for everyday shoppers, not for business owners sending twenty thousand dollars to factories. We built WorldFirst for that. We use specialist exchange rates and send Chinese money straight to Chinese bank accounts. The leak shrinks to hundreds. Every order. Every year. Just search WorldFirst.

  • My order size varies. Sometimes 5K sometimes 30K. This math doesn't reflect reality.

  • PayPal got business rates that lower right? You picking retail tier to make it sound bad.

Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers through PayPal, you're getting destroyed and you don't even see itthe real cost is much higher than the receipt. Here's the math. You send 20K to your supplier. The receipt says 20K. The actual cost? is way higher. PayPal hits you with a 4.4% cross-border fee. That's 880 dollars gone. Then they take another 3.5% on the exchange rate. That's another 700 dollars. So your visible cost just hit 1,580. But it gets worse. Stop. Your supplier knows PayPal charges them on the receiving side too. So they pad your invoice 4 to 5%. That's another 800 dollars baked into the PI. Total damage on a 20K order? 2,380 dollars. Eleven percent gone. WorldFirst was built for this. Specialist rate. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. Same supplier. Hundreds in fees, not thousands. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

deleted / added

Here's what every business owner needs to know about the true cost of a 20K PayPal supplier payment. Here's the math. You send 20K to your supplier. The receipt says 20K. The actual cost is way higher. PayPal hits you with a 4.4% cross-border fee. That's 880 dollars gone. Then they take another 3.5% on the exchange rate. That's another 700 dollars. So your visible cost just hit 1,580. But it gets worse. Your supplier knows PayPal charges them on the receiving side too. So they pad your invoice 4 to 5%. That's another 800 dollars baked into the PI. Total damage on a 20K order? 2,380 dollars. Eleven percent gone. WorldFirst was built for this. Specialist rate. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. Same supplier. Hundreds in fees, not thousands. Search WorldFirst.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about the true cost of a 20K PayPal supplier payment. Say you run a small business. You buy products from a factory in China. You owe them twenty thousand dollars for your next order. So you open PayPal and send the money. The screen says twenty thousand. Done, right? Not really. Here is what is going on behind that screen. First, PayPal takes a four point four percent fee for sending money across borders. That is eight hundred and eighty dollars gone. Then PayPal also changes your dollars into Chinese money, and they add three and a half percent on top of the real exchange rate. That is another seven hundred dollars. So far you have lost one thousand five hundred and eighty dollars and the receipt does not show most of it. Now here is the part most people miss. Your supplier in China also pays a fee when they get PayPal money. So to protect themselves, they quietly add four or five percent onto your invoice. That is another eight hundred dollars hidden inside the price they quoted you. Add it all up. Your twenty thousand dollar order actually cost you about twenty two thousand three hundred and eighty dollars. That is eleven percent of your money gone. We built WorldFirst for this exact problem. We give you a specialist exchange rate, and we send Chinese money straight to Chinese bank accounts. Same supplier, same order, but the fees drop from thousands to hundreds. Just search WorldFirst.

255 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • My supplier never pad my PI for PayPal lah. Where this 4-5% from?
  • PayPal fee for me is 3.9% not 4.4%. Numbers feel off.
  • Eleven percent? That's only if everything stacks worst case. Not always.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Never hear before. They got license in MY?
  • Show me real rate comparison side by side la, not just claim.

Receipt-doesn't-match-reality opener

Your PayPal receipt is lying to you. Not technically. Just functionally. Here's how. You sent 20K to your Chinese supplier. The receipt confirmed 20K. Done deal, right? Wrong. PayPal took 4.4% off the top, 880 dollars, before the wire even moved. Then they marked up the exchange rate 3.5%, another 700 dollars gone, baked into the rate so it never shows as a fee. That's 1,580 dollars on a 20K order. And here's the part nobody warns you about. Your supplier sees PayPal coming and adds 4 to 5% to your invoice because they're eating a receiving fee on their end. So your actual cost lands closer to 22,380 dollars. The receipt said 20K. You paid 22K. Stop trusting receipts that hide the math. WorldFirst publishes the rate. Specialist FX. Direct CNY to Chinese banks. Search WorldFirst. Period.

PayPal charges four point four percent to send money across borders. On a twenty thousand dollar order, that is eight hundred and eighty dollars. You see this one on the receipt. Here's what every business owner needs to know about the gap between a PayPal receipt and the real supplier cost. The receipt is not the cost. You send $20K to your Chinese factory. Receipt confirms $20K. Done? Not quite. PayPal took 4.4% as a cross-border fee, that's $880, you saw that one. Then 3.5% FX margin on the conversion to CNY, another $700, you didn't see that. Then your supplier added 4 to 5% to the invoice because they're absorbing PayPal's receiving fee on their side. That's $800 hiding in your PI. Total damage on a $20K order? $2,380. Almost 12 percent. PayPal is brilliant for retail. It's the wrong tool for $20K supplier orders. WorldFirst is built differently. Specialist FX. Direct CNY to Chinese banks. The rate you see is the rate you get. That's the difference.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about the gap between a PayPal receipt and the real supplier cost. The receipt is not the real cost. Picture this. You send twenty thousand dollars to your factory in China. PayPal confirms twenty thousand dollars. Job done, right? Not quite. Let us break down what really happened. First, PayPal took four point four percent as a cross border fee. That is eight hundred and eighty dollars. You saw that one on the receipt. Then PayPal added three and a half percent on top of the exchange rate when they changed your dollars into Chinese money. That is another seven hundred dollars. You did not see this one because it was hidden inside the rate. Then your supplier added four or five percent to the invoice because they get charged a fee on their side when they receive PayPal money. That is another eight hundred dollars hiding in your price. Add it up and the real damage on a twenty thousand dollar order is two thousand three hundred and eighty dollars. That is almost twelve percent gone. PayPal is great for everyday shopping. It is just the wrong tool for twenty thousand dollar supplier orders. We built WorldFirst differently. Specialist exchange rates. Chinese money sent straight to Chinese banks. The rate you see is the rate you get. That is the real difference. Just search WorldFirst.

228 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Rate you see is rate you get - so WorldFirst takes zero margin? Sure or not?
  • Brilliant for retail wrong tool for B2B - actually this part fair.
  • Almost 12% only if supplier really adds 4-5%. Not standard for everyone.
  • WorldFirst direct CNY to Chinese banks - they have license for SAFE compliance?
  • I can audit PayPal cost. How do I audit WorldFirst cost before I switch?

→ → →

Watch this. PayPal at 200 dollars charges you 9 bucks. You don't even notice. PayPal at 20 thousand charges you 880 dollars. Same app. Same swipe. The product didn't change. The math did. Personal-volume apps are priced for personal volumes. When you scale up from buying a sweater to paying a factory, that percentage doesn't shrink. It grows with every zero. 4.4% on 200 is coffee money. 4.4% on 20K is a flight to Guangzhou. And we haven't even talked about FX. PayPal adds another 3.5% margin on the dollar-to-CNY conversion. That's 700 more dollars gone before your supplier sees a cent. Total cost: roughly 1,580 dollars on a 20K wire. Stop. WorldFirst is priced for business-volume payments. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. The bigger the order, the more you save. Search WorldFirst. Period.

PayPal at 20 thousand charges you 880 dollars. Same app. Same swipe. The product didn't change. The math did. Personal-volume apps are priced for personal volumes. When you scale up from buying a sweater to paying a factory, that percentage doesn't shrink. It grows with every zero. 4.4% on 200 is coffee money. 4.4% on 20K is a flight to Guangzhou. This is what PayPal fees look like at 200 bucks. And this is what they look like at 20K. A small story about scale. Watch what happens. You open PayPal. You send 200 dollars. PayPal takes 9 bucks. You don't even look twice. Now imagine the same screen. The same button. The same little logo. But the number is bigger. You send 20 thousand. And PayPal takes 880 dollars from you. The app didn't grow up. The app didn't add new features. The only thing that changed was the size of your payment. Here's the part nobody explains. Apps for normal people charge a percent. That percent feels tiny when you spend a little. But that same percent gets huge when you spend a lot. 4.4 percent on 200 dollars is coffee money. 4.4 percent on 20 thousand is a plane ticket to Guangzhou. And we haven't even talked about swapping dollars to Chinese money. PayPal adds another 3 and a half percent on that swap. That's 700 more dollars gone before your supplier even sees the money. Add it up. One 20 thousand dollar payment really costs you about 1,580 dollars. We made WorldFirst for business size payments. We use a rate close to what the big banks use. We send to Chinese accounts the same day. The bigger your order, the more you keep. Search WorldFirst.

#2 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 132 words

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China through PayPal. You think it's the easy option. It's not. You send 20K. Supplier sees the funds. Then PayPal puts a 21-day hold on the receiver side. Supplier won't start production because the money isn't fully released. You're calling PayPal. PayPal says it's policy. Supplier says they want T/T next time. Meanwhile you're paying 880 in fees and another 700 in FX margin. 1,580 dollars to send a payment that doesn't even fully arrive for three weeks. Your customers are waiting. Your inventory is late. Your margin is bleeding. And you're staring at a hold screen on an app built for refunding sneakers. I see this every week. Every single week. Stop. WorldFirst. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. No hold. Supplier starts. You sleep. Search WorldFirst. Now.

A business owner sends 20K through PayPal. Three weeks later the supplier still won't start. You think PayPal is the easy way. It's not. Here's what really happens. You send 20 thousand dollars to your factory. The supplier sees the money show up on their screen. They're happy. Then PayPal puts a 21 day hold on their side. The money is there, but it's locked. Your supplier won't start production yet. They can't touch it. So you call PayPal. PayPal says it's policy. There's nothing you can do. You call your supplier. They tell you they want a bank wire next time. They don't trust this rail. While all this is happening, you've still paid 880 dollars in fees. Plus 700 more in the currency swap. That's 1,580 dollars. To send a payment that doesn't even fully land for three weeks. Your customers are waiting. Your inventory is late. Your margin is bleeding. And you're staring at a hold screen on an app that was built for refunding sneakers. This happens every week. Every single week. We built WorldFirst the other way. We use a rate close to the big banks. We send to Chinese accounts the same day. No hold. Supplier starts. You sleep. Search WorldFirst.

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers through PayPal, you're being charged like a tourist. Here's the math. PayPal at 200 dollars feels cheap. 4.4% is 9 bucks. You shrug. PayPal at 20 thousand is 880 dollars. Same app. Same button. Same brand. Different reality. And that's just the transaction fee. FX margin to CNY adds another 3.5%. That's 700 more. Now your supplier adds 4 to 5% to the PI because they're eating a receiving fee too. Your 20K payment just cost you over 2,300 dollars in real money. Stop. PayPal was built for buying t-shirts, not paying factories. WorldFirst is built for business-volume payments. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. If you wouldn't hand a bank teller 880 dollars in cash, don't hand it to an app. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Now your supplier adds 4 to 5% to the PI because they're eating a receiving fee too. Your 20K payment just cost you over 2,300 dollars in real money. Stop. PayPal was built for buying t-shirts, not paying factories. WorldFirst is built for business-volume payments. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. This is why paying Chinese suppliers through PayPal makes a business owner bleed margin. Here's the math. PayPal at 200 dollars feels cheap. 4.4% is 9 bucks. You shrug. PayPal at 20 thousand is 880 dollars. Same app. Same button. Same brand. Different reality. And that's just the transaction fee. FX margin to CNY adds another 3.5%. That's 700 more. Now your supplier adds 4 to 5% to the PI because they're eating a receiving fee too. Your 20K payment just cost you over 2,300 dollars in real money. PayPal was built for buying t-shirts, not paying factories. WorldFirst is built for business-volume payments. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. This is why paying Chinese suppliers through PayPal makes a business owner bleed margin. You own a small business. You buy from a factory in China. You use PayPal to pay them. It feels easy. It feels safe. But there's a story under that easy button. We want to walk you through it. Imagine you send 200 dollars first. PayPal charges you about 9 dollars. You don't really notice. You shrug. It feels like nothing. Now your business grows. The order gets bigger. You send 20 thousand dollars instead. Same app. Same button. Same logo. But now PayPal takes 880 dollars from you. That's just the first fee. Then they swap your dollars for Chinese money. They take another 3 and a half percent on that swap. That's about 700 more dollars gone. And here's the part most people miss. Your supplier on the other end pays a fee too. So they quietly raise their price by 4 or 5 percent. That's another 800 dollars baked into your invoice. Add it up. One 20 thousand dollar payment really cost you over 2,300 dollars. PayPal was made for buying a t-shirt. It was not made for paying a factory. We built WorldFirst for this exact job. We use a rate close to what the big banks use. We send the money to Chinese bank accounts the same day. If you wouldn't hand a stranger 880 dollars in cash, don't hand it to an app. Worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

236 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • 4.4% sound high lah, my PayPal sometimes show lower fee.
  • Supplier add 4-5% to PI? My one just quote and that's it. How I know if hidden?
  • Who is WorldFirst leh, first time hearing.
  • Sound like ad. Show real PayPal screenshot vs WorldFirst screenshot.
  • Tourist prices ok funny but I just want to know got Malaysia license or not.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

PayPal charged you 880 dollars on your last supplier payment and you didn't blink. Here's why. At 200 bucks the fee feels invisible because the fixed-fee portion eats most of the pain. At 20 thousand the percentage takes over. 4.4% kicks in fully. That's 880 dollars on transaction fee alone. Then add 3.5% FX margin on the conversion to CNY. That's another 700. Then your supplier quietly adds 4 to 5% to the PI because PayPal charges them too. By the time the money lands, you've paid roughly 2,300 dollars to use an app that was designed to split a dinner bill. I'll say it. PayPal isn't a B2B tool. It's a consumer wallet. WorldFirst is the business rail. Near-interbank rates. No PI surcharge from suppliers. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Stop paying app-tier fees on business-tier orders. Search WorldFirst. Period.

This is why a business owner pays 880 dollars on a PayPal wire and doesn't blink. You sent a payment to your supplier last month. PayPal took 880 dollars from you. And you barely felt it. We want to explain why. When you send 200 bucks, the fee feels invisible. That's because there's a small set fee mixed in. It hides the real percent. But when you send 20 thousand, the percent takes over. 4.4 percent kicks in for real. That alone is 880 dollars. Then PayPal swaps your money to Chinese yuan. They add another 3 and a half percent on that swap. That's 700 more dollars. Now here's the hidden part. Your supplier also pays a fee on their side. So they quietly add 4 to 5 percent to your invoice. That's around 800 more dollars baked into your bill. Add it all up. One supplier payment really cost you about 2,300 dollars. And you used an app that was made for splitting a dinner bill with your friends. PayPal is a wallet for shoppers. We built WorldFirst as a rail for businesses. We use rates close to the big banks. Suppliers don't add a fee on our rail because they don't get charged on it. We send to Chinese bank accounts the same day. Search WorldFirst.

Direct address + problem stack

If you pay Chinese suppliers through PayPal, you need to hear this. Real talk. Three things happen when you scale up and you only feel one of them. One. At 200 bucks you pay 9 dollars in fees and you barely notice. Two. At 20 thousand you pay 880 dollars in transaction fees and it feels like getting hit by a truck. Same app. Same percentage. Bigger number. Three. PayPal adds another 3.5% FX margin on the CNY conversion. That's 700 more. Plus your supplier quietly adds 4 to 5% to the PI because they're also paying PayPal a receiving fee. Your 20K supplier order just cost you roughly 2,300 dollars in real money, plus 21-day hold risk on the supplier side that they're pricing into your quote. Stop using consumer apps for business volumes. WorldFirst is built for this. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. No PI surcharge. If you're a business owner paying China, you need WorldFirst. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Bigger number. Three. PayPal adds another 3.5% FX margin on the CNY conversion. That's 700 more. Plus your supplier quietly adds 4 to 5% to the PI because they're also paying PayPal a receiving fee. Your 20K supplier order just cost you roughly 2,300 dollars in real money, plus 21-day hold risk on the supplier side that they're pricing into your quote. When a business owner scales past 20K wires, here are 3 things to fix right away. Three things happen when you scale up and you only feel one of them. One. At 200 bucks you pay 9 dollars in fees and you barely notice. Two. At 20 thousand you pay 880 dollars in transaction fees and it feels like getting hit by a truck. Same app. Same percentage. Bigger number. Three. PayPal adds another 3.5% FX margin on the CNY conversion. That's 700 more. Plus your supplier quietly adds 4 to 5% to the PI because they're also paying PayPal a receiving fee. When a business owner scales past 20K wires, here are 3 things to fix right away. Three things happen when your orders get bigger. And most business owners only ever feel one of them. We want to walk you through all three. First thing. You send 200 bucks. PayPal takes 9 dollars. You barely notice it. Life goes on. Second thing. Your business grows. You send 20 thousand to your supplier. PayPal takes 880 dollars from you in fees alone. It feels like getting hit by a truck. The app didn't change. The percent didn't change. Just the number. Third thing. PayPal also swaps your dollars to Chinese money. They quietly add 3 and a half percent on that swap. That's 700 more dollars. And there's a fourth hidden layer. Your supplier on the other end pays a fee too. So they raise your invoice by 4 to 5 percent. That's around 800 more dollars baked in. Plus your supplier worries about a 21 day hold on their PayPal account. So they price that risk into your quote as well. Add it up. One supplier order really cost you about 2,300 dollars. We built WorldFirst for this exact moment. We use a rate close to the big banks. We send to Chinese accounts the same day. No hidden swap margin. No fee on the supplier side. If you're a business owner paying China, WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

I'll say it. PayPal is the most expensive rail in your supply chain and nobody talks about it. PayPal at 200 dollars costs 9 bucks. Nobody cares. PayPal at 20K costs 880 dollars. Now you care. Now run the math. Once a month. That's 880 a month. 10K a year just on transaction fees, before FX. Add the 3.5% FX margin and you're at 1,580 per wire. Twelve wires a year. That's nearly 19 grand a year. Disappearing into an app you only signed up for because it was easy. That's a hire. That's inventory. That's your margin. PayPal won't tell you this because they make more money the bigger your orders get. WorldFirst uses near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. The percentage goes down, not up. Period.

#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 138 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the problem most business owners don't see when they scale a PayPal habit. The fee scales linearly. The pain doesn't. At 200 bucks, the percentage feels like nothing because it's wrapped inside a fixed-fee floor. At 20 thousand, the percentage is the whole story. 4.4% on 20K is 880 dollars. The app didn't get fancier. The app didn't add features. The app just multiplied the same number against a bigger payment. And on top of that, PayPal adds a 3 to 4% FX margin when you convert to CNY. That's another 600 to 800 dollars buried in the rate. So your 20K supplier payment actually costs you closer to 1,580. The mechanism is volume mismatch. PayPal was built for retail, not factory orders. Stop. WorldFirst is built for business volume. Near-interbank rates. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Search WorldFirst. Period.

That's another 600 to 800 dollars buried in the rate. So your 20K supplier payment actually costs you closer to 1,580. The mechanism is volume mismatch. PayPal was built for retail, not factory orders. Stop. WorldFirst is built for business volume. Near-interbank rates. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Search WorldFirst. Period.

This is why scaling a PayPal habit silently scales your supplier-payment loss. The fee scales linearly. The pain doesn't. At 200 bucks, the percentage feels like nothing because it's wrapped inside a fixed-fee floor. At 20 thousand, the percentage is the whole story. 4.4% on 20K is 880 dollars. The app didn't get fancier. The app didn't add features. The app just multiplied the same number against a bigger payment. And on top of that, PayPal adds a 3 to 4% FX margin when you convert to CNY. That's another 600 to 800 dollars buried in the rate. So your 20K supplier payment actually costs you closer to 1,580. The mechanism is volume mismatch. PayPal was built for retail, not factory orders. WorldFirst is built for business volume. Near-interbank rates. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Search WorldFirst.

This is why scaling a PayPal habit silently scales your supplier-payment loss. The fees grow in a straight line. But the pain doesn't. It hides. Let us explain. When you send 200 bucks, the percent feels like nothing. That's because PayPal tucks a small set fee into the total. The percent hides inside that. But when you send 20 thousand, the percent is the whole story. There's nowhere for it to hide. 4.4 percent of 20 thousand is 880 dollars. Gone. The app didn't get smarter. It didn't add new tools. It just took the same percent and pointed it at a bigger number. And there's more. When PayPal turns your dollars into Chinese money, they add 3 to 4 percent on top of the real rate. That's another 600 to 800 dollars hidden inside the exchange. So your 20 thousand supplier payment really costs you closer to 1,580 dollars. The whole problem has a name. It's a size mismatch. PayPal was made for shoppers buying small things. It was not made for businesses paying factories. We made WorldFirst for business size payments. We use a rate close to the big banks. We send to Chinese accounts the same day. Search WorldFirst.

203 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Fee scale linearly pain not, ok this teaching I can use.
  • 3 to 4% FX is more honest. At least give a range.
  • 1,580 on 20K is what 7.9%? Still feel high but ok if true.
  • WorldFirst built for business volume, can verify? Got minimum?
  • Who regulate WorldFirst in Malaysia?

Stop using PayPal to pay Chinese suppliers. I'm serious. PayPal is a tool. It's the wrong tool. You wouldn't use a calculator app to do your taxes. So why are you using a consumer wallet to send factory-scale payments? Here's what happens. At 200 dollars you pay 9 bucks. Fine. At 20K you pay 880. Painful. Add 3.5% FX margin on the CNY conversion. Another 700. Now your supplier adds 4 to 5% to the PI because they're eating a receiving fee on their side. That's another 800 to 1000 baked into your invoice. Your 20K order really costs you 22 grand. WorldFirst is built for this. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. No PI surcharge from suppliers because they actually want to receive on this rail. Use the right tool for the job. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about PayPal. It's not the fixed fee that gets you. It's the percentage that doesn't shrink as you scale. At 200 dollars you pay 9 bucks. At 20K you pay 880. The app stayed the same. Your payment got bigger. PayPal got richer. Then there's the 3 to 4% FX margin on CNY conversion. That's 600 to 800 more. Silently baked into the rate. Then your supplier prices in their own PayPal receiving fee, quietly adding 4 to 5% to your PI. None of this is on a receipt. None of this is in the app. This is how it works. Every single time you scale up. PayPal doesn't change the percentage. So you better. WorldFirst is built for business-volume payments. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. The rate you see is the rate you get. Period.

#1 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 123 words

Real talk + supplier's view

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatLow

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China. You're treating the credit card like a payment method. Your supplier is treating it like a liability. You see 2.95% as a fee you'll happily eat. Your supplier sees 2.95% coming out of their margin because the merchant pays card fees, not the cardholder. You see chargeback protection. Your supplier sees 180 days of their bank reversing the money on goods they already shipped. You see Visa. Your supplier sees a system 95% of their factory peers don't even use. The card isn't bad. It's just not for this. I see this every week. Every single week. Stop. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers in CNY on Alipay, WeChat, and local bank rails. The systems they actually use. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about who really pays the card fee on China orders. You're treating the credit card like a payment method. Your supplier is treating it like a liability. You see 2.95% as a fee you'll happily eat. Your supplier sees 2.95% coming out of their margin because the merchant pays card fees, not the cardholder. You see chargeback protection. Your supplier sees 180 days of their bank reversing the money on goods they already shipped. You see Visa. Your supplier sees a system 95% of their factory peers don't even use. The card isn't bad. It's just not for this. This happens every week. Every single week. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers in CNY on Alipay, WeChat, and local bank rails. The systems they actually use. Search WorldFirst.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about who really pays the card fee on China orders. You're looking at the credit card like a way to pay. Your supplier is looking at it like a risk to their business. Picture both sides at the same time. You see a three percent fee, and you're happy to eat it. Your supplier sees that same three percent coming out of their own pocket, because in card payments, the seller pays the fee, not the buyer. Most people don't even know that. You see protection if something goes wrong. Your supplier sees six months of fear where their bank could pull the money back on goods they already shipped. You see Visa, the brand you know. Your supplier sees a system that 95 percent of other factories around them don't even use. So the card isn't bad. It's just not made for this kind of deal. This same scene plays out every single week between buyers and Chinese suppliers. At WorldFirst, we pay Chinese suppliers in their own money through Alipay, WeChat, and local bank rails. The same apps they use every day. Just search WorldFirst.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Merchant pays card fees not cardholder, ok this part I actually didn't know. Useful.
  • 95% of factory peers don't use Visa, this is direct card right? Trade Assurance different.
  • Buyer vs supplier perspective, this is the best take in the set actually. Empathy wins.
  • This happens every week, bit much. Maybe every few months for me.
  • WorldFirst on Alipay WeChat local bank rails, which exactly? Partnership?
#2 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 140 words

Cold open + mechanism stack

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Watch this. You offered your supplier a credit card. They asked for T/T. You thought they were being difficult. They weren't. Here's the actual stack. One. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese suppliers have card acceptance set up. Bank card terminals are rare in Chinese B2B. Most factories were never wired for Visa. Two. The 2.95% processing fee comes out of the supplier's margin, not yours. They're not paying that on a 50K order. Three. The 180-day chargeback window means if you dispute, the money gets clawed back after materials are already cut. Four. Alipay and WeChat Pay are the standard. That's what every other buyer uses. Period. Stop blaming the supplier. The card was built for Amazon checkouts, not factory POs. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers in CNY on the rails they already use. No 180-day risk. No 3% fee on their side. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Watch this. You offered your supplier a credit card. They asked for T/T. You thought they were being difficult. They weren't. Here's the actual stack. One. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese suppliers have card acceptance set up. Bank card terminals are rare in Chinese B2B. Most factories were never wired for Visa. Two. The 2.95% processing fee comes out of the supplier's margin, not yours. They're not paying that on a 50K order. Three. The 180-day chargeback window means if you dispute, the money gets clawed back after materials are already cut. Four. Alipay and WeChat Pay are the standard. That's what every other buyer uses. Period. Stop blaming the supplier. TheThe card was built for Amazon checkouts, not factory POs. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers in CNY on the rails they already use. No 180-day risk. No 3% fee on their side. Search WorldFirst. Period.

In this video: the 4 reasons a Chinese supplier picks T/T over your card, explained simply. You offered your supplier a credit card. They asked for T/T. You thought they were being difficult. They weren't. Here's the actual stack. One. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese suppliers have card acceptance set up. Bank card terminals are rare in Chinese B2B. Most factories were never wired for Visa. Two. The 2.95% processing fee comes out of the supplier's margin, not yours. They're not paying that on a 50K order. Three. The 180-day chargeback window means if you dispute, the money gets clawed back after materials are already cut. Four. Alipay and WeChat Pay are the standard. That's what every other buyer uses. The card was built for Amazon checkouts, not factory POs. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers in CNY on the rails they already use. No 180-day risk. No 3% fee on their side. Search WorldFirst.

In this video: the 4 reasons a Chinese supplier picks T/T over your card, explained simply. You wanted to pay your supplier with a credit card. They asked for a bank transfer instead. You thought they were being annoying. They weren't. Let me walk you through what's actually happening. First thing. Out of every 100 Chinese suppliers, only 3 to 5 are set up to take a credit card. The little card machines you see at coffee shops? Most Chinese factories don't have them. They were never wired up for that. Second thing. When you swipe a card, the supplier pays about three percent of the total to the card company. On a 50,000 dollar order, that's 1,500 dollars coming out of their pocket. Not yours. Third thing. The credit card gives you 180 days to call the bank and get your money back. That's six months. The factory has already bought the cloth, made the parts, shipped the boxes. If you call the bank later, they lose everything. Fourth thing. In China, almost everyone uses Alipay or WeChat Pay. That's what every other buyer in China pays with. So your card was built for buying stuff on Amazon, not for paying a real factory. At WorldFirst, we send Chinese money straight to your supplier using the same apps they already use every day. No six-month risk. No three percent fee they have to eat. Just search WorldFirst.

224 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Supplier pays the 2.95% not me? Sure or not? On my Alibaba card transactions buyer side also charged.
  • Trade Assurance card payment got escrow lah. Not the same as direct card.
  • Where the 3 to 5% acceptance number come from? Show me the study.
  • Most factories never wired for Visa, true. But that's why I use Trade Assurance.
  • WorldFirst on Alipay rails? They got partnership or just messaging?
#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 144 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Listen. If you're a business owner trying to pay Chinese suppliers with a credit card, you're going to get rejected. Here's why. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese suppliers accept cards directly. The other 95% run on Alipay or WeChat Pay. So when you offer Visa, your supplier hears 'I want to pay you with a tool you can't accept.' Then it gets worse. A credit card gives you 180 days to dispute the charge. On goods that already shipped. To a factory that already spent the money on materials. So for the supplier, your card isn't a payment. It's a loan with a return policy. Stop. The card isn't broken. The category was built for retail, not factory orders. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese suppliers on the same rails they already use. No 180-day chargeback risk. No 3% fee on their side. Search WorldFirst. Period.

The other 95% run on Alipay or WeChat Pay. So when you offer Visa, your supplier hears 'I want to pay you with a tool you can't accept.' Then it gets worse. A credit card gives you 180 days to dispute the charge. On goods that already shipped. To a factory that already spent the money on materials. So for the supplier, your card isn't a payment. Here's what every business owner needs to know about why Chinese suppliers refuse credit cards. Here's why. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese suppliers accept cards directly. The other 95% run on Alipay or WeChat Pay. So when you offer Visa, your supplier hears 'I want to pay you with a tool you can't accept.' Then it gets worse. A credit card gives you 180 days to dispute the charge. On goods that already shipped. To a factory that already spent the money on materials. So for the supplier, your card isn't a payment. It's a loan with a return policy. The card isn't broken. The category was built for retail, not factory orders. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese suppliers on the same rails they already use. No 180-day chargeback risk. No 3% fee on their side. Search WorldFirst.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about why Chinese suppliers refuse credit cards. You run a small business. You buy stuff from a factory in China. You try to pay with your credit card, and the supplier says no. You get frustrated. You think the supplier is being difficult. They're not. Here's what's really going on. Out of every 100 Chinese suppliers, only 3 to 5 of them can even take a credit card. The other 95 use apps called Alipay or WeChat Pay. So when you hold up your Visa, your supplier is basically hearing, 'I want to pay you with something you can't use.' And it gets worse. With a credit card, you have 180 days to call the bank and take the money back. That's six months. But your supplier already bought the cloth, the metal, the parts. They already shipped your order. So if you call the bank in month five, the supplier loses everything. The order, the materials, the money. To them, your card isn't a payment. It's a loan with a return policy. The card isn't broken. It was just built for shopping at stores, not for paying factories. At WorldFirst, we send Chinese money straight to your supplier through the apps they use every day. No chargeback fear. No three percent fee they have to swallow. Just search WorldFirst.

Here's what every importer should understand about why Chinese suppliers refuse cards. You thought your supplier was being unreasonable. They weren't. You were just using the wrong tool. Picture it like this. You offered to pay with a credit card. Your supplier said no, send a bank transfer. You probably thought it was about the fees. It wasn't. Let me show you what your supplier saw. They saw a card payment that gave you 180 days to take your money back. That's six months. On a 50,000 dollar order. By week one, the factory had already paid for all the cloth, the metal, the parts to make your stuff. If you called the bank in month five and got your money back, the supplier would lose the order, the boxes sitting in their warehouse, and the cost of all those materials. That's not a payment. That's a loan with a return policy. And here's another thing. Almost no Chinese factories have those little card machines anyway. They use Alipay and WeChat Pay. So your supplier wasn't being difficult. The credit card was just made for buying stuff at stores, not for paying a factory. At WorldFirst, we send Chinese money straight to your supplier through the apps they use every day. No chargeback fear. No three percent fee eating their pay. Just search WorldFirst.

215 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • My factory pays salary and ops via bank transfer lah, not WeChat. WeChat for retail mostly.
  • 95% no terminal, where this come from? My supplier in Yiwu got QR code everywhere.
  • Material lose in month 5 chargeback ok this part interesting. Got real case?
  • Trade Assurance escrow handle the chargeback risk lah. Not all card payment same.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Why I trust you over Alibaba?
#5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 149 words

Direct address + 3-thing stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

If you've ever offered a credit card to a Chinese supplier and got told no, you need to hear this. Real talk. Three things are happening at once. One. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories accept Visa or Mastercard. Bank card terminals are rare in Chinese B2B. The infrastructure isn't there. Two. The 2.95% processing fee gets eaten by the supplier, not you. On a 12K order that's 360 bucks coming out of their margin. They're not absorbing that. Three. The 180-day chargeback window means if you dispute next quarter, they lose everything. Materials gone. Inventory gone. Their bank claws the money back. So your supplier isn't being stubborn. They're protecting their business. Stop trying to force the card. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to your supplier on Alipay, WeChat, and bank rails. The systems they actually use. No chargeback. No 3% fee on their side. If you're paying China, WorldFirst is the tool. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories accept Visa or Mastercard. Bank card terminals are rare in Chinese B2B. The infrastructure isn't there. Two. The 2.95% processing fee gets eaten by the supplier, not you. On a 12K order that's 360 bucks coming out of their margin. They're not absorbing that. Three. The 180-day chargeback window means if you dispute next quarter, they lose everything. Materials gone. Inventory gone. Their bank claws the money back. So your supplier isn't being stubborn. Here's what every business owner needs to know about why the card gets rejected in China. Three things are happening at once. One. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories accept Visa or Mastercard. Bank card terminals are rare in Chinese B2B. The infrastructure isn't there. Two. The 2.95% processing fee gets eaten by the supplier, not you. On a 12K order that's 360 bucks coming out of their margin. They're not absorbing that. Three. The 180-day chargeback window means if you dispute next quarter, they lose everything. Materials gone. Inventory gone. Their bank claws the money back. So your supplier isn't being stubborn. They're protecting their business. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to your supplier on Alipay, WeChat, and bank rails. The systems they actually use. No chargeback. No 3% fee on their side. If you're paying China, WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

Here's what every business owner needs to know about why the card gets rejected in China. Listen up. Three things are happening at the same time, and once you see them, the whole picture makes sense. First thing. Out of every 100 Chinese factories, only 3 to 5 take Visa or Mastercard. The little card machines you see at shops in your country? Most Chinese factories don't have them. The wiring just isn't there. Second thing. When a card gets used, the supplier pays about three percent to the card company. Not you. On a 12,000 dollar order, that's 360 dollars coming out of their pay. They're not going to swallow that on every order. Third thing. The card lets you call the bank up to 180 days later and take the money back. Six months. By then the factory has already bought the parts, made your order, shipped it. If you call the bank in month five, the factory loses everything. The cloth, the boxes, the cash. So your supplier isn't being stubborn. They're protecting their family business. At WorldFirst, we send Chinese money straight to your supplier through Alipay, WeChat, and local bank rails. The same apps they use to pay their own kids' school fees. No chargeback fear. No three percent on their side. If you pay China a lot, WorldFirst is worth a look. Just search WorldFirst.

232 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • 12K order 360 fee, math check out. But still on Alibaba I never pay 360 extra leh.
  • 3 to 5% acceptance is direct card right? Trade Assurance got different model.
  • Supplier eat the 2.95%, where the source? My Alibaba transaction showed buyer fee.
  • Worth a look ok but who is WorldFirst? Owned by Ant Group? Trust factor.
  • Protecting their business, this empathy line is good. First time I see content explain supplier side.
#6 PG2 Gary Vee Opinion-led 134 words

I'll say it opinion-as-truth

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

I'll say it. The credit card is the most overrated tool in cross-border supplier payments and nobody wants to admit it. You offered Visa. Your supplier said T/T. You blamed the supplier. The supplier wasn't the problem. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories even have card acceptance. 95% have run on Alipay or WeChat for a decade. The card isn't part of their world. Plus the supplier eats the 2.95% fee. Plus they carry 180 days of chargeback risk. On goods they already shipped. So when you push the card, you're asking them to take less money and more risk for the same order. WorldFirst pays them in CNY on the rails they actually use. No fee on their side. No 180-day risk. The card category was built for retail. Factory orders are a different game. Period.

I'll say it. The credit card is the most overrated tool in cross-border supplier payments and nobody wants to admit it. You offered Visa. Your supplier said T/T. You blamed the supplier. The supplier wasn't the problem. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories even have card acceptance. 95% have run on Alipay or WeChat for a decade. The card isn't part of their world. Plus the supplier eats the 2.95% fee. Plus they carry 180 days of chargeback risk. On goods they already shipped. So when you push the card, you're asking them to take less money and more risk for the same order. WorldFirst pays them in CNY on the rails they actually use. No fee on their side. No 180-day risk. The card category was built for retail. Factory orders are a different game. Period.

In this video: why the credit card fails for cross-border supplier payments, explained simply. You offered Visa. Your supplier said T/T. You blamed the supplier. The supplier wasn't the problem. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories even have card acceptance. 95% have run on Alipay or WeChat for a decade. The card isn't part of their world. Plus the supplier eats the 2.95% fee. In this video: why the credit card fails for cross-border supplier payments, explained simply. The credit card is the most overrated tool when it comes to paying suppliers across borders. Picture it. You offered Visa. Your supplier asked for a bank transfer instead. You blamed the supplier. But the supplier wasn't the problem. Let me break it down. Out of every 100 Chinese factories, only 3 to 5 can even take a credit card. The other 95 have been using Alipay or WeChat for over ten years. The credit card just isn't part of their world. On top of that, when you pay by card, the supplier has to pay about three percent to the card company. That comes out of their pocket. And on top of that, they carry six months of risk where you could call the bank and take the money back. On goods they already shipped. So when you push the card, you're basically asking them to take less money and more risk for the same order. Why would anyone agree to that? At WorldFirst, we pay them in their own money through the apps they actually use. No fee on their side. No six-month risk. The credit card was built for shopping. Factory orders are a completely different game.

210 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Most overrated tool? Shipping cost overrate me lah, not card fee.
  • Trade Assurance card on Alibaba is fine for me. So 'not part of their world' is too strong.
  • Less money more risk same order, ok this framing actually clean.
  • How WorldFirst make money if no fee on supplier side? Got buyer side fee?
  • Show real comparison please. Card vs WorldFirst on same 12K transaction.
#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 164 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's what most business owners don't understand about credit cards and Chinese suppliers. The card isn't the problem. The category is. Credit cards were built for retail. You buy a sweater, you don't like it, you dispute, you get your money back. Beautiful. Now apply that to a factory order. You commission 10,000 units. The factory buys raw materials in week one. Cuts fabric in week three. Ships in week eight. You receive in week twelve. And the card gives you 180 days to dispute the whole thing. After materials are gone. After production is finished. After the ship has left port. No supplier on earth is going to accept that risk for a 2.95% fee they have to absorb. Stop. 95% of Chinese suppliers don't even have card terminals. They run on Alipay, WeChat Pay, and bank transfers. That's the category. WorldFirst sends CNY direct on the same rails. No chargeback risk for the supplier. No 3% fee they have to swallow. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Here's what most business owners don't understand about credit cards and Chinese suppliers. The card isn't the problem. The category is. Credit cards were built for retail. You buy a sweater, you don't like it, you dispute, you get your money back. Beautiful. Now apply that to a factory order. You commission 10,000 units. The factory buys raw materials in week one. Cuts fabric in week three. Ships in week eight. You receive in week twelve. And the card gives you 180 days to dispute the whole thing. After materials are gone. After production is finished. After the ship has left port. No supplier on earth is going to accept that risk for a 2.95% fee they have to absorb. Stop. 95% of Chinese suppliers don't even have card terminals. They run on Alipay, WeChat Pay, and bank transfers. That's the category. WorldFirst sends CNY direct on the same rails. No chargeback risk for the supplier. No 3% fee they have to swallow. Search WorldFirst. Period.

deleted / added

In this video: why credit cards were built for retail, not factory orders, explained simply. The card isn't the problem. The category is. Credit cards were built for retail. You buy a sweater, you don't like it, you dispute, you get your money back. Beautiful. Now apply that to a factory order. You commission 10,000 units. The factory buys raw materials in week one. Cuts fabric in week three. Ships in week eight. You receive in week twelve. And the card gives you 180 days to dispute the whole thing. After materials are gone. After production is finished. After the ship has left port. No supplier on earth is going to accept that risk for a 2.95% fee they have to absorb. 95% of Chinese suppliers don't even have card terminals. They run on Alipay, WeChat Pay, and bank transfers. That's the category. WorldFirst sends CNY direct on the same rails. No chargeback risk for the supplier. No 3% fee they have to swallow. Search WorldFirst.

In this video: why credit cards were built for retail, not factory orders, explained simply. The card isn't the problem. The type of payment is. Let me explain. Credit cards were made for shopping. You buy a sweater at the store. You wear it once, decide you hate it, return it, get your money back. Lovely. Now picture a factory order instead. You order 10,000 t-shirts from a factory in China. Week one, the factory buys all the cloth and thread. Week three, they cut the fabric. Week eight, they pack the boxes and put them on a ship. Week twelve, the ship lands at your warehouse. Through all of that, your credit card gives you 180 days to call the bank and take the money back. That's after the cloth is bought. After the t-shirts are sewn. After the ship has left China. No factory in the world is going to take that risk for a three percent fee they have to pay out of their own pocket. And on top of that, almost no Chinese factories even have card machines. They use Alipay, WeChat Pay, and bank transfers. That's how everyone in China pays everyone. At WorldFirst, we send Chinese money straight to your supplier on those same apps. No 180-day fear. No three percent fee for them. Just search WorldFirst.

221 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Card built for retail, this part actually make sense. Good teaching.
  • No supplier on earth take card? My larger supplier in Shenzhen accept card no problem.
  • Week 12 still inside the 180 day chargeback, ok now I understand why supplier scared.
  • WorldFirst CNY direct on same rails, what rails exactly? Show me.
  • If supplier eat 2.95% how come some Alibaba sellers still happy with card?
#8 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 129 words

Truth nobody's telling you

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about credit cards and Chinese suppliers. The card was never meant for this. The card was built for B2C. Coffee shops. Online stores. Hotel rooms. Places where the buyer walks away with the product on day one and disputes if it's broken. Now look at a factory order. 90-day production. 30-day shipping. 180-day chargeback window that outlasts both. Your supplier ships first, then waits five months to know if the money is real. Meanwhile only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories even have card terminals. The other 95% are on Alipay and WeChat. So when you push the card, you're forcing a retail tool onto a wholesale workflow. WorldFirst is built for the wholesale workflow. CNY direct to the supplier. Rails they already use. No chargeback drama. Period.

Here's the truthsomething nobody's telling you about credit cards and Chinese suppliers. The card was never meant for this. The card was built for B2C. Coffee shops. Online stores. Hotel rooms. Places where the buyer walks away with the product on day one and disputes if it's broken. Now look at a factory order. 90-day production. 30-day shipping. 180-day chargeback window that outlasts both. Your supplier ships first, then waits five months to know if the money is real. Meanwhile only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories even have card terminals. The other 95% are on Alipay and WeChat. So when you push the card, you're forcing a retail tool onto a wholesale workflow. WorldFirst is built for the wholesale workflowbuilt differently. CNY direct to the supplier. Rails they already use. No chargeback drama. Period.

In this video: why the card was built for retail, not factory orders, explained simply. The card was never meant for this. The card was built for B2C. Coffee shops. Online stores. Hotel rooms. Places where the buyer walks away with the product on day one and disputes if it's broken. Now look at a factory order. 90-day production. 30-day shipping. 180-day chargeback window that outlasts both. Your supplier ships first, then waits five months to know if the money is real. Meanwhile only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories even have card terminals. The other 95% are on Alipay and WeChat. So when you push the card, you're forcing a retail tool onto a wholesale workflow. WorldFirst is built differently. CNY direct to the supplier. Rails they already use. No chargeback drama.

In this video: why the card was built for retail, not factory orders, explained simply. The credit card was never meant for this kind of payment. It was built for stuff like coffee shops, online stores, hotel rooms. Places where you buy something, you take it home or use it that day, and if it's broken you can call the bank to take it back. Easy. Now picture a factory order instead. The factory spends three months making your stuff. Then a month shipping it across the ocean. That's four months total. But your credit card gives you six whole months to call the bank and reverse the payment. So your supplier ships first, then sits there for five months wondering if your money is real. Meanwhile, only 3 to 5 out of every 100 Chinese factories even have card machines. The other 95 use Alipay and WeChat. So when you push the card, you're forcing a shopping tool onto a factory job. At WorldFirst, we built things differently. We send Chinese money straight to your supplier through the apps they already use. No chargeback drama. Just search WorldFirst.

  • 90-day production 30-day shipping 180-day chargeback, math check. Good visual.
  • Card never meant for this, ok but Alibaba Trade Assurance designed card workflow for this exact case.
  • Coffee shops hotel rooms example, good. Made it click.
  • WorldFirst built differently, fine but show me how on a real transaction.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Need to know regulator before I trust.

The factory floor isn't a retail counter. Search WorldFirst. Now.

#1 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 143 words

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Real talk for every business owner who's ever stared at a transfer receipt and thought the math felt off. You're not crazy. The rate you saw on Google before you signed off, that's not the rate you got. The rate your bank app showed you, that already had a 2 to 4 percent margin baked in. You don't see that margin anywhere on the receipt. It's not a fee. It's not a charge. It's just a slightly worse rate quote than the one banks use among themselves. Run that 2 percent across a year of supplier payments. On 50,000 a month, the gap between a personal-tier spread and a business-tier spread is 9,000 dollars. Per year. Vanishing inside the rate quote. I see this every single week with business owners who didn't even know there was a second tier. Stop accepting retail spreads on business volume. WorldFirst quotes business-tier by default. Search WorldFirst. Now.

If a business owner ever stared at a wire receipt thinking it felt off. You were not wrong. The rate you saw on Google right before you signed off, that was not the rate you actually got. The rate your bank app showed you on the screen already had a markup baked in. Around 2 to 4 cents per dollar. You will not find that markup anywhere on your receipt. It is not listed as a fee. It is not listed as a charge. It just sits inside the rate quote as a slightly worse number than the one banks trade at among themselves. Now run that 2 percent across a year of paying your suppliers. Picture 50,000 dollars a month going out the door. The gap between a personal app spread and a business platform spread on that volume works out to 9,000 dollars a year. Vanishing inside the rate quote. We see this every week with business owners who never knew there was a second tier available to them. At WorldFirst, we quote the business tier by default. Search WorldFirst.

In this video: how a 1.5 percent gap compounds into 9,000 a year, explained simply. Picture a business owner like you. Every month, you send 50,000 dollars to your suppliers in China. You set up your transfer tool a few years ago when you were smaller. It was a quick personal app. Easy to sign up. Easy to use. Here is what most people miss. That personal app gives you a rate that is about 1.5 cents per dollar worse than what a business platform would give you. Now do the math with us. 1.5 percent of 50,000 dollars is 750 dollars. That is what you lose every single month. Just on the rate. Not on fees. Not on charges. Inside the rate. Now multiply by twelve months. You lose 9,000 dollars in one year. Keep going. Three years is 27,000 dollars. Five years is 45,000 dollars. That is the salary of one new team member. That is a full container of stock you could have shipped. That is the profit on an entire product line, just gone. And here is the fix. You do not need to change your bank. You do not need to change your suppliers. You just need to move to a business tier platform built for owners like you. That is what we do at WorldFirst. Search WorldFirst.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Listen. Open Google right now. Type THB to CNY. Whatever number it shows you, that rate does not exist for your wallet. That's the mid-market rate. It's the rate banks use among themselves. The rate you actually get is a different number, a worse number, and the difference is called the spread. On a personal tool, that spread is usually 2 to 4 percent. On a business platform, it's 0.3 to 1 percent. Same currency pair. Same day. Same minute. Two different rates. On a 20,000 dollar supplier wire, 2 percent is 400 dollars. 0.5 percent is 100 dollars. That's 300 dollars that disappeared inside the rate. No line item. No fee notification. Just a worse number than Google showed you. Stop. WorldFirst quotes business-tier rates direct to business owners. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

deleted / added

They said the Google rate is the rate. For wallets, that's wrong. Type THB to CNY. Whatever number it shows you, that rate does not exist for your wallet. That's the mid-market rate. It's the rate banks use among themselves. The rate you actually get is a different number, a worse number, and the difference is called the spread. On a personal tool, that spread is usually 2 to 4 percent. On a business platform, it's 0.3 to 1 percent. Same currency pair. Same day. Same minute. Two different rates. On a 20,000 dollar supplier wire, 2 percent is 400 dollars. 0.5 percent is 100 dollars. That's 300 dollars that disappeared inside the rate. No line item. No fee notification. Just a worse number than Google showed you. WorldFirst quotes business-tier rates direct to business owners. Search WorldFirst.

They said the Google rate is the rate. For wallets, that's wrong. Open Google on your phone. Type in THB to CNY. Look at the number Google gives you. Here is the part most business owners never hear. That rate on Google is not the rate your money gets. That number is called the mid-market rate. It is the rate the big banks use when they trade money with each other in a private club. You and your business are not in that club. So when you go to send money to your supplier in China, the app on your screen shows you a different rate. A worse one. The gap between the good rate and the worse rate has a name. It is called the spread. If you use a personal money app, that spread is usually 2 to 4 cents on every dollar. If you use a business platform, that same spread drops to less than 1 cent on every dollar. The currency is the same. The day is the same. The minute is the same. But the price is different. Here is what that looks like in real money. Say you send 20,000 dollars to your factory. At 2 percent, you lose 400 dollars inside the rate. At half a percent, you only lose 100. That is 300 dollars gone, on one single wire, and it never shows up on your receipt as a fee. It just sits inside the rate, quiet. At WorldFirst, we quote business owners the business tier by default. Search WorldFirst.

252 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)

  • 300 dollars saved on 20k? My last wire from Maybank was not that bad lah, maybe 0.8% only.

If you pay Chinese suppliers, you need to hear this. Real talk. The rate on Google is not the rate you get. The rate on your bank's app is not the rate you get either. There are two FX rates running side by side every minute of every day. The mid-market rate, which banks trade among themselves. And the retail rate, which is what gets quoted to you. The retail rate has a margin built in. On personal tier, 2 to 4 percent. On business tier, 0.3 to 1 percent. That gap is the spread. It's never a line item. Never a fee notification. Just a slightly worse number than what Google showed you. On a 20,000 dollar supplier wire that gap is 300 dollars. On 50,000 a month for a year, it's 9,000 dollars. Stop running business volume through personal-tier spreads. WorldFirst quotes business-tier rates by default. If you pay suppliers in China, WorldFirst is worth checking. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

If a business owner pays Chinese suppliers, this rate gap hits their margin. The rate on Google is not the rate you get. The rate on your bank app is not the rate you get either. Here is what is really going on. At any minute of any day, there are two exchange rates running side by side for the same currency pair. The first one is the mid-market rate. That is what banks use when they trade money among themselves in a private club. The second one is the retail rate. That is what gets quoted to you. The retail rate already has a markup baked in before you ever see it. On a personal app, that markup is between 2 and 4 cents per dollar. On a business platform, it is between half a cent and 1 cent per dollar. That gap is called the spread. You will never see it as a fee. You will never see it as a charge. You will only see it as a slightly worse number than what Google showed you. Here is what it looks like in money. On a 20,000 dollar supplier wire, the gap is around 300 dollars. If you send 50,000 dollars a month, the gap adds up to 9,000 dollars a year. We built WorldFirst so business owners get the business tier by default. If you pay suppliers in China, we are worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 140 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the part of currency exchange most business owners don't understand. There are actually two rates running in parallel at the same moment. Rate one. The mid-market rate. The rate banks quote to each other when they trade currency wholesale. That's the rate Google shows you. Rate two. The retail rate. The rate quoted to anyone outside the wholesale system. That includes you. The retail rate already has a margin baked into it. On personal-tier products that margin is 2 to 4 percent. On business-tier products it's 0.3 to 1 percent. Same currency pair. Same exact second. Two different prices depending on which tier you're on. The retail margin is called the spread. It's how providers make money on FX. They quote you a slightly worse rate than what they're actually paying. Stop accepting retail-tier spreads on business volume. WorldFirst quotes business-tier on every transfer. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Here's the part of currency exchange most business owners don't understand. There are actually two rates running in parallel at the same moment. Rate one. The mid-market rate. The rate banks quote to each other when they trade currency wholesale. That's the rate Google shows you. Rate two. The retail rate. The rate quoted to anyone outside the wholesale system. That includes you. The retail rate already has a margin baked into it. On personal-tier products that margin is 2 to 4 percent. On business-tier products it's 0.3 to 1 percent. Same currency pair. Same exact second. Two different prices depending on which tier you're on. The retail margin is called the spread. It's how providers make money on FX. They quote you a slightly worse rate than what they're actually paying. Stop accepting retail-tier spreads on business volume. WorldFirst quotes business-tier on every transfer. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

In this video: why the rate on your screen isn't the real one, explained simply. There are actually two rates running in parallel at the same moment. Rate one. The mid-market rate. The rate banks quote to each other when they trade currency wholesale. That's the rate Google shows you. Rate two. The retail rate. The rate quoted to anyone outside the wholesale system. That includes you. The retail rate already has a margin baked into it. On personal-tier products that margin is 2 to 4 percent. On business-tier products it's 0.3 to 1 percent. Same currency pair. In this video: why the rate on your screen isn't the real one, explained simply. At any second of any day, there are not one but two rates running side by side for the same pair of currencies. Let us walk you through both. Rate number one is called the mid-market rate. This is the rate that big banks use when they trade money with each other in their own wholesale club. When you type a currency pair into Google, this is the rate Google shows you. Rate number two is called the retail rate. This is the rate quoted to everyone who is not in the wholesale club. That includes you. The retail rate already has a markup built into it before you even see it on screen. If you are on a personal app, that markup is between 2 and 4 cents per dollar. If you are on a business platform, the markup is between half a cent and 1 cent per dollar. Same currency. Same second. Two different prices. That gap between the wholesale rate and your retail rate has a name. It is called the spread. The spread is how money providers earn on every transfer. They quote you a slightly worse rate than the one they are actually paying. At WorldFirst, we quote the business tier on every transfer. Search WorldFirst.
