#1 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 126 words

Real talk + frustration escalation

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatLow

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China. You're working harder every year and somehow keeping less. You added two new SKUs. You doubled marketing. You found a better factory. And your gross margin still went down. You know what nobody told you? Your bank takes 4% of your sourcing budget every year and the bigger you grow, the bigger they take. 500K spend, 20K to the bank. Scale to 1M, 40K to the bank. You're literally subsidising their growth with yours. Meanwhile you're losing sleep over a 50 cent unit price. Stop. The leak is the rail, not the factory. WorldFirst sits at 0.3 to 0.5% on the same wires. The 4% stops bleeding. Your scale starts compounding for you instead of for the bank. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Here is what every growing importer needs to know about why scale feeds the bank. They're working harder every year and somehow keeping less. Sound familiar? Picture this. The owner added two new product lines. They doubled the marketing budget. They even found a better factory in Guangzhou. And the gross margin still dropped. Here's what nobody told them. Scale up to one million dollars in sourcing, the bank takes 40,000 dollars. Double the work, double their take. The business is effectively subsidising the bank's growth with its own. Meanwhile, the owner is losing sleep over a 50 cent difference on a unit price. The leak is not the factory. The leak is the payment route. WorldFirst, the international payment service, sits at 0.3 to 0.5% on the same kind of payments. The 4% stops bleeding. The scale starts compounding for the business, not for the bank. #2 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 126 words

Cold-open + compounding budget math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Watch this. Your sourcing budget shrinks 4% every year and you don't even feel it. 500K to Chinese suppliers? Your bank quietly kept 20,000 dollars. Year two, your business grows 10%. You spend 550K. The leak grows with you. That's 22,000 gone. Year three, 605K spent, 24,200 leaked. Add it up. 66,200 dollars in three years. Not a fee. Not a charge. Just the exchange rate margin baked into every wire. And here's what kills me. You can't fix this by negotiating with your supplier. They never touched that money. Your annual leak goes from 20K to under 3K. Stop. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Your bank quietly kept 20,000 dollars. Year two, your business grows 10%. You spend 550K. The leak grows with you. That's 22,000 gone. Year three, 605K spent, 24,200 leaked. Add it up. 66,200 dollars in three years. Not a fee. Not a charge. Just the exchange rate margin baked into every wire. And here's the part that hurts. You can't fix this by negotiating with your supplier. They never touched that money. Your bank took it on the conversion. The only fix is the rail itself. WorldFirst runs 0.3 to 0.5% all-in. Same suppliers. Same wires. Your annual leak goes from 20K to under 3K. Search WorldFirst.

This is why growing 10% a year makes a business owner poorer, not richer. Every year, a quiet 4% slips out of the sourcing budget. Not a fee. Not a charge on the statement. It's hidden inside the exchange rate the bank uses when changing money into Chinese yuan. Let's run it slowly. Year one. A business owner spends 500,000 dollars buying from Chinese suppliers. The bank quietly keeps 20,000 dollars in that 4% margin. Year two. The business grows by 10 percent. Now they spend 550,000 dollars. The leak grows with the business. That's 22,000 dollars gone. Year three. They spend 605,000 dollars. Another 24,200 dollars walks out. Add it up. 66,200 dollars in three years. None of it shows up on a fee line. It's just the gap between the real market rate and what the bank gave them. Here's the part that stings. A business owner cannot fix this by talking to the supplier. The supplier never had that money. The bank took it during the currency swap, before the payment even left the country. The only real fix is to change where the payment travels. WorldFirst, the payment service, runs the same kind of payments at 0.3 to 0.5%. Same Chinese suppliers. Same payment routes. The annual leak drops from 20,000 to under 3,000 dollars. Search WorldFirst.

  • Three years compound assume my business grow 10% every year. Got that easy meh.
  • 66K over 3 years? Where the 4% assumption come from. CIMB charge me less.
  • If I really grow 10% a year I won't care about 4% lah.
  • Same suppliers same wires sound too clean. CN bank also got their own cut sometimes.

Listen. If you're a business owner sourcing from China, your bank is taking 4% of your annual budget. Every year. Here's the math. You spend 500K a year on Chinese suppliers. Your bank charges a 4% exchange rate margin. That's 20,000 dollars. Gone. Not on a fee line. Not on a statement. Baked into the rate so you never see it. Now run it over three years. That's 60,000 dollars your bank kept that should have stayed in your business. Stop. You can't negotiate this with your supplier. The supplier never saw that money. Your bank took it before the wire even left. The fix is not a harder PI negotiation. The fix is switching the wire rail. WorldFirst runs at 0.3 to 0.5%. Same 500K. Same suppliers. The leak drops to 2,000 dollars a year. You just got 18K back. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Here is what every business owner needs to know about the 4% leaving their sourcing budget. Every year. Here's the math. You spend 500K a year on Chinese suppliers. Your bank charges a 4% exchange rate margin. That's 20,000 dollars. Gone. Not on a fee line. Not on a statement. Baked into the rate so you never see it. Now run it over three years. That's 60,000 dollars your bank kept that should have stayed in your business. You can't negotiate this with your supplier. The supplier never saw that money. Here is what every business owner needs to know about the 4% leaving their sourcing budget. A business owner in Malaysia just paid their Chinese supplier. The factory got the goods ready. Everyone's happy. But here's the part nobody talks about. The bank quietly took 4% of that payment. Not as a fee. Not on the statement. They hid it inside the exchange rate. Let's walk through the numbers slowly. Say a business owner spends 500,000 dollars a year buying from China. The bank charges a 4% margin on the currency swap from ringgit or USD into Chinese yuan. That's 20,000 dollars gone in one year. The supplier never saw that money. The bank took it before the payment even crossed the border. Now keep going. Year two, the leak repeats. Year three, the leak repeats. After three years, 60,000 dollars stayed with the bank. That money should have gone back into the business. More stock. A new hire. Better packaging. Gone. Here's the thing. A business owner cannot fix this by pushing the supplier for a better price. The supplier is not the leak. The bank is the leak. The fix is switching where the payment travels. WorldFirst, the payment service WorldFirst owns, charges only 0.3 to 0.5% on the same 500K, paying the same suppliers. Suddenly the annual leak drops from 20,000 to about 2,000 dollars. That's 18,000 a year back in the business. Search WorldFirst.

  • 500K a year on suppliers, I wish lah. Small SME got nowhere near that.

Your bank took 20,000 dollars from you this year and you said thank you. Here's how. You spent 500K sourcing from China. Every wire carried a 4% margin in the exchange rate. 20K total. No fee line. No invoice. Buried in the conversion. You won't find it on a statement because banks don't itemize it. Most business owners think they're losing money to bad supplier deals. Wrong. The supplier never saw that 4%. Your bank pocketed it before the money left your country. You tried negotiating MOQ. You tried squeezing the PI. You're chasing pennies on a unit price while your bank takes 4% of the whole budget. WorldFirst is 0.3 to 0.5%. Same wires. Same suppliers. The 4% goes back into your inventory budget. Or your hire. Or your runway. Stop signing off on this. Search WorldFirst. Period.

This is why a business owner blames the supplier. It was never the supplier. Let's walk through how. Say they spent 500,000 dollars buying from Chinese suppliers over twelve months. Every single payment had a 4% margin tucked into the exchange rate. So when ringgit or USD got swapped into Chinese yuan, the bank quietly kept 4% of the value. Add up every payment and that's 20,000 dollars in one year. No fee line on the statement. No invoice. No alert. It's hidden inside the conversion itself. Banks don't break it out, so most business owners never see it. Most owners think they're losing money on bad supplier deals or weak negotiation. That's wrong. The supplier never touched that 4%. The bank kept it before the payment ever crossed the border into China. Meanwhile, the business owner spent weeks haggling over the minimum order, called MOQ. They pushed on the proforma invoice, the PI. They chased small wins on the unit price. That's good work, but it's chasing pennies while the bank quietly takes 4% of the entire sourcing budget. Here's the simple fix. WorldFirst, the international payment service, runs the same kind of payments at 0.3 to 0.5%. Same suppliers. Same destinations. Suddenly that 4% comes back. Into the stock budget. Into a new hire. Into the runway. Search WorldFirst.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Didn't even notice? Bro of course I noticed my supplier got less. Just thought it was fees.

  • Supplier never saw the money, ok that part actually clear my doubt. Fair point.
  • Banks don't itemize FX margin? They do in fine print. Just nobody reads.
  • Who is WorldFirst? First time hearing. Got Malaysia license?
#5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 145 words

Direct address + problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

If you source from China, you need to hear this. Real talk. Your sourcing budget is leaking 4% a year and you're looking in the wrong place to plug it. Three things are happening every wire. One. Your bank quotes you a USD-CNY rate. You accept it. That rate is 4% worse than the live market rate. Two. The 4% gets baked into the conversion. No fee line, no statement, nothing to alert you. Three. On 500K of annual sourcing, that's 20,000 dollars that left your business and never reached your supplier. You can negotiate harder with the factory all day. The 4% is not on the supplier side. It's on the bank side. WorldFirst runs the same wires at 0.3 to 0.5%. The leak shrinks from 20K to under 3K. You don't get the years back. But you can stop year four right now. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Three. On 500K of annual sourcing, that's 20,000 dollars that left your business and never reached your supplier. You can negotiate harder with the factory all day. The 4% is not on the supplier side. It's on the bank side. WorldFirst runs the same wires at 0.3 to 0.5%. The leak shrinks from 20K to under 3K. You don'tcan't get the past years back. But youYou can stop year four right now. Search WorldFirst. Period.

deleted / added

When a business owner wires China, three things happen on every payment. Your sourcing budget is leaking 4% a year and you're looking in the wrong place to plug it. Three things are happening every wire. One. Your bank quotes you a USD-CNY rate. You accept it. That rate is 4% worse than the live market rate. Two. The 4% gets baked into the conversion. No fee line, no statement, nothing to alert you. Three. On 500K of annual sourcing, that's 20,000 dollars that left your business and never reached your supplier. You can negotiate harder with the factory all day. The 4% is not on the supplier side. It's on the bank side. WorldFirst runs the same wires at 0.3 to 0.5%. The leak shrinks from 20K to under 3K. You can't get the past years back. You can stop year four right now. Search WorldFirst.

When a business owner wires China, three things happen on every payment. The sourcing budget is leaking 4% a year, and most owners are looking in the wrong place to fix it. Let's walk through what really happens every time a payment goes out. Step one. The bank gives a quote to swap US dollars or Malaysian ringgit into Chinese yuan, the currency suppliers ask for. The owner accepts the rate. The number looks normal. But it's 4% worse than the real market rate, the live rate banks use with each other. Step two. That 4% gap gets folded into the swap itself. So there's no fee line on the statement. No charge to question. No alert in online banking. Nothing flags it. Step three. On a 500,000 dollar sourcing year, that's 20,000 dollars that quietly left the business and never reached the Chinese supplier. The factory was paid the agreed amount. The bank just took its cut on the way through. Here's the part that matters. A business owner can push the factory for a better deal all day. It won't help. The 4% leak is not on the supplier side. It's on the bank side. WorldFirst, the international payment service, runs the same kind of payments at 0.3 to 0.5%. Suddenly the annual leak drops from 20,000 dollars to under 3,000. The past years are gone. But year four can stop today. Search WorldFirst.

234 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Year four stop right now sound nice but you assume I switch all my wires. Got annual contract with my bank.
  • 4% worse than live market rate? Where you get the live market rate from. Reuters?
  • Under 3K leak. Means WorldFirst still got margin. Just smaller. Fine but say it lah.
  • Three things every wire is a clean breakdown. I like this one.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Sound American. Got SEA support?

Same money, same suppliers, the 66K stays in your business. Period.

Here is what every business owner needs to know about their most expensive vendor. The most expensive vendor in a business is the bank that handles supplier payments to China, and most business owners have never thought of it that way. Let's walk through why. Imagine a 500,000 dollar sourcing budget in year one. The bank takes 4% on the exchange rate margin, the gap between the real market rate and the rate they actually use to swap into Chinese yuan. That's 20,000 dollars gone. Year two, the business grows. Now they spend a bit more. 22,000 dollars gone. Year three, 24,000 dollars gone. Add it up. 66,000 dollars over three years. That's a junior hire. That's a warehouse rack. That's the margin pressure a business owner has been blaming on logistics or competition. And here's the quiet part. None of it shows on the statement. None of it shows on the payment confirmation. It's hidden inside the rate the bank used when swapping into yuan. WorldFirst, the international payment service, runs the same kind of payments at 0.3 to 0.5%. Same money. Same suppliers. The 66,000 dollars stays in the business.

  • Hire, warehouse rack, ok inspiration but I want numbers comparison please.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the problem most business owners miss when they source from China. You're negotiating with the wrong person. You spend three weeks pushing your supplier on unit price. You shave 2% off the PI. You feel great. Meanwhile, your bank is taking 4% of every wire through the exchange rate margin. On 500K of annual sourcing, that's 20,000 dollars. Twice what you saved negotiating. And it compounds. Year two, your budget grows 10%, the leak grows with it. 22K. Year three, 24K. Total three-year leak: 66K. The supplier never touched that money. It vanished into the conversion before the wire crossed the border. Stop. The supplier is not the leak. The rail is the leak. WorldFirst sits at 0.3 to 0.5%. Same wires, same suppliers, 17K of that 20K stays in your business. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

More business owners need to hear this. 2% on the PI can't beat 4%. They're negotiating with the wrong person. Picture a Malaysian importer who just spent three full weeks pushing the supplier on the unit price. They knocked 2% off the proforma invoice, the PI. That's the price-quote document the factory sends. The owner feels great. They saved real money. But while they were arm-wrestling over 2%, the bank was quietly taking 4% on every single payment to that supplier. How? Through the exchange rate margin, the gap between what the money is really worth and the rate the bank uses. On a 500,000 dollar yearly sourcing budget, that 4% adds up to 20,000 dollars. Twice the savings won from three weeks of supplier haggling. And it gets worse. Year two, the business grows by 10 percent. The leak grows with it. 22,000 dollars. Year three, 24,000 dollars. Over three years, that's 66,000 dollars gone. The supplier never touched that money. It disappeared during the currency swap, before the payment even left Malaysia. Here's the lesson. The supplier is not the leak. The payment route is the leak. WorldFirst, the international payment service, sits at 0.3 to 0.5%. Same suppliers. Same payments. 17,000 of that 20,000 stays in the business every year. Search WorldFirst.

Stop using the supplier negotiation to save 4% on a sourcing budget. Try negotiating with the bank this quarter instead of the supplier. Hear out the reason. Last quarter, a typical business owner spent three full weeks pushing their Chinese factory for a 2% price drop on a single unit. They felt smart when it landed. Meanwhile, the bank was taking 4% on every single payment to that same factory, and the owner didn't even blink. On a 500,000 dollar sourcing year, that's 20,000 dollars in margin gone. Not into the supplier's pocket. The factory got paid the agreed amount. The 20,000 dollars went into the bank's pocket, through the exchange rate margin on the swap into Chinese yuan. So the business owner is fighting the wrong fight. The factory is not the leak. The payment route is the leak. WorldFirst, the international payment service, sits at 0.3 to 0.5% on the same kind of payments to the same factories. The 4% comes home. Run the numbers. If a business owner spent more than 250,000 dollars on Chinese suppliers last year, this is the highest return on investment hour they'll have this month. Search WorldFirst.

#10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 132 words

Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the truth nobody's telling business owners about international wires. Your bank's biggest revenue line on your account is not your wire fee. It's your exchange rate. They quote you USD-CNY at a rate 4% worse than the real market rate. On 500K of sourcing, that's 20K a year. Compound it. Three years, 66K. Five years, over 110K. Silent. No statement entry. No annual summary. You'll never see it because it's not a fee, it's a margin. And the worst part. You can't fix this by negotiating with your supplier. They never had the money. Your bank took it on the conversion. WorldFirst doesn't play this game. 0.3 to 0.5% on the same wires. The rate you see is close to the rate banks use with each other. That's the difference. Period.

Here's the truthsomething nobody's telling business owners about international wires. Your bank's biggest revenue line on your account is not your wire fee. It's your exchange rate. They said "the wire fee is what banks make." Nope. It is the rate. Your bank's biggest revenue line on your account is not your wire fee. It's your exchange rate. They quote you USD-CNY at a rate 4% worse than the real market rate. On 500K of sourcing, that's 20K a year. Compound it. Three years, 66K. Five years, over 110K. Quiet. No statement entry. No annual summary. You'll never see it because it's not a fee, it's a margin. And the part most business owners miss. You can't fix this by negotiating with your supplier. They never had the money. Your bank took it on the conversion. WorldFirst is built differently. 0.3 to 0.5% on the same wires. The rate you see is close to the rate banks use with each other. That's the difference.

They said "the wire fee is what banks make." Nope. It is the rate. The bank's biggest revenue line on the business account is not the wire fee. It's the exchange rate. Let's walk through what that means. When a business owner pays a Chinese supplier, the bank swaps Malaysian ringgit or US dollars into Chinese yuan. The bank quotes a rate that looks fine. But it's about 4% worse than the real market rate, the rate banks use with each other. That 4% is hidden inside the swap. On a 500,000 dollar sourcing year, that's 20,000 dollars a year. Stack the years. Three years, 66,000 dollars gone. Five years, over 110,000 dollars. Quiet. No statement entry. No annual summary. No alert. It's invisible because it's not a fee. It's a margin tucked into the rate. Here's the part most business owners miss. The supplier never had the money. The factory got paid the agreed amount. The 4% disappeared during the currency swap, before the payment even left the country. The bank took it on the conversion. WorldFirst, the international payment service, is built differently. 0.3 to 0.5% on the same kind of payments. The rate the business owner sees is close to the rate banks use with each other. That's the difference.

209 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • 110K over 5 years assume too many things. Cooked numbers.
  • Biggest revenue line on my account is the rate. Damn never thought about that.
  • Close to the rate banks use with each other. Soft hedge. Means they still take a cut lah.
  • Supplier never had the money, technically true. Good reframe.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Need to research. SEA team?

#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 145 words

30-second timer + brutal specificity

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatLow

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Thirty seconds. That's all it takes to find the foreign exchange margin your bank hid in your last wire to China. Watch. Step one. Pull up your wire confirmation. Five seconds. Find the field that says applied rate or exchange rate used. Got it. Step two. Open a new tab. Google USD to CNY. Look at the rate. Note the time. Ten seconds. Step three. The math. Interbank minus applied. Divide by interbank. Times 100. Ten seconds. That's the foreign exchange margin percent. Five seconds to multiply by your wire amount. That's the dollar amount they took. Real example. Applied 7.05. Interbank that morning 7.22. Margin 2.35 percent. On 50,000 dollars, you paid 1,175 dollars in invisible foreign exchange spread. The wire fee on the receipt? Forty bucks. The real cost? Thirty times that. Stop. WorldFirst shows you the rate before you click send. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Can a business owner find a hidden foreign exchange fee in 30 seconds? Watch. That's all it takes to find the foreign exchange margin your bank hid in your last wire to China. Watch. Step one. Pull up your wire confirmation. Five seconds. Find the field that says applied rate or exchange rate used. Got it. Step two. Open a new tab. Google USD to CNY. Look at the rate. Note the time. Ten seconds. Step three. The math. Interbank minus applied. Divide by interbank. Times 100. Ten seconds. That's the foreign exchange margin percent. Five seconds to multiply by your wire amount. That's the dollar amount they took. Real example. Applied 7.05. Interbank that morning 7.22. Margin 2.35 percent. On 50,000 dollars, you paid 1,175 dollars in invisible foreign exchange spread. The wire fee on the receipt? Forty bucks. The real cost? Thirty times that. WorldFirst shows you the rate before you click send. Search WorldFirst.

Can a business owner find a hidden foreign exchange fee in 30 seconds? Watch. That's all we need to show you a fee your bank hid inside your last wire to China. Ready? Let's walk through it together. Step one, the first five seconds. Open up your wire confirmation. Look for a field that says applied rate, or rate used, or something like that. Got it? Good. Step two, ten more seconds. Open a fresh tab on your browser. Type USD to CNY into Google. Look at the rate it gives you. Take a quick note of the time too. Step three, the math part, ten more seconds. Take the interbank rate from Google. Subtract the applied rate from your receipt. Divide that little gap by the interbank rate. Then multiply by one hundred. That number you just made is the foreign exchange margin in percent. Five last seconds. Multiply that percent by the size of your wire. That's the real dollar amount your bank took. Let's do a real one together. Applied rate, 7.05. Interbank that morning, 7.22. The margin is 2.35 percent. On a 50,000 dollar wire, that's 1,175 dollars in invisible foreign exchange spread. And what does the receipt say the wire fee was? Forty bucks. The real cost was about thirty times bigger. At WorldFirst, we show you the rate before you click send. Search WorldFirst.

  • Interbank rate moves every second. Which exact moment to compare?

Everyone says banks overcharge on China wires. Nobody shows the 30-second proof. Save this. If you're a business owner paying suppliers in China, you're about to find a fee your bank never showed you. Three numbers. That's all you need. Number one. The applied rate. It's printed at the bottom of your confirmation. Find it. Number two. The interbank rate. Google USD to CNY for the exact time the wire was booked. Number three. Your wire amount. Now subtract applied from interbank. Divide by interbank. Multiply by 100. That percent is the foreign exchange margin your bank quietly took. Example. Applied 7.05. Interbank 7.22. Everyone says banks overcharge on China wires. Nobody shows the 30-second proof. Save this. Pull up the last wire receipt you sent to a supplier in China. Got it? Good. We want to show you a fee your bank never told you about. It's hiding inside the exchange rate. You only need three little numbers to find it. Here's the first one. On your wire receipt, somewhere near the bottom, you'll see a line that says applied rate or rate used. That's the price your bank used to swap your US dollars into Chinese yuan. Write that number down. Now the second one. Open Google. Type in USD to CNY. Look at the rate it shows you for around the same time your wire went out. That's called the interbank rate. It's the real wholesale price that banks pay each other. The third number is easy. It's just how big your wire was in US dollars. Now do the math with us. Take the interbank rate and subtract the applied rate. Divide that by the interbank rate. Multiply by one hundred. That little percent? That's the secret fee your bank slipped onto the rate. Let's try a real example. Say the applied rate was 7.05 and the interbank rate was 7.22. The gap is 2.35 percent. On a 50,000 dollar wire, that's 1,175 dollars gone, and you'd never see it on the receipt. At WorldFirst, we show you the rate before you click send. One rate. No surprise. Search WorldFirst.

#5 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 135 words

Accusation opener + receipt walkthrough

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMid

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Your bank charged you 1,175 dollars on your last wire to China and didn't put it on the receipt. Here's how to find it in thirty seconds. Open your wire confirmation. Look near the bottom. There's a line called applied rate or exchange rate used. Write that number down. 7.05. That's what your bank used to convert USD to CNY. Now open Google. Type USD to CNY. Look at the live rate. 7.22. Same day, same hour. That's the interbank rate, the rate banks trade with each other. The gap between 7.22 and 7.05 is the foreign exchange margin your bank took. Math. 7.22 minus 7.05 equals 0.17. 0.17 divided by 7.22 times 100 equals 2.35 percent. On a 50,000 dollar wire, that's 1,175 dollars. Not on the receipt. Stop. WorldFirst shows the rate upfront. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Look at the live rate. 7.22. Same day, same hour. That's the interbank rate, the rate banks trade with each other. The gap between 7.22 and 7.05 is the foreign exchange margin your bank took. The wire receipt says 40 dollars. The real cost on the rate was 1,175 dollars. Here's how to find it in thirty seconds. Open your wire confirmation. Look near the bottom. There's a line called applied rate or exchange rate used. Write that number down. 7.05. That's what your bank used to convert USD to CNY. Now open Google. Type USD to CNY. Look at the live rate. 7.22. Same day, same hour. That's the interbank rate, the rate banks trade with each other. The gap between 7.22 and 7.05 is the foreign exchange margin your bank took. The wire receipt says 40 dollars. The real cost on the rate was 1,175 dollars. Sounds wild, right? Here's how we can spot it together in thirty seconds. First, open up your wire confirmation. Look near the bottom of the page. You'll see a line called applied rate, or maybe exchange rate used. Let's say it shows 7.05. Write that down. That's the rate your bank used to swap your US dollars into Chinese yuan. Now open Google in a new tab. Type in USD to CNY. Look at the live rate. Let's say it says 7.22 for the same day, same hour. That's called the interbank rate, the rate that banks trade with each other behind the scenes. Now here's the trick. The little gap between 7.22 and 7.05 is the secret fee your bank stuck on top of the rate. Let's do the simple math. 7.22 minus 7.05 is 0.17. Divide 0.17 by 7.22, then multiply by one hundred. You get 2.35 percent. On a 50,000 dollar wire, that's 1,175 dollars. And it's nowhere on your receipt. At WorldFirst, we show you the rate up front before you send. Search WorldFirst.

198 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Pocketed sounds like accusation. My bank not stealing, just charging margin.
  • $1,175 on $50K is 2.35%, but my last wire was way smaller. Math don't scale to my situation.
  • Receipt do show applied rate la. Just need to read carefully.
  • Search WorldFirst? In Malaysia I search and got mixed results. Confusing.
  • Why use 7.22 not the rate at booking time? Google rate is delayed.

Write it down. 7.05. Open Google. Type USD to CNY. Note the rate at that moment. 7.22. That's the foreign exchange interbank rate. The real wholesale price. The gap between those two numbers, expressed as a percent, is what your bank charged you on top of the wire fee. 7.22 minus 7.05, divided by 7.22, times 100. 2.35 percent. On 50,000 dollars, that's 1,175 dollars. Do six wires a year? Seven thousand dollars. Twelve wires? Fourteen thousand. None of it on the receipt. Stop signing off on this. On 50,000 dollars, that's 1,175 dollars. Do six wires a year? Seven thousand dollars. Twelve wires? Fourteen thousand. None of it on the receipt. WorldFirst shows you the rate before you click send. One rate. No surprise margin. Search WorldFirst.

Don't send another wire to China until a business owner has done this one check. Today, right now. Pull up your most recent wire confirmation on your phone or laptop. Find the line called applied rate. That's the exchange rate your bank used to swap your US dollars into Chinese yuan. Write it down. Let's pretend it says 7.05. Now open Google. Type in USD to CNY. Note the live rate at that moment. Let's say it shows 7.22. That second number is called the interbank rate. It's the real wholesale price big banks pay each other. The little gap between those two numbers, turned into a percent, is what your bank charged you on top of the wire fee. Let's do the math together. 7.22 minus 7.05, divided by 7.22, times one hundred. That gives you 2.35 percent. On a 50,000 dollar wire, that's about 1,175 dollars. Now stretch it out. Six wires in a year? That's seven thousand. Twelve wires? Fourteen thousand. And none of it shows up on the receipt. At WorldFirst, we show you the rate before you click send. One rate. No surprise. Search WorldFirst.

189 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • 6 wires a year? Some of us send 30+. Math feels light.
  • Or some of us only do 2 wires a year. So 2,300 not 14,000. Why scare us with big numbers?
  • WorldFirst no surprise margin? Sure or not? All FX have spread.
  • My SCB statement does show FX cost separately. Maybe not all banks hide.
  • What if my wire was at off-hours when interbank was different from Google now?
#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 148 words

Mechanism-first tutorial

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's something most business owners don't know. The biggest cost on your wire to China isn't on your wire confirmation. It's hidden inside the exchange rate they used. Foreign exchange margin. Here's how to find it in thirty seconds. You need three numbers. One. The applied rate. The exchange rate printed on your confirmation. Usually 7-point-something for USD to CNY. Two. The interbank rate. Google USD to CNY at the same date and time the wire was booked. This is the real rate banks pay each other. Three. Your wire amount in USD. Now subtract applied from interbank. Divide that by interbank. Multiply by 100. That percent is what your bank quietly added to the rate. Multiply that percent by your wire amount. That's the dollar cost. Most SEA bank confirmations show the applied rate but never the interbank reference. By design. Stop. WorldFirst shows you both. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Here's something most business owners don't know. The biggest cost on your wire to China isn't on your wire confirmation. It's hidden inside the exchange rate they used. Foreign exchange margin. Here's how to find it in thirty seconds. You need three numbers. One. The applied rate. The exchange rate printed on your confirmation. Usually 7-point-something for USD to CNY. Two. The interbank rate. Google USD to CNY at the same date and time the wire was booked. This is the real rate banks pay each other. Three. Your wire amount in USD. Now subtract applied from interbank. Divide that by interbank. Multiply by 100. That percent is what your bank quietly added to the rate. Multiply that percent by your wire amount. That's the dollar cost. Most SEA bank confirmations show the applied rate but never the interbank reference. By design. Stop. WorldFirst shows you both. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

deleted / added

The biggest cost on a China wire isn't on the confirmation. It's hidden in the rate. The biggest cost on your wire to China isn't on your wire confirmation. It's hidden inside the exchange rate they used. Foreign exchange margin. Here's how to find it in thirty seconds. You need three numbers. One. The applied rate. The exchange rate printed on your confirmation. Usually 7-point-something for USD to CNY. Two. The interbank rate. Google USD to CNY at the same date and time the wire was booked. This is the real rate banks pay each other. Three. Your wire amount in USD. Now subtract applied from interbank. Divide that by interbank. Multiply by 100. That percent is what your bank quietly added to the rate. Multiply that percent by your wire amount. That's the dollar cost. Most SEA bank confirmations show the applied rate but never the interbank reference. By design. WorldFirst shows you both. Search WorldFirst.

The biggest cost on a China wire isn't on the confirmation. It's hidden in the rate. When you wire money to a supplier in China, your biggest cost isn't the fee on the receipt. It's a quiet little extra hidden inside the exchange rate your bank used. It even has a name. People call it foreign exchange margin. We want to show you how to find it in thirty seconds. You only need three little numbers. Number one is the applied rate. That's the exchange rate printed on your wire confirmation. For US dollars to Chinese yuan, it's usually somewhere around seven-point-something. Number two is the interbank rate. Open Google and search USD to CNY for around the same date and time your wire went out. That's the real wholesale rate that banks pay each other. Number three is just the size of your wire in US dollars. Easy. Now do the math with us. Take the interbank rate. Subtract the applied rate. Divide that by the interbank rate. Multiply by one hundred. That percent is the quiet extra your bank slipped on top. Multiply that percent by your wire amount and you've got the real dollar cost. Most banks here in Southeast Asia show you the applied rate but never the interbank rate next to it. That's on purpose. At WorldFirst, we show you both. Search WorldFirst.

220 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • By design what design? Bank legally required to show rate so what's hidden?
  • USD to CNY is 7-point-something but my bank quotes RM-CNY direct. Different scenario.
  • WorldFirst show both rates? Need to see screenshot before believe.
  • I'm in Malaysia not just SEA. Where's the MY-specific info?
  • How do I know Google rate is the real interbank? Could also be marked up.

More business owners need to hear this. The wire receipt isn't telling them the real cost. You probably think you know what your bank charged you on that last payment. You don't. Here's why. That wire fee on the receipt, the one that says forty bucks or whatever? That's the smallest part of the cost. #9 PG3 Gary Vee Opinion-led 144 words

Stop trusting the receipt + reframe

Soc4.0 Aud4.2 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMid

Hammer/wood analogy is plain English and lands. 'Use the right tool for the job' is generic but clean.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Stop trusting your wire receipt. I'm serious. The line that says wire fee, forty dollars, that's not your real cost. The real cost is the foreign exchange margin and you can find it in thirty seconds. Look. Your confirmation shows an applied rate. That's the rate your bank used to convert. Google shows the interbank rate. That's the wholesale rate. The gap between those two numbers is what your bank quietly took on top. On USD-CNY, that gap is usually 1.5 to 3 percent. On a 50,000 dollar wire, that's up to 1,500 dollars. The receipt will never tell you this. The receipt is designed to make you feel good about forty dollars. So stop looking at the receipt. Look at the rate. WorldFirst puts the rate up front. The number you see is the number you get. Search WorldFirst. Now.

#10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 137 words

Truth nobody's telling you

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatMid

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about wire transfers to China. The fee on the receipt is bait. It's there to anchor your attention. Forty bucks. Feels cheap. So you don't look further. But the real money is on the rate. The applied rate on your confirmation versus the interbank rate at the same time. That gap is the foreign exchange margin. It's never broken out. Never disclosed. By design. Banks know if they put 'plus 2.35 percent foreign exchange margin' on the receipt, you'd shop around. So they hide it inside the rate. Thirty seconds of math reveals it. Pull the applied rate. Pull the interbank rate. Run the formula. There's the number you actually paid. Could be 1,000 dollars per wire. Could be more. Every time. WorldFirst shows the rate before you click send. One number. Period.

Pull the applied rate. Pull the interbank rate. Run the formula. There's the number you actually paid. Could be 1,000 dollars per wire. Could be more. Every time. WorldFirst shows the rate before you click send. One number. Period.

They said the wire fee is the cost. That's bait. The real fee is on the rate. The fee printed on the receipt is bait. It's just there to grab your attention. Forty bucks. Feels cheap. So you don't bother looking any further. But the real money is sitting on the exchange rate. Compare the applied rate on your confirmation against the interbank rate at the same moment. That little gap is the foreign exchange margin. It's never broken out. Never disclosed on the receipt. And that's on purpose. Banks know if they actually wrote 'plus 2.35 percent foreign exchange margin' on the bill, you'd start shopping around. So they tuck it inside the rate instead. The good news is thirty seconds of math reveals all of it. Pull the applied rate from your confirmation. Pull the interbank rate from Google. Run the simple formula. The number you get is what you actually paid. Could be a thousand dollars per wire. Could be more. Every single time. At WorldFirst, we show you the rate before you click send. One number. The business owner feels valued. The wire goes out. The supplier gets less than expected. And nobody connects the dots. The dots are this. Good is not a rate. It's a feeling. The actual rate, the real interbank rate that banks use with each other, you can pull up on Google in 5 seconds. Search USD to CNY. Compare. Search WorldFirst. Now.

If a business owner has ever heard "good rate" from a bank, watch this. We see the same story play out every week. Picture it. The relationship manager calls. They sound friendly. They use the word good. The business owner feels valued, feels like they are being looked after. The wire goes out. A few days later, the supplier in China receives a little less than expected. And nobody ever joins the dots between the two. Let us join them for you. Good is not a rate. Good is a feeling. The actual rate, the real wholesale rate that banks use when they trade money between themselves, you can find in 5 seconds on Google. Just type USD to CNY. The number is right there. Compare it to what your bank just quoted you. If your bank is 2 cents off on every dollar, then the word good has been quietly costing you 2 percent on every wire you sent all year. That is real money. Real margin. So next time, trust the number, not the adjective. We built WorldFirst to give you that number, right on the screen. Have a look. Just search WorldFirst.

Here's how to translate "we'll give you a good rate" into the actual number. We'll give you a good rate. Translation. We'll give you a rate that sounds good next to the rate we'd give a tourist. 10am check. Interbank USD-CNY, the rate banks use with each other, sitting at 7.22. Bank quote on your wire. 7.05. The word good is doing 2.4 percent of heavy lifting. On a 50K supplier payment that's 1,200 dollars your bank kept and never line-itemed. Start asking for the number instead of the adjective. Ask your bank, what's your USD-CNY rate today, then open Google and compare. Half a percent off is normal. Two percent off is them charging you for the word good. WorldFirst gives you near-interbank rates with no adjective games. Search WorldFirst.

Here's how to translate "we'll give you a good rate" into the actual number. We will give you a good rate. Sounds nice. Here is what it really means. We will give you a rate that sounds good when you put it next to the worst rate we could legally give a tourist at the airport. Not the real rate. The tourist rate. That is what good is being compared to. Let us prove it with real numbers. At 10am on a normal Tuesday, the real wholesale rate for US dollars to Chinese yuan, the rate that banks use when they trade with each other, was 7.22. Your bank quoted you 7.05 for your wire. That tiny gap is 2.4 cents on every dollar. So on a 50,000 dollar payment to your supplier, the bank quietly kept 1,200 dollars. There is no line on the statement that says, we kept this. It is hidden inside the rate itself. So here is what we want you to do. Next time your bank uses a word like good, just ask them for the number. Say, what is your exact USD to CNY rate today? Write it down. Then open Google and type USD to CNY. Compare the two. If your bank is half a percent off, that is normal. If they are two full percent off, they are charging you for the word good. We built WorldFirst to do this differently. Our rates sit close to the real wholesale number. No fancy words, just the number. Have a look. Just search WorldFirst.

260 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Tourist rate? Cmon now, business wire not same as money changer lah.
  • 1,200 on 50K, ok the math works. But once-off example or always?
  • Half a percent normal, two percent bad. Where got rule like this?
  • WorldFirst no adjective game, sure or not? Read your own marketing first.
  • What's your fee model then? Free? Hidden somewhere also?

If your relationship manager said we'll give you a good rate, you need this. Here's the test. Two columns. Left column. What the bank quoted you. Say 7.05 USD to CNY on a wire to your supplier. Right column. What the rate actually was at that minute. Real interbank, the rate banks use with each other. 7.22. The gap is 2.4 percent. On 10K, you paid 240 hidden. On 50K, 1,200 hidden. On 100K, 2,400 hidden. None of it shows up as a fee. You don't need a treasury team. You need one browser tab. XE.com or Google USD to CNY. Compare before you confirm. If your bank's rate is more than half a percent off the real one, the word good is doing the lying. WorldFirst quotes near-interbank with the live rate shown before confirmation. Search WorldFirst. Period.

If your relationship manager said we'll give you a good rate, you need this. Here's the test. Two columns. Left column. What the bank quoted you. Say 7.05 USD to CNY on a wire to your supplier. Right column. What the rate actually was at that minute. Real interbank, the rate banks use with each other. 7.22. The gap is 2.4 percent. On 10K, you paid 240 hidden. On 50K, 1,200 hidden. On 100K, 2,400 hidden. None of it shows up as a fee. You don't need a treasury team. You need one browser tab. XE.com or Google USD to CNY. Compare before you confirm. deleted / added

If a business owner wants honest FX, this two-column test takes 10 seconds. Here's the test. Two columns. Left column. What the bank quoted you. Say 7.05 USD to CNY on a wire to your supplier. Right column. What the rate actually was at that minute. Real interbank, the rate banks use with each other. 7.22. The gap is 2.4 percent. On 10K, you paid 240 hidden. On 50K, 1,200 hidden. On 100K, 2,400 hidden. None of it shows up as a fee. You don't need a treasury team. You need one browser tab. XE.com or Google USD to CNY. Compare before you confirm. If your bank's rate is more than half a percent off the real one, the word good is doing the work. WorldFirst quotes near-interbank with the live rate shown before confirmation. Search WorldFirst.

If a business owner wants honest FX, this two-column test takes 10 seconds. Here is how it works. Grab a piece of paper. Draw a line down the middle so you have two columns. In the left column, write down exactly what the bank quoted you. Let us say they offered 7.05 yuan for every US dollar on a wire to your supplier. In the right column, write what the real wholesale rate actually was at that exact minute. That is called the interbank rate, the rate banks use when they trade money with each other. Say it was 7.22. Now look at the gap between the two columns. That gap is 2.4 cents on every dollar. So on a 10,000 dollar wire, you quietly paid 240 dollars hidden in the rate. On a 50,000 dollar wire, that is 1,200 dollars. On a 100,000 dollar wire, that is 2,400 dollars. None of it ever shows up as a fee, because it is built into the rate itself. The good news is you do not need a treasury team to catch this. You need one browser tab. Open XE.com, or just Google USD to CNY. Compare before you click confirm. If your bank is more than half a percent off the real number, that word good is doing all the work. We built WorldFirst so you see the live rate right before you confirm the wire. Have a look. Just search WorldFirst.

256 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Two column test ok I can do this. Practical advice for once.
  • Half percent threshold still feel made up but at least sound reasonable.
  • Live rate shown before confirmation, sure WorldFirst do this?
  • Who is WorldFirst? Never hear before in KL.
  • Compare before confirm, ok useful tip even if I don't switch.
#4 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 144 words

Phrase-decode classic PAS

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Listen. When your bank tells you we'll give you a good rate, they mean good for them. Here's the math. 10am Tuesday. Real interbank USD-CNY, the rate banks use with each other, is 7.22. Your bank quotes you 7.05. That looks fine. It's not. The gap is 2.4 percent. On a 10K wire that's 240 dollars gone. On 100K it's 2,400. Same percent. Scales with every wire you send. You never see it on a statement because it's baked into the rate itself. Banks compete on the word good. You should compete on the actual number. Stop. Open XE.com or just Google USD to CNY before you confirm the wire. If your quote is more than half a percent off, you're paying a silent margin. Period. WorldFirst quotes near the interbank rate so the number you see is the number you pay. Search WorldFirst. Period.

240 dollars lost on a 10K wire. Here's the math banks hide. Here's the math. 10am Tuesday. Real interbank USD-CNY, the rate banks use with each other, is 7.22. Your bank quotes you 7.05. That looks fine. It's not. The gap is 2.4 percent. On a 10K wire that's 240 dollars gone. On 100K it's 2,400. Same percent. Scales with every wire you send. You never see it on a statement because it's baked into the rate itself. Banks compete on the word good. You should compete on the actual number. Open XE.com or just Google USD to CNY before you confirm the wire. If your quote is more than half a percent off, you're paying a silent margin. WorldFirst quotes near the interbank rate so the number you see is the number you pay. Worth a look. Search WorldFirst.

240 dollars lost on a 10K wire. Here's the math banks hide. Picture it. You are sending money to your supplier in China. The bank is friendly. They say, do not worry, we will give you a good rate. That sounds caring. It is not. Good for who? Good for them. Here is what we mean. Every minute of every day, there is one real rate that banks use when they trade money with each other. People call it the interbank rate. Think of it as the wholesale price for money. On a normal Tuesday morning, the real wholesale price for one US dollar might be 7.22 Chinese yuan. Now, the rate your bank gives you on your wire? Maybe 7.05. Looks close. It is not. That little gap is 2.4 cents on every dollar. So on a 10,000 dollar wire, that is 240 dollars quietly kept by your bank. On a 100,000 dollar wire, that is 2,400 dollars. Same percentage. Scales with every wire you send. And here is the part most people miss. You will never see this on your statement. There is no line that says, by the way, we kept this much. It is baked right into the rate. The fix is simple, and it is free. Before you click confirm on any wire, open a new tab. Type XE.com, or just Google USD to CNY. Compare what your bank quoted you with what the real rate is at that moment. If the gap is more than half a percent, your bank is keeping the difference. We built WorldFirst to fix this. Our rates sit close to that real wholesale number, so the rate you see is the rate you pay. Have a look. Just search WorldFirst.

301 words (longer, 5th-6th grade English, story-mode)
Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • 7.22 vs 7.05? One snapshot only what. CNY rate move every minute lah.
  • Half a percent threshold, who set this rule? Sound made up.
  • My Maybank quote not so bad. Maybe US bank different.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Got Malaysia license or not?
  • Sound like ad. Show me real screenshot side by side la.

I'll say it opinion-as-truth

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

I'll say it. Good rate is the most successful piece of marketing the banks ever ran. Because good is comparative and they get to pick what it's compared to. You send a wire to your supplier in China. Bank says good rate. You feel taken care of. Meanwhile the real interbank rate, the rate banks use with each other, is sitting 2 to 3 percent better than what they just quoted you. On 50K, that's a thousand dollars or more, gone in one click. You'll never see it because the margin lives inside the rate, not on the statement. The fix is so simple. Google USD to CNY before you confirm. If you're more than half a percent off, you're paying for the word good. WorldFirst publishes rates near interbank. Numbers, not adjectives. Search WorldFirst.

"We'll give you a good rate." The most successful banking line ever written. Because good is comparative and they get to pick what it's compared to. You send a wire to your supplier in China. Bank says good rate. You feel taken care of. Meanwhile the real interbank rate, the rate banks use with each other, is sitting 2 to 3 percent better than what they just quoted you. "We'll give you a good rate." The most successful banking line ever written. Good rate is probably the most successful piece of marketing that banks ever came up with. Here is why it works so well. The word good is a comparison word. It only means something when you put it next to something else. And the bank gets to quietly pick what to compare it to. So picture this. You are sending a wire to your supplier in China. Your bank says, we have a good rate for you today. You feel valued. You feel taken care of. Meanwhile, the real wholesale rate, the one banks use when they trade money with each other, is sitting 2 to 3 percent better than what they just quoted you. On a 50,000 dollar wire, that is a thousand dollars or more, gone in one click. And you will never see it on your statement. Because the cost does not live in a fee. It lives inside the rate itself. The good news is the fix is simple. Before you click confirm on your next wire, open Google. Type USD to CNY. Look at the number. Then compare it to what your bank is offering you. If the gap is more than half a percent, you are paying for the word good. We built WorldFirst so the rate sits close to the real wholesale number. Numbers, not adjectives. Have a look. Just search WorldFirst.

  • 2 to 3 percent range, better than fixed number. But still got source?
  • Half percent threshold rule, where from? Sound arbitrary.
  • Margin in rate not statement, ya this part true actually.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Got license in Malaysia or just online thing?

This is why one word on a bank call quietly eats yearly margin. The word is good. As in, we will give you a good rate. Here is why that little word is so expensive. Picture Tuesday morning at 10am. The real wholesale rate for US dollars to Chinese yuan, the rate banks use when they trade with each other, sits at 7.22. Your bank tells you they have a good rate for you. They quote you 7.05. That gap is 2.4 cents on every dollar. On a 10,000 dollar wire, that is 240 dollars. On a 100,000 dollar wire, that is 2,400 dollars. Now think about this. Most importers do not just send one wire a year. They send wires every month. Sometimes every week. Now do the math over a whole year. The word good might have quietly cost you enough money to hire someone new. Or to build up your inventory before Q3. Or to drop your prices and beat a competitor. None of that ever shows up on a statement, because the cost is hidden inside the rate itself. Banks lean on the word good because most business owners are too busy to check. So here is the fix. It is free, and it takes 10 seconds. Open a new tab. Go to XE.com. Compare the rate. Or even simpler, Google USD to CNY. Then ask your bank for the actual number, not the adjective. We built WorldFirst so you can see the real rate before you click confirm. Have a look. Just search WorldFirst.

Mechanism reveal, no accusation

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's what most business owners don't realize about the phrase good rate. Good is not a rate. It's a relative word. Good compared to what. Here's the answer your bank hopes you never ask. Tuesday 10am. Real interbank USD-CNY, the rate banks use with each other, is 7.22. Your bank's good rate. 7.05. The gap is 2.4 percent. On every wire. Buried in the exchange rate itself, never a line item on the statement. So when the bank says good, they mean better than what they could legally charge a tourist. Not better than the real cost. Better than the worst case. The fix is one tab. XE.com. Google. Wise mid-market quote. All free. All real-time. Compare before you confirm. WorldFirst publishes rates near interbank so good actually means good. Search WorldFirst. Period.

In this video: what "good rate" really means, explained simply. Interbank = the rate banks use with each other. Good is not really a rate. It is a word that compares one thing to another. So the moment you hear the word good, the question you should ask is, good compared to what? That is the question your bank really hopes you never ask out loud. Let us answer it for you. Picture Tuesday at 10am. The real wholesale rate for US dollars to Chinese yuan, the rate banks use when they trade between themselves, is 7.22. Your bank's good rate for your wire is 7.05. The gap is 2.4 cents on every dollar. On every wire you send. And it is not a fee you can see. It is buried inside the exchange rate itself. So when your bank uses the word good, they mean better than the worst rate they could have given a tourist changing money at the airport. Not better than the real wholesale price. Better than the worst case. The fix is one browser tab. You have three free options. XE.com. A simple Google search for USD to CNY. Or check the Wise mid-market quote. All of them are free. All of them are updated in real time. Just compare before you click confirm. We built WorldFirst so the rate you see sits close to that real wholesale number. So when we say good, we actually mean good. Have a look. Just search WorldFirst.

More business owners need to hear this. "Good rate" is marketing, not a number. When your bank tells you, we will give you a good rate, we want you to understand exactly what that sentence is. It is marketing. It is not a number. Let us show you what good actually meant on a real wire last Tuesday. A friend of ours runs a small import business. Here is the simple idea we want you to take away. Words like good are not rates. Numbers are rates. So next time your bank uses an adjective, just ask them for the number. Stop accepting adjectives reframe

Soc4.0 Aud4.2 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Stop accepting adjectives from your bank. I'm serious. Good rate. Best rate. Preferred rate. Special rate. None of those are numbers. None of those tell you what your supplier actually receives in China. Here's what one looked like last week. Interbank USD-CNY, the rate banks use with each other, 7.22. Bank's good rate to a business owner I know, 7.05. Gap, 2.4 percent. On his 80K wire, that's almost 2K vanishing into the rate. You wouldn't sign a contract with the word good as a price. Don't accept it on a wire either. Ask for the number. Compare to Google. WorldFirst quotes near-interbank with the number shown before you confirm. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Time for business owners to throw away the adjective and ask for the number. Your bank is selling you adjectives. Listen to the words they use. Good rate. Best rate. Preferred rate. Special rate. Notice anything? Not one of them is actually a number. Not one of them tells you what your supplier in China will actually receive at the other end. Let us show you what that looks like in real life. Last week, a business owner we know sent an 80,000 dollar wire to his supplier. The real wholesale rate that day, the rate banks use when they trade with each other, was 7.22. His bank called him and said, we have a good rate for you. The number behind that good rate was 7.05. The gap was 2.4 cents on every dollar. Almost 2,000 dollars quietly vanished into the rate on a single wire. Here is the way to think about it. You would never sign a contract with your supplier where the price was simply the word good. You would want the actual number. Same thing applies to wires. Ask for the number. Write it down. Then open Google and type USD to CNY. Compare them side by side. We built WorldFirst so the number is right there on the screen before you click confirm. Have a look. Just search WorldFirst.

#10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 115 words

Truth nobody tells / conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Hook · library-driven · click any to swap (★ = recommended default)
→ → →

Here's the truth nobody is telling business owners about the phrase good rate. It's a trick of comparison. Banks compare your rate to a worse rate they could have given you and call it good. They don't compare it to the real interbank rate, the rate banks use with each other. On a typical day, that gap is 2 percent. Sometimes more. On 50K, that's 1,000 to 1,500 dollars. Per wire. Never on the statement. The word good is doing all the work. Pull up Google. Search USD to CNY. Look at the number. Then look at your bank's quote. The gap is what good was hiding. WorldFirst quotes near interbank. Adjective free. Search WorldFirst.

They don't compare it to the real interbank rate, the rate banks use with each other. On a typical day, that gap is 2 percent. Sometimes more. On 50K, that's 1,000 to 1,500 dollars. Per wire. Never on the statement. The word good is doing all the work. Pull up Google. Search USD to CNY. Look at the number. Then look at your bank's quote. The gap is what good was hiding. WorldFirst quotes near interbank. Adjective freeinterbank. Numbers, not adjectives. Search WorldFirst.

This is why the comparison trick behind "good rate" quietly eats margin. It's a trick of comparison. Banks compare your rate to a worse rate they could have given you and call it good. They don't compare it to the real interbank rate, the rate banks use with each other. On a typical day, that gap is 2 percent. Sometimes more. On 50K, that's 1,000 to 1,500 dollars. Per wire. Never on the statement. The word good is doing all the work. Pull up Google. Search USD to CNY. Look at the number. Then look at your bank's quote. The gap is what good was hiding. WorldFirst quotes near interbank. Numbers, not adjectives. Search WorldFirst.

This is why the comparison trick behind "good rate" quietly eats margin. It is a quiet trick of comparison. Let us explain. When a bank says good, they are comparing your rate to a worse rate they could have given you. That is their reference point. They are not comparing it to the real wholesale rate, which is the rate banks actually use when they trade money with each other. Two completely different reference points. On a normal day, the gap between what your bank quotes and the real wholesale rate is around 2 cents on every dollar. Sometimes more. On a 50,000 dollar wire, that is 1,000 to 1,500 dollars. Per wire. And it never shows up on your statement, because the cost is hidden inside the rate itself. The word good is doing all the heavy lifting. Here is how to catch it in 5 seconds. Open Google. Type USD to CNY. Look at the number. Then look at the rate your bank quoted you on the screen. The gap between those two numbers is what the word good was quietly hiding. We built WorldFirst so the rate sits close to the real wholesale number. Numbers, not adjectives. Have a look. Just search WorldFirst.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on
  • Trick of comparison, ok this actually a good way to frame it.
  • 2% typical, source please? Or your opinion only?
  • 1000 to 1500 on 50K. Math checks out. But every bank like this?
  • Never on statement, ya FX margin baked in rate. This part true.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Need license proof before I move money.
